rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.23
rows.question: Two converging lenses with focal lengths 15 cm and 25 cm are placed 18 cm apart. An object is located 8.0 cm to the left of the 15 cm focal length lens. Where is the final image formed ?
rows.answer:
Find the image location for the first lens using the lens formula, 1/v − 1/u= 1/f
1/v1= 1/f1 + 1/u1= 1/15 − 1/8.0 which gives v1= − 17 c mv
This image forms the object of the second lens located 17 c m + 18 c m= 35 c m away. Then for the second lens
1/v2= 1/f2 + 1/u2= 1/25 − 1/35 which gives v2= 87.5 c m
The final image lies 87.5 cm to the right of the second lens (25 c m) lens.

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.24
rows.question: An object is placed at a distance 1.50 m from a screen and a convex lens placed in between produces an image magnified 4 times on the screen. What is the focal length and the position of the lens?
rows.answer:
Here, magnification m= I/O= − 4
Let lens is placed at a distance of x from the object. Then
u= − x, and v= (1.5 − x)
using m= v/u, we get − 4= (1.5 − x)/(− x)
or 4 x= 1.5 − x or 5 x= 1.5
Thus x= 0.3 m
The lens is placed at a distance of 0.3 m from the object (or 1.20 m from the screen)
For focal length, we may use m= (f)/(f + u)
or − 4= (f)/(f + (− 0.3)) or − 4 f + 1.2= f or 5 f= 1.2
Thus f= 1.2/5= 0.24
The focal length is 0.24 m (or 24 cm )

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.25
rows.question:
The radius of curvature of a thin plano-convex lens is 1 0 c m (of curved surface) and the refractive index is 1.5 . If the plane surface is silvered, then it behaves like a concave mirror of focal length
(a) 1 0 c m (b)
15 cm
(c) 20 c m
(d) 5 cm
rows.answer:
(a) The silvered plano convex lens behaves as a concave mirror; whose focal length is given by
1/F= 2/f1 + 1/fm
If plane surface is silvered
& fm= R2/2= (∞)/(2) = ∞ & ∴ 1/f1= (μ − 1) (1/R1 − 1/R2) = (μ − 1) (1/R − (1)/(∞)) = (μ − 1)/(R) & ∴ 1/F= (2 (μ − 1))/(R) + (1)/(∞) = (2 (μ − 1))/(R) ⇒ F= (R)/(2 (μ − 1))
Fig. 1.70
Here, R= 10 c m, μ= 1.5
∴ F= (10)/(2 (1.5 − 1)) = 10 c m
## POWER OF A LENS
If focal length of a lens is measured in metre then its reciprocal gives the power (P) of the lens.
i.e., Power of a lens, P= (1)/(f (in m))
The S.I. unit of power is diopter (D).
Power of a combination of lenses: If a number of lenses are placed in close contact with each other, then the power of the combination of lenses is equal to the algebraic sum of the powers of individual lenses. i.e. P= P1 + P2 + …. Pn
## ILLUSTRATIOM : 1.26
A doctor has prescribed a corrective lens of power +1.5 D . Find the focal length of the lens. Is the prescribed lens diverging or converging?
Given: P= + 1.5 D
Focal length f= 100/P c m= 100/1.5= 1000/15= + 66.67 c m= + 0.67 m
As the focal length is + v e, hence it is a converging or convex lens.

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.27
rows.question: A convex lens of focal length 40 cm is in compact with a concave lens of focal length 25 cm . Find the power of the combination of lenses.
rows.answer:
Focal length of combination, 1/F= 1/f1 + 1/f2= 1/40 + (1)/(− 25)
(Since focal length is negative for concave lens)
⇒ F= (− 200)/(3) c m
And, Power, P= (1)/(f (in metre)) = 100/F= (100)/((− 200)/(3)) = − 1.50

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.28
rows.question: A convex lens of focal length 20 cm is placed in contact with concave lens of focal length 60 cm . Calculate : (a) power of each of the lens (b) power of combination (c) focal length of combination (d) nature of combination.
rows.answer:
As we know, power of a lens P= (1)/(f (in metre))
(a) Power of convex lens = 100/20= + 5 D, Power of concave lens = (100)/(− 60) = − 1.67 D
(b) Power of combination P= P1 + P2= (+ 5 D) + (− 1.67 D) = + 3.33 D
(c) Focal length of combination
f (in c m) = 100/P= 100/3.33= + 30.0 c m
(d) As power of combination is positive, therefore, combination behaves like a convex lens.

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.29
rows.question: A convex lens of focal length 10.0 cm is placed in contact with a convex lens of 15.0 cm focal length. What is the focal length of the combination.
rows.answer:
For combination of lenses
1/f= 1/f1 + 1/f2= 1/10 + 1/15= 25/150= 1/6
Therefore, f= 6 c m

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.30
rows.question: Ten identical converging thin lenses, each of focal length 1 0 c m, are in contact. What is the power of the combined lens.
rows.answer:
For thin lenses in contact
& P= P1 + P2 + … … … … … & = 10 P1= (10 × 100)/(10) = 100 D

rows.title: Check Your Knowledge
rows.question:
Check Your Knowledge
A person in a dark room looking through a window can clearly see a person outside in the daylight, whereas the person outside cannot see the person inside. Explain.
rows.answer: When a person inside a darkroom is looking through a window to another person outside in broad daylight, then enough reflected rays come inside to the eye of the observer from the person outside. So he can be seen clearly. But if the person in daylight, looks through the same window to the person inside the dark room, there is hardly any reflected rays coming from the body of the person inside the dark room to the eyes of the observer outside. So the outside observer can't see the person inside the room.

Hence medium A is denser and B is rarer.
Total internal Reflection : When the object is placed in a optically denser medium and if the incident angle is greater than the critical angle then the ray of light gets reflected back to the originating medium. For critical angle ( α ) from medium A to B, B μA
& B μA= (1)/(sin α) or sin α= (1)/(B μA) = μB/μA & ⇒ sin α= (1)/(sin iB) × (sin iA)/(1) & ⇒ α= sin⁻¹ [(sin iA)/(sin iB)]

rows.title: Exercise 2 — Text-Book Exercise — Q7

rows.answer: (d) The waves reflected from the top layer of oil interfere with the wave train reflected from the lower surface of thin oil film producing light and dark coloured pattern.

The distance between object and image in both cases is 90 cm because of principle of reversibility. Hence, there is no shift in image's position.

rows.title: Exercise 2 — Exemplar Questions — Q1
rows.question: A pencil when dipped in water in a glass tumbler appears to be bent at the interface of air and water. Will the pencil appear to bent to be the same extent, if instead of water we use liquid like, kerosene or turpentine. Support your answer with reason.
rows.answer: No, pencil will not appear to bent to the same extent, because bending of light will be different in different liquids since velocity of light at the interface separating two media depends on the relative refractive index of the medium. In case of kerosene, it is more optically denser (refractive index) than water. So, the extent by which light bends in kerosene will be more than how much it bends in water. Hence, the pencil will appear ore bend in kerosene.

Number of images formed when two plane mirrors are inclined at an angle 90° is
(a) 3
(b) 2
(c) 4
(d) 5
rows.answer:
(a) n= (360°)/(90°) = 4
so numbe of images is (n − 1) ⇒ (4 − 1) = 3

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Very Short Answer Questions — Q1
rows.question: What is the relation between focal length and radius of curvature?
rows.answer: Focal length = (Radius of curvature)/(2)

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Very Short Answer Questions — Q2
rows.question: What is reflection of light?
rows.answer: Sending back of light in the same medium by a surface is called reflection of light.

rows.question: A man is moving towards a plane mirror at the speed of 8 c m / s e c. What is the relative speed of man towards his image?
rows.answer: 16 c m / s e c. If an object moves towards the plane mirror with speed v , then image appears to move with speed 2 v.

rows.answer: Benzene

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Very Short Answer Questions — Q6
rows.question: What do you understand by optically denser and optically rarer medium?
rows.answer: Medium in which speed of light is less is known as optically denser medium and the medium in which speed of light is more is optically rarer medium.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Very Short Answer Questions — Q7
rows.question: For the same angle of incidence; the angle of refraction in three different media A, B and C are 150°; 25° and 35° respectively. In which medium velocity of light will be minimum?
rows.answer: In medium B. The medium B has lowest angle of refraction so it is a densest medium.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Very Short Answer Questions — Q8
rows.question: Which lens is known as converging and which is known as diverging lens?
rows.answer: Convex lens is known as converging lens and concave lens is known as diverging lens.

rows.answer: Either at infinity or beyond 2 F.

rows.answer: Its magnification is +1 because the image in a plane mirror is of the same size as the object.

rows.answer: Tracing the reflected ray marking ∠ i and ∠ r

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q1
rows.question: Prove that the refractive index of glass with respect to air is the reciprocal of the refractive index of air with respect to glass.
rows.answer:
Radius of curvature of convex mirror, R= 30 c m.
∴ Focal length of convex mirror, f= R/2= (30 c m)/(2) = 15 c m.
Now h= 5 c m, u= − 20 c m, v=?, h′ = ?
Using the mirror formula 1/f= 1/v + 1/u, we have
& 1/v= 1/f − 1/u= 1/15 − (1)/(− 20) = 1/15 + 1/20= (4 + 3)/(60) & v= 60/7= 8.6 c m
Thus, image is formed at a distance of 8.6 cm behind the convex mirror. The image is virtual and erect.
& m= (h′)/(h) = − v/u & (h′)/(5) = − (8.6)/(− 20) ⇒ h′ = 8.6/20 × 5= 2.15 c m.
So the image size is diminished.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q2
rows.question: What is dispersion of light and what is its cause?
rows.answer:
For convex mirror, we have given
u= − 10 m, R= 2.0 m
So, f= R/2= (2.0 m)/(2) = 1.0 m
Using the mirror formula, 1/u + 1/v= 1/f
We get 1/v= 1/f − 1/u= 1/1.0 + 1/10= 11/10
or v= 10/11= 0.9 m
Thus, the car would appear at 0.9 m from the convex mirror. We know that m= − v/u= (− 10/11)/(− 10) = 1/11
Thus, size of the image of the car will be 1/11 times smaller the actual size of the car through the convex mirror.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q3
rows.question: Find the focal length of a lens of power -2.0 D . What type of lens is this?
rows.answer:
According to cartesian sign convention,
u= − 90 c m, f= − 30 c m.
We have the formula 1/v + 1/u= 1/f
∴ 1/v − 1/90= − 1/30
or 1/v= − 1/30 + 1/90= (− 3 + 1)/(90) = (− 2)/(90)
∴ v= − 45 c m.
Magnification,
& m= (− v)/(u) = (− (− 45))/(− 90) & m= − 1/2
A real, smaller than object and inverted image is formed 45 cm from the mirror on the same side as the object.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q6
rows.question: Refractive index of glass for light of yellow, green and red colours are μy, μg and μr respectively. Rearrange these symbols in the increasing order of values.
rows.answer:
Mirror formula is, 1/f= 1/v + 1/u where u, v and f are object distance, image distance and focal length.
1/v= 1/f − 1/u
Focal length and object distance are both − v e for concave mirror.
It is given u< f,
∴ 1/v becomes (− 1)/(f) + 1/u and is positive.
v becoming + ve denotes a virtual image.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q7
rows.question: An object is placed at a distance of 12 cm in front of a concave mirror. It forms a real image four times larger than the object. Calculate the distance of the image from the mirror.
rows.answer:
An extended object A B is placed in front of a plane mirror M M′. A light ray from A falls perpendicular on a mirror at P. So it comes back along the same path after reflection. Another ray A Q (Q is a point of normal from B), falls on the mirror and after reflection it goes along the path Q R. When these two reflected rays i.e. P A & Q R are produced backward, they meet at a point A′. Similarly, the point B′ is also obtained. It means A′ B′ is the image of AB . This image is virtual and erect which forms as far behind the mirror as the object is in front of it. i.e. A P= A′ P.
B Q= B′ Q
To prove : A B= A′ B′.
△ A′ B′ Q= ∠ A′ Q B′, = ∠ r
(Vertically opposite angles)
∠ i= ∠ r (laws of reflection)
∴ In △ A B Q and △ A′ Q B′, ∠ i= ∠ A′ Q B′
∠ A B Q= ∠ A′ B′ Q= 90°
B Q= B′ Q (because distance of image behind the mirror = distance of object in front of the mirror)
∴ △ A B Q ≅ △ A′ B′ Q. (ASA).
∴ A B= A′ B′ (C P C T) i.e. size of object = size of image.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q8
rows.question: With respect to air, the refractive index of ice is 1.31 and that of rock salt is 1.54 . Calculate the refractive index of rock salt with respect to ice.
rows.answer:
(a) Laws of refraction of light:
(i) The incident ray, the refracted ray at the point of incidence and the normal all lies in the same plane for the two given medium.
(ii) The ratio of sine of angle of incidence (i.e., sin i ) to the sine of angle of refraction (i.e., sin r ) is always constant for the light of given colour and for the given pair of media.
Mathematically, (sin i)/(sin r) = constant = 1 n2
The constant ' n ' is called refractive index of the second medium with respect to the first medium.
Absolute refractive index of the medium is given by
n= (Speed of light in vacuum (c))/(Speed of light in media (v))
(b) Given nA= 2.0 and nB= 1.5
Speed of light in medium B = 2 × 10⁸ m / s
nB= (Speed of light in vacuum (c))/(Speed of light in media B)
1.5= (c)/(2 × 10⁸)
Speed of light in vacuum c= 2 × 10⁸ × 1.5
= 3.0 × 10⁸ m / s
Speed of light in medium ' A '
nA= (Speed of light in vacuum)/(Speed of light in media ' A ')
2.0= (3 × 10⁸)/(Speed of light in media' A′)
Speed of light in medium A= (3 × 10⁸)/(2)
= 1.5 × 10⁸ m / s

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q9
rows.question: A concave mirror and a convex lens are held separately in water. What changes, if any, do you expect in the focal length of either?
rows.answer:
(i) Optical centre: It is a point on the principal axis of the lens, such that a ray of light passing through it goes undeviated.

hi= v/u h 0= (− 10 c m)/(− 20 c m) × 4= 2 c m
size of image is 2 cm .

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q10
rows.question: Briefly describe an activity to find approximate focal length of a convex lens.
rows.answer:
(i) Two light rays whose path of reflection are known are:
(a) The incident ray passes through the center of curvature: In this case, light after reflecting from the concave mirror comes back along the same path. This happens because light is incident prependicular on the mirror surface.
Concave mirror

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q11
rows.question: If the image formed by mirror for all positions of the object placed in front of it is always virtual and diminished, state the type of the mirror. Draw a ray diagram in support of your answer. Where are such mirrors commonly used and why?
rows.answer:
Convex mirror
Use of convex mirror-as rear view mirror in vehicles because its field of view is larger and it forms virtual erect and diminished images of objects behind.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q12
rows.question: What is meant by power of a lens? What does its sign ( + ve or - ve) indicate? State its S.I. unit. How is this unit related to focal length of a lens?
rows.answer:
(i) Ability of lens to converge or diverge the light rays.
(ii) + ve sign → converging lens/convex lens - ve sign → diverging lens/concave lens
(iii) S.I. unit of power is dioptre 1 dioptre = 1 / focal length (m)

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q13
rows.question: An objects 4 cm in height, is placed at 15 cm in front of a concave mirror of focal length 10 cm . At what distance from the mirror should a screen be placed to obtain a sharp image of the object. Calculate the height of the image.
rows.answer:
We know that mirror equation,
& 1/f= 1/v + 1/u ⇒ & 1/v + (1)/(− 15) = (1)/(− 10) ⇒ & 1/v= (1)/(− 10) + 1/15 ⇒ & 1/v= (1)/(− 10) + 1/15 ⇒ & 1/v= ((− 15 + 10))/(150) = (− 5)/(150) & v= − 30 c m
Thus a sharp image of the object will obtain If we place the screen in front of the mirror at a distance of 30 cm from the mirror.
Magnification is given by
& m= (− v)/(u) = h1/h0 ⇒ & (− 30)/(− 15) = h1/4 & − 2= h/4 ⇒ & h1= − 8 c m
The height of image will be 8 cm .

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q14
rows.question: An object is placed at a distance of 30 cm in front of a convex mirror of focal length 15 cm . write four characteristics of the image formed by the mirror.
rows.answer:
Four properties of image formed by the given convex mirror are:
(i) Image is always erect.
(ii) Smaller in size
(iii) Virtual
(iv) Always form behind the mirror between focus and pole

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q15
rows.question: If the image formed by a spherical mirror for all positions of the object placed in front of it is always erect and diminished, what type of mirror is it? Draw a labelled ray diagram to support your answer.
rows.answer:
Convex mirror. It forms an image smaller than that of the object.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Long Answer Questions — Q6
rows.question: Use the mirror formula to show that for an object lying between the pole and focus of a concave mirror, the image formed is always virtual in nature.
rows.answer:
(d) iB> iA or sin−¹ (1/μB) > sin−¹ (1/μA) or μA> μB
Hence medium A is denser and B is rarer.
Total internal Reflection : When the object is placed in a optically denser medium and if the incident angle is greater than the critical angle then the ray of light gets reflected back to the originating medium. For critical angle ( α ) from medium A to B, B μA
& B μA= (1)/(sin α) or sin α= (1)/(B μA) = μB/μA & ⇒ sin α= (1)/(sin iB) × (sin iA)/(1) & ⇒ α= sin⁻¹ [(sin iA)/(sin iB)]

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Long Answer Questions — Q7
rows.question: Prove that the size of image formed by a plane mirror is equal to that of object.
rows.answer: (d) The waves reflected from the top layer of oil interfere with the wave train reflected from the lower surface of thin oil film producing light and dark coloured pattern.

Use these two rays and draw ray diagram to locate the image of an object placed between pole and focus of a concave mirror.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Long Answer Questions — Q11
rows.question:
An object is placed at a distance of 60 cm from a concave lens of focal length 30 cm .
(i) Use lens formula to find the distance of the image from the lens.
(ii) List four characteristics of the image (nature, position, size, erect/inverted) formed by the lens in this case.
(iii) Draw ray diagram to justify your answer of part (ii).
rows.answer:
(20, 2)
For a concave mirror
Given : h= 2 c m, u= − 20 c m, f= − 10 c m
v=? h′ =?
From mirror equation, we know that
1/v= 1/f − 1/u= (1)/(− 10) − (1)/(− 20) = − 1/10 + 1/20= (− 2 + 1)/(20) = (− 1)/(20) ∴ v= − 20 c m.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Long Answer Questions — Q13
rows.question:
Rishi went to a palmist to show his palm. The palmist used a special lens for this purpose.
(i) State the nature of the lens and reason for its use.
(ii) Where should the palmist place/hold the lens so as to have a real and magnified images of an object?
(iii) If the focal length of this lens is 10 cm and the lens is held at a distance of 5 cm from the palm, use lens formula to find the position and size of the image.
rows.answer:
(d) Using, mirror formula,
1/v − 1/u= 1/f
Here, f= + 10 c m, u= − 15 c m
⇒ v= (10 × − 15)/(− 15 + 10) = 30 c m
To get speed of image, differentiation Eq. (i) with respect to time,
(d v)/(d t) = v²/u² ((d u)/(d t))
∴ (d v)/(d t) = ((+ 30)²)/((− 15)²) × (+ 2 c m s−¹) ⇒ (d v)/(d t) = + 8 c m s−¹
So, image is moving at 8 c m s−¹ away from lens.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Reasoning Based Questions — Q1
rows.question: When we sit in front of a plane mirror and write with our right hand, it appears in the mirror that we are writing with the left hand. Why?
rows.answer: It is because of lateral inversion or sideways reversal.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Reasoning Based Questions — Q2
rows.question: Explain why, though both a plane mirror and a sheet of paper reflect light but we can see the image of our face in a plane mirror but not in a sheet of paper?
rows.answer: Sheet of paper produces diffuse or irregular reflection.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Reasoning Based Questions — Q3
rows.question: When a spherical mirror is held towards the Sun and its sharp image is formed on a piece of carbon paper for some time, a hole is burnt in the carbon paper. Why is a hole burnt in the carbon paper?
rows.answer: It is because lot of sun's heat rays are concentrated at the point of sun's image which burn the hole in carbon paper.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Reasoning Based Questions — Q4
rows.question: Explain why, a ray of light passing through the centre of curvature of a concave mirror gets reflected back along the same path.
rows.answer: When a ray of light falls normally on the mirror surface, angle of incidence = angle of reflection = 0°

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Reasoning Based Questions — Q5
rows.question: Why can you not use a cancave mirror as a rear-view mirror in vehicles?
rows.answer: Concave mirror produces inverted image of distant objects. Hence vehicles running on the road will appear upside down. Also, produces large size image, so covers less area behind the vehicle.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Reasoning Based Questions — Q6
rows.question: A ray of light travelling in air enters obliquely into water. Does the ray of light bend towards the normal or away from the normal? Why?
rows.answer: Towards the normal, as its speed decreases rarer to denser medium.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Reasoning Based Questions — Q7
rows.question: Why does a beam of light bend when it enters glass at an angle? Why does it not bend if it enters the glass at right angles?
rows.answer: A beam of light bend when it enters at an angle because its speed decreases in glass. It does not bend, if it enters the glass at right angle as all the parts of light waves reach the glass slab at the same time, enter the glass slab at the same time and hence get slowed down at the same time. Hence no change in direction.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Reasoning Based Questions — Q8
rows.question: Explain why, a real image can be projected on a screen but a virtual image cannot?
rows.answer: A real image can be formed on a screen because light rays actually pass through a real image. And a virtual image cannot be formed on a screen because light rays do not actually pass through a virtual image.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Reasoning Based Questions — Q9
rows.question: Which causes more bending (or more refraction) of light rays passing throught it : a convex lens of long focal length or a convex lens of short focal length? Why?
rows.answer: A convex lens of short focal length causes more bending of light rays passing through it as power of lens, P= (1)/(f (focal length in metre))

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Reasoning Based Questions — Q10
rows.question: Which has more power : a thick convex lens or a thin convex lens, made of the same glass? Give reason for your choice.
rows.answer: Thick convex lens as it has shorter focal length and power of lens, P= (1)/(f (focal length in metre))

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — HOTS Questions — Q1
rows.question: An object of 5 cm is placed 20 cm away from converging lens of focal length 10 cm . Draw the ray diagram to produce the image and find the position, size and the nature of the image formed.
rows.answer:
(i) (a) From figure, angle of incidence, i= 60° and angle of refraction, r= 45°
Refractive index of the medium B relative to medium A, (from Snell's law)
μB A= (sin i)/(sin r) = (sin 60°)/(sin 45°) = (((√(3))/(2)))/(((1)/(√(2)))) = (√(3))/(2)
(ii) (a) Since light rays in the medium B goes towards normal (figure), so it has greater refractive index i.e., denser w.r.t. medium A. Hence, refractive index of medium B relative to medium A is greater than unity.
(iii) (b) In a rectangular glass slab, the emergent rays are parallel to the direction of the incident ray, as the extent of bending of the ray of light at the opposite parallel faces air-glass and glass-air interface of the rectangular glass slab is equal and opposite.
This is why the ray emerges are parallel to the incident ray.
(iv) (d) Among the given material kerosene refractive index, μ= 1.44, water μ= 1.33, mustard oil μ= 1.46 and glycerine μ= 1.74. Glycerine is most optically denser. Therefore, ray of light bend most in glycerine.
(v) (a) (sin i)/(sin r) = n21= v1/v2

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — HOTS Questions — Q2
rows.question: An object 3 cm in height is placed 20 cm from convex lens of focal length 12 cm . Find the nature, position and height of the image.
rows.answer:
Since lens is convex, therefore f is positive.
Given u= − 20 c m, f= + 12 c m, h0= 3 c m, v=?, hi= ?
Since formula for a lens is
1/v − 1/u= 1/f, 1/v − (1)/(− 20) = 1/12
⇒ 1/v + 1/20= 1/12 ⇒ 1/v= 1/12 − 1/20
⇒ 1/v= (5 − 3)/(60) = 1/30 ⇒ v= 30 c m
Since ' v ' is positive, the image is located on the other side of the lens.
m= v/u= (+ 30)/(− 20) = − 1.5
Since m is negative and greater than 1 , the image is real, inverted and larger than the object.
m= hi/h0 ⇒ − 1.5= hi/3 or hi= − 4.5 c m.
Thus the image is 30 cm from the convex lens, located on the other side of the lens. It is real, inverted and enlarged ( 4.5 cm in hight)

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — HOTS Questions — Q3
rows.question: A concave lens has focal length of 15 cm . At what distance should the object from the lens be placed so that it forms an image at 10 cm from the lens ? Also, find the magnification produced by the lens.
rows.answer:
A concave lens always forms a virtual, erect image on the same side of the object.
Image distance v= − 10 c m
Focal length f= − 15 c m,
Object distance u= ?
Since 1/v − 1/u= 1/f or 1/u= 1/v − 1/f
1/u= (1)/(− 10) − (1)/((− 15)) = − 1/10 + 1/15
1/u= (− 3 + 2)/(30) = (1)/(− 30) or u= − 30 c m.
Thus, the object distance is 30 cm .
Magnification, m= v/u or m= (− 10 c m)/(− 30 c m) = 1/3= + 0.33

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — HOTS Questions — Q4
rows.question: A man standing in front of a spherical mirror, finds his image having a very small head, a fat body and legs of normal size. What types of mirrors are used in the small parts?
rows.answer:
A very small head → Convex mirror
A fat body → Concave mirror
Legs of normal size → Plane mirror
Case Based MCQs

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Case Based MCQs — Q1
rows.question:
Read the following and answer any four questions from 1 (𝐢) to 1 (𝐯).
Light travels through a vacuum at a speed c= 3 × 10⁸ m / s. It can also travel through many materials, such as air, water and glass. Atoms in the material absorb, reemit and scatter the light, however. Therefore, light travels through the material at a speed that is less than c, the actual speed depending on the nature of the material. To describe the extent to which the speed of light in a material medium differs from that in a vacuum, we use a parameter called the index of refraction (or refractive index).
(i) Figure shows a ray of light as it travels from medium A to
medium B. Retractive index of the medium B relative to medium A is
(a) (√(3))/(2)
(b) (√(2))/(√(3))
(c) (1)/(√(2))
(d) √(2)
(ii) A light ray enters from medium A to medium B as shown
in the figure. The refractive index of medium B relative to A will be
(a) greater than unity
(b) less than unity
(c) equal to unity
(d) zero
(iii) The path of a ray of light coming from air passing through a rectangular glass slab traced by four students shown as A, B, C and D in the figure. Which one of them is correct?
(a) A
(b) B
(c) C
(d) D
(iv) You are given water, mustard oil, glycerine and kerosene. In which of these media, a ray of light incident obliquely at same angle would bend the most?
(a) Kerosene
(b) Water
(c) Mustard oil
(d) Glycerine
(v) A ray of light is incident in medium 1 on a surface that separates medium 1 from medium 2. Let v1 and v2 represent the velocity of light in medium 1 and medium 2 respectively. Also let n12 and n21 represent the refractive index of medium 1 with respect to medium 2 and refractive index of medium 2 with respect to medium 1 , respectively. If i and r denote the angle of incidence and angle of refraction, then-
(a) (sin i)/(sin r) = n21 v1/v2
(b) (sin i)/(sin r) = n21 v2/v1
(c) (sin i)/(sin r) = n12 v1/v2
(d) (sin i)/(sin r) = n12 v2/v1

rows.question:
Light waves can be polarised as they are
(a) transverse
(b) of high frequency
(c) longitudinal
(d) reflected
rows.answer:
Light waves can be polarised as they are
(a) transverse
(b) of high frequency
(c) longitudinal

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.1
rows.question: Two mirrors make an angle of 120° with each other as in Fig. 1.9, A ray is incident on mirror M1 at an angle of 65° to the normal. Find the direction of the ray after it is reflected from mirror M2.
rows.answer:
From the law of reflection, we know that the first reflected ray also makes an angle of 65° with the normal. Thus, this ray makes an angle of 90° − 65°, or 25°, with the horizontal. We build the geometric model triangle shown in Figure 1.9 from the triangle made by the first reflected ray and the two mirrors. The first reflected ray makes an angle of 35° with M2 (because the sum of the interior angles of any triangle is 180° ). This means that this ray makes an angle of 55° with the normal to M2. Hence, from the law of reflection, the second reflected ray makes an angle of 55° with the normal to M2.
Fig. 1.9 Mirrors M1 and M2 make an angle of 120° with each other.

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.2
rows.question: Find the minimum height of a plane mirror where one can see his full image.
rows.answer:
Let H L be the height of the person and E, the position of his eyes. Now applying laws of reflection, we have, M′ L′ = 1/2 E L and M H′ = 1/2 H E
Now, H′ L′ = M M′ − M H′ − L′ M′
= H L − 1/2 E L − 1/2 H E= H L − 1/2 H L= 1/2 H L
So the required height of the mirror be half of the height of the person.
Fig. 1.15

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.3
rows.question: Two plane mirrors are inclined at an angle θ. A ray of light is incident on one mirror at an angle of incidence i. The ray is reflected from this mirror, falls on the second mirror from where it is reflected parallel to the first mirror. What is the value of i, the angle of incidence in term of θ ?
rows.answer:
The situation is illustrated in fig. 1.16. X A is the incident ray. B C is the final reflected ray. It is given that B C is parallel to mirror M1. Look at the assignment of the angles carefully. Now N2 is normal to mirror M2.
Therefore β= θ
Then from △ O A B, θ + β + 90° − i= 180°
or θ + θ + 90° − i= 180° or i= 2 θ − 90°
Fig. 1.16
Thus if the angle of incidence is i= 2 θ − 90°, then the final reflected ray will be parallel to the first mirror.

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1. 4
rows.question: Find the x and y co-ordinates, of the image of a point A (2, 0). M N is a plane mirror.
rows.answer:
Image of A will be at A′ (6, 0). Perpendicular distance of image from plane mirror = perpendicular distance of object from plane mirror. Fig. 1.17 ## SPHERICAL MIRRORS A highly polished curved surface whose reflecting surface is a cut part of a hollow sphere of a glass or any polished metal is called spherical mirror. Spherical mirrors are of two types: ## Concave Mirrors Imagine a sphere of hollow glass. If we cut out a spherical cap and polished it with silver on the outside, we have a concave mirror if we look at it from the hollow side. These mirrors, which are referred to as convergent mirrors, concentrate the light rays, causing them to arrive onto a point known as the focal point. Concave mirrors form real images that can be projected onto a screen if the object is farther away than the focal point. If we place the object closer than the focal point, the image is formed upright and large but virtual (i.e., it cannot be projected). ## Convex Mirrors If we polished the inner surface of a concave mirror with silver and look at it toward the outward bulge, we have a convex mirror. Mirrors of this type are also called divergent mirrors because the light beams that reach their surface and are parallel to their main axis are diverted in such a way that they separate, but their extensions come back together again in a point known as the focal point. The images produced by a convex mirror are always virtual, upright, and small. ## Take a large shining spoon. Try to view your face in its curved surface - Do you get the image ? Is it smaller or larger? - Move the spoon slowly away from your face. Observe the image. How does it change? - Reverse the spoon and repeat the Activity. How does the image look like now? - Compare the characteristics of the image on the two surfaces. ## TERMS RELATED TO SPHERICAL MIRROR Centre of curvature ( C ) : It is the centre of sphere of which the mirror is a part. Radius of curvature (R) : It is the radius of the sphere of which the mirror is a part. Pole (P) : It is the geometrical centre of the spherical reflecting surface. Principal axis : It is the straight line joining the centre of curvature to the pole. Focus (F) : When a narrow beam of rays of light, parallel to the principal axis and close to it ( know as paraxial rays), is incident on the surface of a mirror, the reflected beam is found to converge ( concave mirror) or appear to diverge ( convex mirror) from a point on principal axis. This point is called focus. Focal length (f) : It is the distance between the pole and the principal focus. For spherical mirrors, f= R / 2. ## RULES FOR RAY DIAGRAMS (i) A ray, parallel to the principal axis will after reflection, pass through the focus. Fig. 1.19 (a), and (b) mirror then it reflects back along the same path. Fig. 1.20 (a) and (b) (ii) When a ray falls in the direction of centre of curvature of mirror then it reflects back along the same path. Fig. 1.20 (a) and (b) Fig. 1.20 Fig. 1.20 (iii) A ray, passing through the focus is reflected parallel to the principal axis. Fig. 1.21 ( a ), and ( b ) ## Physics ## REFLECTION THROUGH CONCAVE MIRROR F → Principal focus C → Centre of curvature P F= Focal length When a narrow beam of light travelling parallel to the principal axis is incident on the reflecting surface of the concave mirror, the beam after reflection converge at a point on the principal axis. ## IMAGE FORMED BY THE CONCAVE MIRROR P → Pole of mirror P C= Radius of curvature
Position of objectPosition of imageNatureRay diagram
At infinityAt the focusReal, inverted \& diminished
Between infinity \& centre of curvatureBetween focus \& centre of curvatureReal, inverted \& small in size
At centre of curvatureAt centre of curvatureReal, inverted and of same size
Between focus \& centre of curvatureBeyond centre of curvatureReal, inverted and enlarged
At focusAt infinityReal, inverted and very large
Between Focus \& PoleBehind the mirrorErect, virtual \& enlarged
## Uses of Concave Mirror Makeup and shaving mirrors are concave mirrors. When you place your face between the mirror and its focal point, you see an enlarged virtual image of yourself. Concave mirrors are also used in a new method for displaying the speed of a car, as a dentist mirror, in floodlight etc. ## Cautions: Before doing this activity "Do not look at the Sun directly or even into a mirror reflecting sunlight. It may damage your eyes." - Hold a concave mirror in your hand and direct its reflecting surface towards the Sun. - Direct the light reflected by the mirror on to a sheet of paper held close to the mirror. - Move the sheet of paper back and forth gradually unitl you find on the paper sheet a bright, sharp spot of light. - Hold the mirror and the paper in the same position for a few minutes. What do you observe? Why? ## REFLECTION THROUGH CONVEX MIRROR When a narrow beam of light travelling parallel to the principal axis is incident on the reflecting surface of the mirror, the beam after reflection appear to diverge from a point on the principal axis. When a ray incident on convex mirror in the direction of centre of curvature after reflection comes back along the same path. When a ray incident on convex mirror parallel to the principal axis, after reflection, appears to come from the focus. ## IMAGE FORMED BY CONVEX MIRROR A convex mirror forms only virtual images for all positions of the real object. The image is always virtual, erect, smaller than the object and is located between the pole and the focus. The image becomes smaller and moves closer to the focus as the object is moved away from the mirror. Fig. 1.24 (a) Fig. 1.24 (b) The virtual image is diminished in size and upright, relative to the object. A convex mirror form a virtual image of the object, no matter where in front of the mirror the object is placed. ## Uses of Convex Mirror Convex mirrors, give a wider field of view than do other types of mirrors. Therefore, they are often used for security purposes, as in figure 1.25. A mirror with a wide field of view is also needed to give a driver a good rear view. Thus, the outside mirror on the passenger side is often a convex mirror. Printed on such a mirror is usually the warning "vehicles in mirror are closer than they appear." Fig. 1.25 : Security mirror Fig. 1.26 : Rear view mirror The reason for the warning is that the virtual image in figure is reduced in size and therefore looks smaller, just as a distant object would appear in a plane mirror. An unwary driver, thinking that the side-view mirror is a plane mirror, might incorrectly deduce from the small size of the image that the car behind is far enough away to ignore. Convex mirror also used in street lighting. Virtual image cannot be obtained on a screen. In case of mirror virtual image is formed behind the mirror and in case of lens, virtual image is formed on the same side as the object. ## COMPARING THE FIELD OF VIEW OF A PLANE MIRROR AND A CONVEX MIRROR For a fair comparison to be made the two mirrors must be the same size and the eye must be placed at the same distance from each of them. The maximum angle of view is obtained when the angle of reflection at the mirror is a maximum, i.e., when the normal to the mirror are drawn at the extreme edges of the mirror. The normals to the convex mirror are lines which are continuations of the radii at the edges of the mirror. Once the angles of reflection have been drawn, equal angles must be drawn on the other side of the normals to give the position of the incident rays. The angle of incidence for the convex mirror is much greater than the angle of incidence for the plane mirror, hence the convex mirror has a greater field of view than that of plane mirror. ## SIGN CONVENTION (1) All distances are measured from the pole. (2) The distance measured along the direction of propagation of light are taken as positive and the direction opposite to the propagation of light is taken as negative. (3) The distances (heights) measured above the principal axis i.e. along positive Y axis, are taken as positive while distances below the principal axis i.e. along negative Y axis are taken as negative. In this convention, the focal length ( f ) and radius of curvature ( R ) are negative for a concave mirror and positive for a convex mirror. The sign convention can be summarized in the following figure. Note : Many students get confused in applying sign convention and a small error in sign of one variable will throw all your calculation. Please study 3 points carefully ( put known quantities with proper sign, unknown quantity will come automatically with proper sign). In following situations, the sign convention has been applied. 1. Consider the mirror shown in Fig. 1.28 (a). Here, the object is on the left and image is also on the left. The mirror is a concave mirror. The incident ray is directed from left to right and so the positive X-axis is also from left to right. Here, object distance u= − P O, image distance v= − P I radius of curvature R= − P C, focal length f= negative. Note : Many students get confused in applying sign convention and a small error in sign of one variable will throw all your calculation. Please study 3 points carefully ( put known quantities with proper sign, unknown quantity will come automatically with proper sign). 2. Consider the mirror shown in Fig. 1.28 (b). Here, the object is on the left and image is on the right and the mirror is concave. The incident ray is once again from left to right and so the positive X-axis. Here, object distance u= − P O, image distance v= + P I, radius of curvature R= − P C, focal length f= negative. 3. Consider the mirror shown in Fig. 1.27 (c). Here, the object is virtual and on the left, image is also on the right and the mirror is a convex. The incident ray is from the right and the positive X-axis is from right to left. Here, object distance u= + P O, image distance v= − P I, radius of curvature R= + P C, focal length f= positive. ## RELATION BETWEEN FOCAL LENGTH (f) AND RADIUS OF CURVATURE ( R ) FOR SPHERICAL MIRRORS The focal length (f) of a mirror is half of the radius of curvature (R), that is, f= R/2 Proof : In fig. 1.29, incident ray O X is parallel to the principal axis. After reflection, it actually passes through focus F in case of concave mirror and appears to come from focus F in case of a convex mirror. CXN is normal to mirror at X. For concave mirror (Fig. 1.29 (a) (because ∠ i= ∠ r ) ∠ O X C= ∠ F X C= θ But ∠ O X C= ∠ X C F (alternate angles) For convex mirror (Fig. 1.29 (b) ∠ O X N= ∠ Y X N= θ But ∠ O X N= ∠ X C F (corresponding angles) and ∠ Y X N= ∠ F X C (vertically opposite) Hence (in both cases), ∠ F X C= ∠ X C F there fore, in △ F C X, F C= F X For mirror of small aperture, X will be near P, then F X = F P Hence, F C= P F and 2 P F= P F + P F= P F + F C= P C i.e. 2 f= R or f= R / 2 (Proved). Light-Reflection and Refraction - Her eye at point 𝐏 looks into the mirror. Which of the numbered cards can she see reflected in the mirror? Card number 2, she can see through the mirror. According to law of reflection, angle of incidence is equal to angle of reflection. Light ray coming from card number 2 makes the same angle with the normal at the point of incidence as the light ray going to eye makes with the normal. ## MIRROR FORMULA A relationship among the object distance (u), the image distance (v) and the focal length (f) of a mirror is called the mirror formula. The formula is given by 1/f= 1/u + 1/v Proof: Take an object A B beyond C of a concave mirror M M′. A ray A D parallel to principal axis passes through focus after reflection. Another ray A E which is passing through C comes back along the same path after reflection. These two reflected rays intersect at A′. From A′ draw perpendicular A′ B′ on the principal axis. So A′ B′ is real and inverted image which is formed between C and F which is smaller than the object in size. Draw D G perpendicular to the principal axis. So, applying sign convention, we get P B= − u; P B′ = − v; P F= − f; P C= − 2 f In △ A B C and △ A′ B′ C, ∠ A B C= ∠ A′ B′ C= 90° ∠ A C B= ∠ A′ C B′ (Vertically opposite angles) ∴ △ A B C ~ △ A′ B′ C ( A A similarity) (A B)/(A′ B′) = (B C)/(B′ C) (Corresponding sides of similar triangles are in proportion)... In △ D G F and △ A′ B′ F, ∠ D G F= ∠ A′ B′ F= 90° ∠ D F G= ∠ A′ F B′ (Vertically opposite angles) △ D G F ~ △ A′ B′ F ( A A similarity) (D G)/(A′ B′) = (G F)/(B′ F) (Corresponding sides of similar triangles are in proportion) But A B= D G (the perpendicular distance between two parallel lines are equal) ∴ (A B)/(A′ B′) = (G F)/(B′ F) From eq. (1) and (2), we get, (B C)/(B′ C) = (G F)/(B′ F) Let us assume that mirror is very small, ∴ G and P are very close to each other so that G F= P F. From (3), (B C)/(B′ C) = (P F)/(B′ F) ⇒ (P B − P C)/(P C − P B′) = (P F)/(P B′ − P F) ⇒ (− u − (− 2 f))/(− 2 f − (− v)) = (− f)/(− v − (− f)) Fig. 1.32 ⇒ (− u + 2 f)/(− 2 f + v) = (− f)/(− v + f) ⇒ (− v + f) (− u + 2 f) = − f (− 2 f + v) ⇒ v u − 2 f v − f u + 2 f²= 2 f² − f v ⇒ u v= − f v + 2 f v + f u ∴ u v= v f + u f Dividing both sides by u v f, (u v)/(u v f) = (v f)/(u v f) + (u f)/(u v f) ⇒ 1/f= 1/u + 1/v

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.5
rows.question: The radius of curvature of a spherical mirror is 2 0 c m. What is its focal length ?
rows.answer:
As we know, focal length (f) of a mirror is half of the radius of curvature (R).
∴ f= R/2= 20/2= 10 c m

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.6
rows.question: An object is placed at a distance of 1 0 c m from a convex mirror of focal length 1 5 c m. Find the position and nature of the image.
rows.answer:
Given : u= − 10 c m, f= + 15 c m, v= ?
Using the mirror formula 1/f= 1/u + 1/v, we have
1/v= 1/f − 1/u= 1/15 − (1)/((− 10)) or 1/v= 1/15 + 1/10= (2 + 3)/(30) = 5/30 or v= 30/5= 6 c m.
Hence, the image is formed at a distance of 6 cm behind the mirror. The image is virtual and erect.

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.7
rows.question: We wish to obtain an erect image of an object, using a concave mirror of focal length 1 5 c m. What should be the range of distance of the object from the mirror ? What is the nature of the image? Is the image larger or smaller than the object? Draw a ray diagram to show the image formation in this case.
rows.answer:
Here, focal length, f= − 15 c m.
For getting an erect image using a concave mirror the object should be placed at a distance less than the focal length (i.e.) 15 cm from pole. Image will be virtual, enlarged and erect.
Fig. 1.33
## MAGNIFICATION
If the mirror is plane, the size of the image is always equal to the size of the object i.e., magnification is unity. But the case is different for a curved mirror. The size of the image is different from the size of the object in such a 'mirror'. Image may be greater or smaller in size than the object depending upon the nature of the mirror or the location of the object.
Let I and O be the size of the image and the object respectively. The ratio I/O is called magnification, and it is denoted by m.
Magnification, m= I/O
This is also called linear magnification.
Superficial magnification: It is the ratio of area of image to the area of object. Superficial magnification = (area of image)/(area of object) = ((Iarea)²)/((Oarea)²) Other formulae of magnification
Magnification, m= − v/u= − ((v − f)/(f)) = (f − v)/(f) = − ((f)/(u − f)) = (f)/(f − u)
## NEWTON'S FORMULA
This formula provides a relationship among the focal length ( f ), the distance of the object from focus ( x ) and the distance of the image from focus ( y ).
This can be expressed as x y= f²
Note that here the object distance and the image distance are measured from the focus of the mirror not from the pole.

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.8
rows.question: An object 3 cm high is placed at a distance of 10 cm in front of a concave mirror of focal length 20 cm . Find the position, nature and size of the image formed.
rows.answer:
Here, size of object, h= 3 c m
u= − 10 c m (sign convention)
f= − 20 c m (sign convention)
Using 1/u + 1/v= 1/f, we have − 1/10 + 1/v= − 1/20 or 1/v= − 1/20 + 1/10= 1/20∴ v= 20 c m
Thus, image is formed at a distance of 20 cm from the concave mirror. Since v is positive, so image is formed behind the concave mirror. It means the image is virtual (nature).
Using, m= (h′)/(h) = − (v)/(u′) we get (h′)/(h) = (− 20 c m)/(− 10 c m) = 2
or h′ = 2 × h= 2 × 3 c m= 6 c m
Thus, size of the image is 6 cm .
## ILLUSTRATIOM : 1.9
An object, 4.0 cm in size, is placed at 25.0 cm in front of a concave mirror of focal length 15.0 cm . At what distance from the mirror should a screen be placed in order to obtain a sharp image? Find the nature and the size of the image.
Object-size, h= + 4.0 c m; Object-distance, u= − 25.0 c m; Focal length, f= − 15.0 c m;
Image-distance, v= ? Image-size, h′ = ?
From 1/v + 1/u= 1/f or 1/v= 1/f − 1/u= (1)/(− 15.0) − (1)/(− 25.0) = − 1/15.0 + 1/25.0
or 1/v= (− 5.0 + 3.0)/(75.0) = (− 2.0)/(75.0) or v= − 37.5 c m.
The screen should be placed at 37.5 cm . from the mirror. The image is real.
Also, magnification, m= (h′)/(h) = − v/u or h′ = − (v h)/(u) = ((− 37.5 c m) (+ 4.0 c m))/((− 25.0 c m))
Height of the image, h′ = − 6.0 c m.
The image is inverted and enlarged.
## ILLUSTRATIOM : 1.10
A convex mirror used for rear-view on an automobile has a radius of curvature of 3. 0 0 m. If a bus is located at 5. 0 0 𝐦 from this mirror, find the position, nature and size of the image.
Radius of curvature, R= + 3.00 m; Object-distance, u= − 5.00 m; Image-distance, v= ? Height of the image, h′ = ?
Focal length, f= R / 2= + (3.00 m)/(2) = + 1.50 m
Since 1/v + 1/u= 1/f or 1/v= 1/f − 1/u= + 1/1.50 − (1)/((− 5.00)) = 1/1.50 + 1/5.00= (5.00 + 1.50)/(7.50) ⇒ v= (+ 7.50)/(6.50) = + 1.15 m
The image is 1.15 m at the back of the mirror.
Magnification, m= (h′)/(h) = − v/u= − (1.15 m)/(− 5.00 m) = + 0.23
The image is virtual, erect and smaller in size by a factor of 0.23 .

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.11
rows.question: An object is placed at a distance of 8 c m from a concave mirror of focal length 1 0 c m. Find the position and nature of the image formed. Also draw the ray diagram.
rows.answer:
Here, (using sign convention)
Focal length, f= − 10 c m
Object distance, u= − 8 c m
Image distance, v= ?
From mirror formula, 1/u + 1/v= 1/f
Putting values, we get
1/v= (1)/(− 10) − (1)/(− 8) = (− 4 + 5)/(40) = 1/40
∴ v= + 40 c m
The image is formed at a distance of 40 cm behind the mirror.
It is virtual, erect and enlarged.
The ray diagram is shown.
Incident rays taken are one parallel to principal axis and the other appearing to come from side of centre. Reflected ray appear to come from A′, forming virtual image of A, I A′ is erect, virtual of real obejct O A.

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.12
rows.question: An object 4.0 cm in size is placed at a distance of 25.0 cm in front of a convex mirror of radius of curvature 40 cm . Find (i) the position, (ii) the size and (iii) nature of the image.
rows.answer:
Here, h= + 4.0 c m
u= − 25.0 c m (sign conventions)
R= + 40 c m
∴ f= R/2= 40/2= 20.0 c m
(i) Determination of the position of the image.
Using, 1/u + 1/v= 1/f we get 1/v= 1/f − 1/u= 1/20 − (1)/((− 25)) = 1/20 + 1/25= 9/100
or v= 100/9 11.11 c m
Thus, the image is at 11.11 cm behind the convex mirror.
(ii) Determination of the size of the image
Using, m= (h′)/(h) = − v/u or h′ = − v/u h or, h′ = − ((100 / 9) (4))/(− 25) = 16/9= 1.78 c m
Thus, size of image = 1.78 c m.
(iii) Nature of the image : Since h′ is positive, so the image is erect.

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.13
rows.question: A concave mirror of focal length f produces a real image 𝐧 times the size of the object. What is the distance of the object from the mirror?
rows.answer:
Here, magnification, m= − n; m= (f)/(f − u)
− n= (− f)/(− (f − u)) ⇒ n f − n u= − f
− n u= − f − n f ⇒ u= ((n + 1))/(n) f

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.14
rows.question: The focal length of a concave mirror is 3 0 c m. Find the position of the object in front of the mirror, so that the image is three times the size of the object.
rows.answer:
Here image can be real or virtual. If the image is real
f= − 30 c m, u=?, m= − 3
m= (f)/(f − u) ⇒ − 3= (− 30)/(− 30 − u) ⇒ u= − 40 c m.
If the image is virtual m= (f)/(f − u) ⇒ 3= (− 30)/(− 30 − u) ⇒ u= − 20 c m.
## REFRACTION OF LIGHT
The bending of the light ray from its path in passing from one medium to the other medium is called refraction of light.
If the refracted ray bends towards the normal relative to the incident ray (passing obliquely), then the second medium is said to be denser than the first medium. But if the refracted ray bends away from the normal, then the second medium is said to be rarer than the first medium.
Fig. 1.43 (a)
Fig. 1.43 (b)
If a ray of light passing normally i.e., at right angles from one medium to another optical medium then it does not bend or deviate from its path.
Cause of refraction of light: Refraction of light takes place due to change in the speed of light as it enters from one medium to another medium.
Refraction causes many illusions. One of them is the apparent bending of a stick that is partially submerged in water. The submerged part appears closer to the surface than it actually is. The same is true when you look at a fish in water. The fish appears nearer to the surface and closer than it really is (Fig. 1.44). If we look straight down into water, an object submerged 4 meters beneath the surface appears to be only 3 meters deep. Because of refraction, submerged objects appear to be magnified.
Fig.1.44 Because of refraction, a submerged object appears to be nearer to the surface than it actually is
## Atmospheric Refraction
Refraction occurs in the earth's atmosphere. Whenever we watch a sunset, we see the sun for several minutes after it has sunk below the horizon (Fig. 1.45). The earth's atmosphere is thin at the top and dense at the bottom. Because light travels faster in thin air than in dense air, parts of the wavefronts of sunlight at high altitude travel faster than parts closer to the ground. Light rays bend. The density of the atmosphere changes gradually, so light rays bend gradually and follow a curved path. So we gain additional minutes of daylight each day. Furthermore, when the sun (or moon) is near the horizon, the rays from the lower edge are bent more than the rays from the upper edge. This shortens the vertical diameter, causing the sun to appear elliptical figure).
Fig. 1.45. Because of atmospheric refraction, when the sun is near the horizon it appears to be higher in the sky.
If, while standing on the bank of a stream, you wished to spear a fish swimming in the water out in front of you, would you aim above, below, or directly at the observed fish to make a direct hit? If you decided instead to zap the fish with a laser, would you aim above, below, or directly at the observed fish? Defend your answer.
When you try to hit the fish with a spear, you need to aim below as the fish is actually at a lower depth than that observed by you from outside the water due to refraction of light.
When you aim with a laser light then you should aim above as the path of laser will change as it enters in water. Since it is going from rarer to denser medium, it bends towards the normal and deviates from the original path.
## Laws of Refraction
There is two laws of refraction
1. The incident ray, the refracted ray, and the normal to the refracting surface at the point of incidence are in the same plane.
2. The ratio of sine of angle of incidence to the sine of angle of refraction is constant for a pair of media i.e. (sin i)/(sin r) = constant (1 μ2)
Where μ2 is the refractive index of medium 2 w.r.t. medium 1. This law is also known as snell's law.
## Application of Snell's Law
When light passes from rarer to denser medium it bends toward the normal fig 1.46 (a).
Using Snell's law
μ1 sin θ1= μ2 sin θ2 ⇒ (sin θ1)/(sin θ2) = μ2/μ1
Thus, if μ2> μ1 then θ2< θ1
When light passes from denser to rarer medium it bends away from the normal fig 1.46(b).
From Snell's law, (sin θ1)/(sin θ2) = μ2/μ1
Fig. 1.46 (a) Ray bending towards normal.
Fig. 1.46 (b) Ray bending away from normal.
When light propagates through a series of layers of different medium, then according to Snell's law
μ1 sin ϕ1= μ2 sin ϕ2= μ3 sin ϕ3= _ _ _ _ = constant
Did You Know?
If 1 μ2, 2 μ3 and 3 μ1 be refractive index of medium 2 w.r.t. medium 1, refractive index of medium 3 w.r.t medium 2 and refractive index of medium 1 w.r.t. medium 3 respectively then 1 μ2 × 2 μ3 × 3 μ1= 1.
## REFRACTIVE INDEX
Light travels through a vacuum at a speed c= 3.00 × 10⁸ m / s. It can also travel through many materials, such as air, water and glass. Atoms in the material absorb, re-emit and scatter the light, however. Therefore, light travels through the material at a speed that is less than c , the actual speed depending on the nature of the material.
To describe the extent to which the speed of light in a material medium differs from that in a vacuum, we use a parameter called the index of refraction (or refractive index).
## Absolute Refractive Index
It is defined as the ratio of speed of light in free space c to that in a given medium v.
i.e. μ or n= c/v
Also, μ or n= (sin i)/(sin r) (incident ray in vacuum or air)
It is a scalar and has no units and dimensions.
As in vacuum or free space, speed of light of all wavelengths is maximum and equal to c , so, for all wavelengths the refractive index of free space is minimum and is μ= c/v= c/c= 1
For a given light, denser is the medium, lesser will be the speed of light and so greater will be the refractive index, e.g. as vglass< vwater, μG> μw
i.e. for a given light refractive index depends on nature of medium [i.e., μ ∝ (1 / v)
For a given medium (other than free-space), the speed of light of different wavelengths is different, i.e., v ∝ λ and μ= (c / v), μ ∝ 1/λ i.e. greater the wavelength of light lesser will be the refractive index e.g. λR> λB, so in water or glass μR> μB, i.e., for a given medium (other than free space) refractive index depends on wavelength of light.
As for light in free space c= f λ0 vand in a medium v= f λ,
μ= c/v= λ0/λ
i.e., for a given light and medium refractive index is equal to the ratio of wavelength of light in free space to that in the medium. Refractive index decreases with the increase in temperature.
## Relative Refractive Index
When light passes from one medium to the other, the refractive index of medium 2 relative to 1 is written as 1 μ2 and is defined as 1 μ2= μ2/μ1= ((c / v2))/((c / v1)) = v1/v2
While using the concept of relative μ, it must be kept in mind that :
2 μ1= μ1/μ2= v2/v1 So that, (1 μ2) (2 μ1) = v1/v2 × v2/v1= 1 i.e., 1 μ2= (1)/(2 μ1)
Usually ' μ ' is used for relative refractive index and it implies the refractive index of denser medium relative to rarer one,
i.e., μ= μD/μR= vR/vD> 1
In lens theory μ is used for the refractive index of material of lens relative to the medium,
i.e., μ= ((μ)Lens)/((μ)Medium) and can be greater than, less than or equal to unity.
If you place a glass test tube in water, you will be able to see the tube. If you place it in clear soyabean oil, you may not be able to see it. What does this tell you about the speed of light in the oil and in the glass?
If a glass test tube is placed in water and it can be seen, this indicates the speed of light in glass and water is different. So refraction takes place.
When the same glass tube is placed in soyabean oil, it cannot be seen that means the speed of light is same in both oil and glass. So no refraction takes place between the two media.

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.15
rows.question: Light waves of 5895 \AA wavelength travels from vacuum to a medium of refractive index of 1.5 . Find the velocity of light and wavelength in medium.
rows.answer:
If velocity of light in vacuum is c then velocity of light in medium is v= c/n= (3 × 10⁸)/(1.5) = 2 × 10⁸ m / s e c
Wavelength of light in medium λm= λ/n= 5895/1.5= 3930 \AA.
## ILLUSTRATIOM : 1.16
The refractive index of water is 4 / 3 and of glass is 3 / 2. Calculate the refractive index of glass with respect to water.
Let the 3 media be 1,2 and 3,1 for air, 2 for water, 3 for glass in that order.
Given : 1 μ2= 4/3, 2 μ3=?, 3 μ1= (1)/(3 / 2), [∵ 1 μ3= 3/2 and ∴ 3 μ1 is the reciprocal of 1 μ3]
Now using the formula, 1 μ2 · 2 μ3 · 3 μ1= 1 and substituting values, we have
4/3 · 2 μ3 · 2/3= 1 ⇒ 2 μ3= (1 × 3 × 3)/(4 × 2) = 9/8
∴ The refractive index of glass with respect to water is 9 / 8

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.17
rows.question: The refractive index of a material is 1.33 . If velocity of light in vacuum is 3 × 10⁸ m s−¹, find the velocity of light in the material.
rows.answer:
Refractivge index, μ= (Velocity of light in vacuum)/(Velocity of light in material)
∴ 1.33= (3 × 10⁸ m s−¹)/(Velocity of light in material)
∴ Velocity of light in material = (3 × 10⁸)/(1.33) = 2.25 × 10⁸ m s−¹
## LLUSTRATION : 1.18
The angle of incidence in air for a ray of light is 4 0°. If ray travels through water of refractive index 4 / 3, find angle of refraction.
According to snell's law, a μw= (sin i)/(sin r)
∴ & sin r= (sin i)/(a) μw= (sin 40)/(4 / 3) = (3 × 0.6427)/(4) = 0.4820 & r= sin⁻¹ (0.4820) = 28.82° (approx.)

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.19
rows.question: Light reflected from a fish strikes the surface of the water at an angle of 3 8° to the normal. What is the angle of refraction of the light into the air ?
rows.answer:
Snell's law gives, nw sin θ1= na sin θ2 ⇒ sin θ2= (1.33) sin 38°
Therefore, the angle of refraction of the light into air θ2= 55°

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.20
rows.question: Light strikes an interface between two materials of refractive indices n1 and n2, at an angle θ1 to the normal to the surface. Show that a ray of light is bent towards the normal if n1< n2 and that a ray is bent away from the normal if n1> n2.
rows.answer:
Snell's law gives for any light ray sin θ2= n1/n2 sin θ1
If n1< n2 then sin θ2< sin θ1 and θ2< θ1. This means that the ray is bent toward the normal.
If n1> n2 then sin θ2> sin θ1 and θ2> θ1. This means that the ray is bent away from the normal.

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.21
rows.question: One light wave is incident upon a plate of refractive index μ. Find the incident angle i, for which refractive and reflective waves are mutually perpendicular.
rows.answer:
According to snell's laws, (sin i)/(sin r) = μ
Angle between refractive and reflective waves = 180° − (i + r) = 90° i + r= 90° ⇒ r= 90° − i
μ= (sin i)/(sin (90° − i)) = (sin i)/(cos i) = tan i ⇒ i= tan−¹ (μ)

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.22
rows.question:
Figure 1.49, shows the path of ray of light passing through a glass slab. By geometrical construction calculate the refractive index of glass applying Snell's law.
Fig. 1.49
rows.answer:
A O is the incident ray on the surface of glass and O B is the refracted ray through the glass. With O as centre draw a circle of some suitable radius cutting the incident ray at A and the refracted ray at
B. Draw perpen
diculars A C and B D to the normal C O D.
Refractive index μ= (sin i)/(sin r) = (A C)/(B D) = (1.2 c m)/(0.8 c m) = 1.5
Fig. 1.50

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.23
rows.question: Two converging lenses with focal lengths 15 cm and 25 cm are placed 18 cm apart. An object is located 8.0 cm to the left of the 15 cm focal length lens. Where is the final image formed ?
rows.answer:
Find the image location for the first lens using the lens formula, 1/v − 1/u= 1/f
1/v1= 1/f1 + 1/u1= 1/15 − 1/8.0 which gives v1= − 17 c mv
This image forms the object of the second lens located 17 c m + 18 c m= 35 c m away. Then for the second lens
1/v2= 1/f2 + 1/u2= 1/25 − 1/35 which gives v2= 87.5 c m
The final image lies 87.5 cm to the right of the second lens (25 c m) lens.

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.24
rows.question: An object is placed at a distance 1.50 m from a screen and a convex lens placed in between produces an image magnified 4 times on the screen. What is the focal length and the position of the lens?
rows.answer:
Here, magnification m= I/O= − 4
Let lens is placed at a distance of x from the object. Then
u= − x, and v= (1.5 − x)
using m= v/u, we get − 4= (1.5 − x)/(− x)
or 4 x= 1.5 − x or 5 x= 1.5
Thus x= 0.3 m
The lens is placed at a distance of 0.3 m from the object (or 1.20 m from the screen)
For focal length, we may use m= (f)/(f + u)
or − 4= (f)/(f + (− 0.3)) or − 4 f + 1.2= f or 5 f= 1.2
Thus f= 1.2/5= 0.24
The focal length is 0.24 m (or 24 cm )

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.25
rows.question:
The radius of curvature of a thin plano-convex lens is 1 0 c m (of curved surface) and the refractive index is 1.5 . If the plane surface is silvered, then it behaves like a concave mirror of focal length
(a) 1 0 c m (b)
15 cm
(c) 20 c m
(d) 5 cm
rows.answer:
(a) The silvered plano convex lens behaves as a concave mirror; whose focal length is given by
1/F= 2/f1 + 1/fm
If plane surface is silvered
& fm= R2/2= (∞)/(2) = ∞ & ∴ 1/f1= (μ − 1) (1/R1 − 1/R2) = (μ − 1) (1/R − (1)/(∞)) = (μ − 1)/(R) & ∴ 1/F= (2 (μ − 1))/(R) + (1)/(∞) = (2 (μ − 1))/(R) ⇒ F= (R)/(2 (μ − 1))
Fig. 1.70
Here, R= 10 c m, μ= 1.5
∴ F= (10)/(2 (1.5 − 1)) = 10 c m
## POWER OF A LENS
If focal length of a lens is measured in metre then its reciprocal gives the power (P) of the lens.
i.e., Power of a lens, P= (1)/(f (in m))
The S.I. unit of power is diopter (D).
Power of a combination of lenses: If a number of lenses are placed in close contact with each other, then the power of the combination of lenses is equal to the algebraic sum of the powers of individual lenses. i.e. P= P1 + P2 + …. Pn
## ILLUSTRATIOM : 1.26
A doctor has prescribed a corrective lens of power +1.5 D . Find the focal length of the lens. Is the prescribed lens diverging or converging?
Given: P= + 1.5 D
Focal length f= 100/P c m= 100/1.5= 1000/15= + 66.67 c m= + 0.67 m
As the focal length is + v e, hence it is a converging or convex lens.

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.27
rows.question: A convex lens of focal length 40 cm is in compact with a concave lens of focal length 25 cm . Find the power of the combination of lenses.
rows.answer:
Focal length of combination, 1/F= 1/f1 + 1/f2= 1/40 + (1)/(− 25)
(Since focal length is negative for concave lens)
⇒ F= (− 200)/(3) c m
And, Power, P= (1)/(f (in metre)) = 100/F= (100)/((− 200)/(3)) = − 1.50

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.28
rows.question: A convex lens of focal length 20 cm is placed in contact with concave lens of focal length 60 cm . Calculate : (a) power of each of the lens (b) power of combination (c) focal length of combination (d) nature of combination.
rows.answer:
As we know, power of a lens P= (1)/(f (in metre))
(a) Power of convex lens = 100/20= + 5 D, Power of concave lens = (100)/(− 60) = − 1.67 D
(b) Power of combination P= P1 + P2= (+ 5 D) + (− 1.67 D) = + 3.33 D
(c) Focal length of combination
f (in c m) = 100/P= 100/3.33= + 30.0 c m
(d) As power of combination is positive, therefore, combination behaves like a convex lens.

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.29
rows.question: A convex lens of focal length 10.0 cm is placed in contact with a convex lens of 15.0 cm focal length. What is the focal length of the combination.
rows.answer:
For combination of lenses
1/f= 1/f1 + 1/f2= 1/10 + 1/15= 25/150= 1/6
Therefore, f= 6 c m

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 1.30
rows.question: Ten identical converging thin lenses, each of focal length 1 0 c m, are in contact. What is the power of the combined lens.
rows.answer:
For thin lenses in contact
& P= P1 + P2 + … … … … … & = 10 P1= (10 × 100)/(10) = 100 D

rows.title: Check Your Knowledge
rows.question:
Check Your Knowledge
A person in a dark room looking through a window can clearly see a person outside in the daylight, whereas the person outside cannot see the person inside. Explain.
rows.answer: When a person inside a darkroom is looking through a window to another person outside in broad daylight, then enough reflected rays come inside to the eye of the observer from the person outside. So he can be seen clearly. But if the person in daylight, looks through the same window to the person inside the dark room, there is hardly any reflected rays coming from the body of the person inside the dark room to the eyes of the observer outside. So the outside observer can't see the person inside the room.

rows.answer:

Number of images formed when two plane mirrors are inclined at an angle 90° is
(a) 3

(c) 4

so numbe of images is (n − 1) ⇒ (4 − 1) = 3

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Very Short Answer Questions — Q1
rows.question: What is the relation between focal length and radius of curvature?

rows.question: What is reflection of light?
rows.answer: Sending back of light in the same medium by a surface is called reflection of light.

rows.answer: 16 c m / s e c. If an object moves towards the plane mirror with speed v , then image appears to move with speed 2 v.

rows.question: What do you understand by optically denser and optically rarer medium?
rows.answer: Medium in which speed of light is less is known as optically denser medium and the medium in which speed of light is more is optically rarer medium.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Very Short Answer Questions — Q7

rows.answer: Convex lens is known as converging lens and concave lens is known as diverging lens.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q1
rows.question: Prove that the refractive index of glass with respect to air is the reciprocal of the refractive index of air with respect to glass.
rows.answer:
Radius of curvature of convex mirror, R= 30 c m.
∴ Focal length of convex mirror, f= R/2= (30 c m)/(2) = 15 c m.
Now h= 5 c m, u= − 20 c m, v=?, h′ = ?
Using the mirror formula 1/f= 1/v + 1/u, we have
& 1/v= 1/f − 1/u= 1/15 − (1)/(− 20) = 1/15 + 1/20= (4 + 3)/(60) & v= 60/7= 8.6 c m
Thus, image is formed at a distance of 8.6 cm behind the convex mirror. The image is virtual and erect.
& m= (h′)/(h) = − v/u & (h′)/(5) = − (8.6)/(− 20) ⇒ h′ = 8.6/20 × 5= 2.15 c m.
So the image size is diminished.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q2
rows.question: What is dispersion of light and what is its cause?
rows.answer:
For convex mirror, we have given
u= − 10 m, R= 2.0 m
So, f= R/2= (2.0 m)/(2) = 1.0 m
Using the mirror formula, 1/u + 1/v= 1/f
We get 1/v= 1/f − 1/u= 1/1.0 + 1/10= 11/10
or v= 10/11= 0.9 m
Thus, the car would appear at 0.9 m from the convex mirror. We know that m= − v/u= (− 10/11)/(− 10) = 1/11
Thus, size of the image of the car will be 1/11 times smaller the actual size of the car through the convex mirror.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q3
rows.question: Find the focal length of a lens of power -2.0 D . What type of lens is this?
rows.answer:
According to cartesian sign convention,
u= − 90 c m, f= − 30 c m.
We have the formula 1/v + 1/u= 1/f
∴ 1/v − 1/90= − 1/30
or 1/v= − 1/30 + 1/90= (− 3 + 1)/(90) = (− 2)/(90)
∴ v= − 45 c m.
Magnification,
& m= (− v)/(u) = (− (− 45))/(− 90) & m= − 1/2
A real, smaller than object and inverted image is formed 45 cm from the mirror on the same side as the object.

rows.question: Under what condition will a concave mirror produce an erect image? A virtual image ? An image smaller than the object? An image larger than the object?

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q6
rows.question: Refractive index of glass for light of yellow, green and red colours are μy, μg and μr respectively. Rearrange these symbols in the increasing order of values.
rows.answer:
Mirror formula is, 1/f= 1/v + 1/u where u, v and f are object distance, image distance and focal length.
1/v= 1/f − 1/u
Focal length and object distance are both − v e for concave mirror.
It is given u< f,
∴ 1/v becomes (− 1)/(f) + 1/u and is positive.
v becoming + ve denotes a virtual image.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q7
rows.question: An object is placed at a distance of 12 cm in front of a concave mirror. It forms a real image four times larger than the object. Calculate the distance of the image from the mirror.
rows.answer:
An extended object A B is placed in front of a plane mirror M M′. A light ray from A falls perpendicular on a mirror at P. So it comes back along the same path after reflection. Another ray A Q (Q is a point of normal from B), falls on the mirror and after reflection it goes along the path Q R. When these two reflected rays i.e. P A & Q R are produced backward, they meet at a point A′. Similarly, the point B′ is also obtained. It means A′ B′ is the image of AB . This image is virtual and erect which forms as far behind the mirror as the object is in front of it. i.e. A P= A′ P.
B Q= B′ Q
To prove : A B= A′ B′.
△ A′ B′ Q= ∠ A′ Q B′, = ∠ r
(Vertically opposite angles)
∠ i= ∠ r (laws of reflection)
∴ In △ A B Q and △ A′ Q B′, ∠ i= ∠ A′ Q B′
∠ A B Q= ∠ A′ B′ Q= 90°
B Q= B′ Q (because distance of image behind the mirror = distance of object in front of the mirror)
∴ △ A B Q ≅ △ A′ B′ Q. (ASA).
∴ A B= A′ B′ (C P C T) i.e. size of object = size of image.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q8
rows.question: With respect to air, the refractive index of ice is 1.31 and that of rock salt is 1.54 . Calculate the refractive index of rock salt with respect to ice.
rows.answer:
(a) Laws of refraction of light:
(i) The incident ray, the refracted ray at the point of incidence and the normal all lies in the same plane for the two given medium.
(ii) The ratio of sine of angle of incidence (i.e., sin i ) to the sine of angle of refraction (i.e., sin r ) is always constant for the light of given colour and for the given pair of media.
Mathematically, (sin i)/(sin r) = constant = 1 n2
The constant ' n ' is called refractive index of the second medium with respect to the first medium.
Absolute refractive index of the medium is given by
n= (Speed of light in vacuum (c))/(Speed of light in media (v))
(b) Given nA= 2.0 and nB= 1.5
Speed of light in medium B = 2 × 10⁸ m / s
nB= (Speed of light in vacuum (c))/(Speed of light in media B)
1.5= (c)/(2 × 10⁸)
Speed of light in vacuum c= 2 × 10⁸ × 1.5
= 3.0 × 10⁸ m / s
Speed of light in medium ' A '
nA= (Speed of light in vacuum)/(Speed of light in media ' A ')
2.0= (3 × 10⁸)/(Speed of light in media' A′)
Speed of light in medium A= (3 × 10⁸)/(2)
= 1.5 × 10⁸ m / s

hi= v/u h 0= (− 10 c m)/(− 20 c m) × 4= 2 c m
size of image is 2 cm .
