A person with a myopic eye cannot see objects beyond 1.2 m distinctly. What should be the type of the corrective lens used to restore proper vision? 2. A person with a defective eye-division is unable to see the objects nearer than 1.5 m . He wants to read books at a distance of 30 m . Find the nature, focal length and power of the lens he needs in his spectacles. 3. Study the figure given below and answer the questions that follow. (a) Which defect of vision has been shown in the figure? (b) What do the points O and I represent ? (c) Which lens is used to correct this defect? (d) Redraw the figure with the corrective lens. 4. In the diagram given below, a defective eye has been corrected. Study the diagram and answer the questions that follow. (a) Name the defect which has been corrected. (b) Name the point N . (c) Why a convex lens, instead of a concave lens, has been used ? (d) Redraw the figure of the defective eye without the corrective lens. ## Case Based MCQs 1. Read the following and attempt any four questions from 1 (i) to 1 (v). The phenomenon of decomposition of the white light into its seven component colours when passing through a prism or through a transparent object delimited by non parallel surfaces is called dispersion of light. A beam of light containing all the visible spectrum of the light is white, because the sum of all the colors generates the white color. The light is decomposed in all the component colours, Violet, Indigo, Blue, Green, Yellow, Orange and Red, called as VIBGYOR. The band of the coloured components of a light beam is called its spectrum. The phenomenon can be explained by thinking that light of different colours (different wavelengths) has different velocities while travelling in a medium vm= f λm. Hence, the change in velocity of light observed when the light passes from the air to the glass, depends on the wavelength. (i) A prism A B C (with B C as base) is placed in different orientations. A narrow beam of white light is incident on the prism as shown in figure. In which of (a) (i) (a) (b) (ii) (i) (ii) the following cases, after dispersion, the third colour from the top corresponds to the colour of the sky? (iii) (iv) (c) (iii) (d) (iv) (ii) Which of the following statements is correct regarding the propagation of light of different colours of white light in air? (a) Red light moves fastest (b) Blue light moves faster than green light (c) All the colours of the white light move with the same speed (d) Yellow light moves with the mean speed as that of the red and the violet light (iii) When white light is allowed to pass through a glass prism, which colour deviates the least? (a) Violet (b) Red (c) Green (d) Orange (iv) When white light is allowed to pass through a glass prism, which colour deviates the most? (a) Indigo (b) Green (c) Red (d) Violet (v) For a prism material, refractive index is highest for (a) Red (b) Yellow (c) Orange (d) Violet

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Check Your Knowledge
rows.question:
Check Your Knowledge
- The angle subtended at the eye by an object is equal to the angle subtended at the eye by the virtual image produced by a magnifying glass. In what sense then does a magnifying glass provide angular magnification?
rows.answer: In magnifying glass the object is placed closer than 25 cm , which produce image at 25 cm . This closer object has larger angular size than the same object at 25 cm . In this way although the angle subtended by virtual image and object is same at eye but angular magnification is achieved.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Exercise 2 I Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar) — Text-Book Questions — Q1
rows.question: What is meant by power of accommodation of the eye?
rows.answer: The ability of eye lens to change its focal length with the help of ciliary muscles is called accomodation power.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Exercise 2 I Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar) — Text-Book Questions — Q2
rows.question: A person with a myopic eye cannot see objects beyond 1.2 m distinctly. What should be the type of the corrective lens used to restore proper vision?
rows.answer:
The person is suffering from myopia. This defect is corrected by using a concave lens of suitable focal length i.e., power.
Power & = (− 1)/(far point (metre)) = (− 1)/(1.2) = (− 10)/(12) & = (− 5)/(6) = − 0.83 dioptre

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Exercise 2 I Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar) — Text-Book Questions — Q3
rows.question: What is the far point and near point of the human eye with normal vision?
rows.answer: A healthy human eye can see the objects at infinity distinctly. So far point is infinity. The eye can also see the nearly objects beyond 25 cm .

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Exercise 2 I Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar) — Text-Book Questions — Q4
rows.question: A student has difficulty in reading the blackboard while sitting in the last row. What could be the defect the child is suffering from? How can it be corrected?
rows.answer: The student is suffering from myopia. It can be corrected by using concave lens of suitable focal length.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Exercise 2 I Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar) — Text-Book Exercise — Q1
rows.question:
The human eye can focus objects at different distances by adjusting the focal length of the eye lens. This is due to
(a) presbyopia
(b) accommodation
(c) near-sightedness
(d) far-sightedness.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Exercise 2 I Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar) — Text-Book Exercise — Q2
rows.question:
The human eye forms the image of an object at its
(a) cornea
(b) iris
(c) pupil
(d) retina.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Exercise 2 I Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar) — Text-Book Exercise — Q3
rows.question:
The least distance of distinct vision for a young adult with normal vision is about
(a) 25 m
(b) 2.5 cm
(c) 25 cm
(d) 2.5 m
rows.answer: (c) 25 cm

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Exercise 2 I Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar) — Text-Book Exercise — Q4
rows.question:
The change in focal length of an eye lens is caused by the action of the
(a) pupil
(b) retina
(c) ciliary muscles
(d) iris

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Exercise 2 I Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar) — Text-Book Exercise — Q5
rows.question: A person needs a lens of power -5.5 dioptres for correcting his distant vision. For correcting his near vision he needs a lens of power +1.5 dioptre. What is the focal length of the lens required for correcting (i) distant vision, and (ii) near vision ?

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Exercise 2 I Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar) — Text-Book Exercise — Q6
rows.question: The far point of a myopic person is 80 cm in front of the eye. What is the nature and power of the lens required to correct the problem?
rows.answer:
The lens used for correcting the disease is concave lens
P & = − (1)/(far point (metre)) = − (1)/(80 c m) = (− 100)/(80) & = − 1.25 D
(a) Near point of a Hypermetropic eye
(b) Hypermetropic eye
(c) Correction for Hypermetropic eye

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Exercise 2 I Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar) — Text-Book Exercise — Q7
rows.question: Make a diagram to show how hypermetropia is corrected. The near point of a hypermetropic eye is 1 m . What is the power of lens required to correct this defect? Assume that the near point of the normal eye is 25 cm .
rows.answer:
It is corrected by using convex lens of suitable focal length.
v= − 1 m= − 100 c m
u= − 25 c m
According to lens formula,
1/v − 1/u= 1/f
1/f= − 1/100 − (1)/(− 25) = − 1/100 + 1/25
1/f= (− 1 + 4)/(100) = 3/100
f= 100/3 c m
P= 1/f= (3 × 100)/(100) = 3 D

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Exercise 2 I Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar) — Text-Book Exercise — Q8
rows.question: Why is a normal eye not able to see clearly the objects placed closer than 25 cm ?
rows.answer: The rays coming from the objects which are at a distance less than 25 cm are not properly focussed at retina because the ciliary muscles cannot be contracted beyond this limit, so as to decrease the focal length of the eye lens.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Exercise 2 I Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar) — Text-Book Exercise — Q9
rows.question: What happens to the image distance in the eye when we increase the distance of an object from the eye?
rows.answer: The image distance remains unchanged. It is because, as distance of object increases the focal length of eye lens is adjusted by ciliary muscles such that image is always formed at retina.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Exercise 2 I Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar) — Text-Book Exercise — Q10
rows.question: Explain why the planets do not twinkle.
rows.answer: Planets are much closer to earth as compared to stars. They are extended source of light. They behave like a number of point sources so that the average variation is zero. Thus, planets do not twinkle.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Exercise 2 I Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar) — Text-Book Exercise — Q11
rows.question: Why does the Sun appear reddish early in the morning?
rows.answer: In the morning, the sun is nearer to the horizon. Blue light have shorter wavelength, so , it scatters more. Therefore the red light which have longer wavelength reaches upto the eye of observer and the sun appears reddish.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Exercise 2 I Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar) — Text-Book Exercise — Q12
rows.question: Why does the sky appear dark instead of blue to an astronaut?
rows.answer: There is nearly no atmosphere for the astronauts as they are flying high in sky. So there is no scattering of light. This is why the sky appear dark to the astronauts.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Exercise 2 I Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar) — Exemplar Questions — Q1
rows.question: A student sitting at the back of the classroom cannot read clearly the letters written on the blackboard. What advice will a doctor give to her? Draw ray diagram for the correction on this defect.
rows.answer: The student is suffering from myopia (near sightedness). Doctor advises her to use a concave lens of appropriate power to correct this defect.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Exercise 2 I Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar) — Exemplar Questions — Q2
rows.question: How will you use two identical prisms so that a narrow beam of white light incident on one prism emerges out of the second prism as white light? Draw the diagram.
rows.answer: By using two identical prisms, one placed inverted with respect to the other.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Exercise 2 I Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar) — Exemplar Questions — Q3
rows.question: Is the position of a star as seen by us its true position? Justify your answer.
rows.answer: No. light from stars undergoes atmospheric refraction which occurs in medium of gradually changing refractive index.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Exercise 2 I Master NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar) — Exemplar Questions — Q4
rows.question: How does refraction take place in the atmosphere? Why do stars twinkle but not the planets?
rows.answer: In atmosphere, we have air layers of different optical densities when light rays pass through the atmosphere having air layers of different optical dinsities, then refraction of light takes place. Planets do not twinkle as they are closer to earth and are seen as extended sources.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: MISCELLANEOUS SOLVED EXAMPLES — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) — Q1
rows.question:
The human eye possesses the power of accommodation. This is the power to :
(a) alter the diameter of the pupil as the intensity of light changes
(b) distinguish between lights of different colours
(c) focus objects at different distances
(d) decide which of the two objects is closer.
rows.answer: (c) The ability of the eye lens to change its shape to focus near and distant objects clearly is called power of accomodation.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: MISCELLANEOUS SOLVED EXAMPLES — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) — Q2
rows.question:
How does the eye change in order to focus on near or distant objects?
(a) The lens moves in or out
(b) The retina moves in or out
(c) The lens becomes thicker or thinner
(d) The pupil gets larger or smaller
rows.answer: (c) Curvature of eye lens is adjusted with the help of ciliary muscles.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: MISCELLANEOUS SOLVED EXAMPLES — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) — Q3
rows.question:
Which of the following changes occur when you walk out of bright sunshine into a poorly lit room?
(a) The pupil becomes larger
(b) The lens becomes thicker
(c) The ciliary muscle relaxes
(d) The pupil becomes smaller
rows.answer: (a) In poorly lit room or dim light the iris expands the pupil to allow more light to enter the eye.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: MISCELLANEOUS SOLVED EXAMPLES — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) — Q4
rows.question:
A person got his eyes tested. The optician's prescription for the spectacles reads:
Left eye : -3.00 D
Right eye : -3.50 D
The person is having a defect of vision called :
(a) presbyopia
(b) myopia
(c) astigmatism
(d) hypermetropia

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: MISCELLANEOUS SOLVED EXAMPLES — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) — Q5
rows.question:
A student sitting on the last bench in the class cannot read the writing on the blackboard clearly but he can read the book lying on his desk clearly. Which of the following statement is correct about the student?
(a) The near point of his eyes has receded away.
(b) The near point of his eyes has come closer to him.
(c) The far point of his eyes has receded away.
(d) The far point of his eyes has come closer to him.
rows.answer: (d) Near the horizon at sunrise and sunset, most of the blue light and shorter wavelengths are scattered away an hence sun appears red.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: MISCELLANEOUS SOLVED EXAMPLES — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) — Q6
rows.question:
A man driving a car can read a distant road sign clearly but finds difficulty in reading the odometer on the dashboard of the car. Which of the following statement is correct about this man?
(a) The near point of his eyes has receded away.
(b) The near point of his eyes has come closer to him.
(c) The far point of his eyes has receded away.
(d) The far point of his eyes has come closer to him.
rows.answer: (a) Scattering of light is not enough at such heights.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: MISCELLANEOUS SOLVED EXAMPLES — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) — Q7
rows.question:
Which of the following is not caused by the atmospheric refraction of light?
(a) Twinkling of stars at night
(b) Sun appearing higher in the sky than it actually is
(c) Sun becoming visible two minutes before actual sunrise
(d) Sun appearing red at sunset

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: MISCELLANEOUS SOLVED EXAMPLES — Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) — Q8
rows.question:
The sky appears dark to passengers flying at very high altitudes mainly because :
(a) Scatterings of light is not enough at such heights.
(b) There is no atmosphere at great heights.
(c) The size of molecules is smaller than the wavelength of visible light.
(d) The light gets scattered towards the earth.
rows.answer:
The defect of the vision is hypermetropia. The formula used is 1/f= 1/v − 1/u
Given, u= − 25 c m and v= − 75 c m.
∴ 1/f= − 1/75 + 1/25= + 2/75 or f= + 75/2= + 37.5 c m
Power of the lens
= (1)/(f (in metres)) = (100)/(75 / 2) = + 8/3= + 2.67 D

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — Fill in the Blanks — Q1
rows.question: The coloured diaphragm between the cornea and the lens is _ _ _ _
rows.answer: iris

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — Fill in the Blanks — Q2
rows.question: The middle point of the iris has a hole, which is called .............
rows.answer: pupil

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — Fill in the Blanks — Q3
rows.question: The screen on which the image is formed by the lens system of the human eye is called _ _ _ _
rows.answer: retina

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — Fill in the Blanks — Q4
rows.question: For young adult with normal vision, least distance of distinct vision = _ _ _ _
rows.answer: 25 cm .

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — Fill in the Blanks — Q5
rows.question: The closest distance at which the eye can focus clearly is called the _ _ _ _
rows.answer: near point

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — Fill in the Blanks — Q6
rows.question: For a normal eye, the range of vision is from _ _ _ _
rows.answer: 25 cm to infinity

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — Fill in the Blanks — Q7
rows.question: A person is short-sighted if his eyeball is too _ _ _ _
rows.answer: long

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — Fill in the Blanks — Q8
rows.question: The eye which cannot simultaneously see with the same distinctness all objects or lines making different inclinations is said to suffer from _ _ _ _
rows.answer: astigmatism.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — Fill in the Blanks — Q9
rows.question: The defect of the eye due to which a person is unable to distinguish between certain colours, known as _ _ _ _
rows.answer: colour blindness

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — Fill in the Blanks — Q10
rows.question: The ability of the eye to focus both near and distant objects, by adjusting its focal length, is called the _ _ _ _
rows.answer: accommodation of the eye.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — Fill in the Blanks — Q11
rows.question: The smallest distance, at which the eye can see objects clearly without strain, is called the _ _ _ _ of the eye.
rows.answer: near point

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — Fill in the Blanks — Q12
rows.question: The splitting of white light into its component colours is called _ _ _ _
rows.answer: dispersion.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — Fill in the Blanks — Q13
rows.question: _ _ _ _ causes the blue colour of sky and the reddening of the Sun at sunrise and sunset.
rows.answer: Scattering of light

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — Fill in the Blanks — Q14
rows.question: Sunlight comprises _ _ _ _ colours.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — True / False — Q1
rows.question: Lens which is used for correcting the presbyopia defect of the eye is concave.
rows.answer: False

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — True / False — Q2
rows.question: The colour that deviates maximum while passing through a glass prism is violet.
rows.answer: True

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — True / False — Q3
rows.question: Water droplets act as tiny prism in the formation of rainbow.
rows.answer: True

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — True / False — Q4
rows.question: The transparent spherical membrane covering the front of the eye is known as cornea.
rows.answer: True

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — True / False — Q5
rows.question: The eye which can see near object clearly is said to suffer from hypermetropia.
rows.answer: False

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — True / False — Q6
rows.question: The eye which cannot see distant objects clearly is said to suffer from myopia.
rows.answer: True

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — True / False — Q7
rows.question: Colour blindness is a genetic disorder which occurs by inheritance.
rows.answer: True

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — True / False — Q8
rows.question: In Myopia the image of distant objects is focussed before the retina.
rows.answer: True

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — True / False — Q9
rows.question: Hypermetropia is corrected by using a convex lens of suitable power.
rows.answer: True

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — True / False — Q10
rows.question: A person suffering from myopia cannot see distant objects clearly.
rows.answer: True

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — True / False — Q11
rows.question: The focal length of a given lens depends on the surrounding medium.
rows.answer: True

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — True / False — Q12
rows.question: A dentist uses a convex mirror to view the inner parts of a patient's mouth.
rows.answer: False

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — True / False — Q13
rows.question: The solar spectrum in general is an absorption spectrum.
rows.answer: True

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — True / False — Q14
rows.question: The sun looks red at sunset because most of the blue light in sunrays is scattered leaving behind red and yellow lights.
rows.answer: True

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — True / False — Q15
rows.question: Clouds look white because water droplets of clouds scatter all colours of light equally.
rows.answer: True

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — True / False — Q16
rows.question: The sun is visible two minutes before the actual sunrise due to atmospheric refraction.
rows.answer: True

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Assertion-Reason — Match the Following — Q1
rows.question: Column II gives lens that can be use to correct the defect of vision given in column I, match them correctly.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Very Short Answer Questions — Q1
rows.question: When a monochromatic light passes through a prism, will it show dispersion?
rows.answer: No, it will not show any dispersion but will show only deviation.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Very Short Answer Questions — Q2
rows.question: What do you mean by a pure spectrum?
rows.answer: A pure spectrum is the one in which the colours are separated from one another using a narrow source of light.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Very Short Answer Questions — Q3
rows.question: What is the common name for short sightedness?
rows.answer: Myopia.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Very Short Answer Questions — Q4
rows.question: Name the defect which is corrected by using an astigmatic lens.
rows.answer: Spherical aberration.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Very Short Answer Questions — Q5
rows.question: Will a star appear to twinkle if seen from free space (say moon) ?
rows.answer: No, because there is no atmosphere in free space for refraction to take place.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Very Short Answer Questions — Q6
rows.question: What prevents rainbows from being seen as complete circles?
rows.answer: The earth comes in the way of the rainbow and prevents it to form a complete circle.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Very Short Answer Questions — Q7
rows.question: What is responsible for the rainbow-coloured fringe commonly seen at the edges of a spot of white light from the beam of lantern or slide projector?
rows.answer: The rainbow - colored fringe formed at the edges of a spot of white light coming from a beam of lantern or slide projector is due to diffraction of light rays coming from different part of the beam.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Very Short Answer Questions — Q8
rows.question: What is the essential condition for observing a rainbow ?
rows.answer: Back of the observer must be towards the sun.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Very Short Answer Questions — Q9
rows.question: Which part of the eye gives the characteristic colour of the eye ?
rows.answer: Iris gives the characteristic colour of the eye.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Very Short Answer Questions — Q10
rows.question: What is the distance at which a watch repairer holds the watch when he is wearing a magnifying glass, if his distance of most distinct vision is 30 cm ? Assume that he is not aware of the action of a convex lens.
rows.answer: The distance of the watch from the eye must be such that its virtual image is formed at 30 cm from his eye.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q1
rows.question: Can a beam of white light when passed through a hollow prism give spectrum? Explain.
rows.answer: No, the dispersion of light will not occur. Dispersion of light is due to change in medium when light passes from air to glass. As medium remains same hence no dispersion.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q2
rows.question: What is colour blindness?
rows.answer: Colour blindness is a defect of the eye due to which a person is unable to distinguish between certain colours, sometime even the primary colours.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q3
rows.question: How do we see colours?
rows.answer: The retina of human eye has a large number of light sensitive cells. These cells are of two types i.e., rod cells and cone cells. The rod shaped cells show response towards the intensity of light rays, while the cone shaped cells respond to colour. It is these cone cells, which make it possible for a man to see different colours and distinguish between them.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q4
rows.question: A person having a myopic eye uses a concave lens of focal length 50 cm . What is the power of the lens?
rows.answer:
Focal length, f= − 50 c m (concave lens)
Now, Power,
P= (1)/(f (in metre)) = (1)/(− 50/100 m) = − 100/50= − 2
Thus, the power of this concave lens is -2 dioptres

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q5
rows.question: Why do different coloured rays deviate differently in the prism?
rows.answer: Different coloured rays deviate differently in the prism because the angle of refraction of different colours is different while passing through the glass prism.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q6
rows.question: Why do stars twinkle on a clear night?
rows.answer: The continuously changing atmosphere causes refraction which is able to cause variation in the light coming from a point-sized star, thus the stars appear to be twinkling.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q7
rows.question: A person having the nearest distance of distinct vision of 32 cm uses a reading lens of 8 cm focal length. What is the magnification of his reading lens?

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q8
rows.question: A man who wears glasses of power 3 dioptre must hold a newspaper at least 25 cm away to see the print clearly. How far away would the newspaper have to be if he took off the glasses and still wanted clear vision?
rows.answer:
As here u= − 0.25 m and f= 1 / P= (1 / 3) m, from lens formula P= 1/f= 1/v − 1/u, we have 3= 1/v − (1)/(− 0.25)
& or 1/v= 3 − 4= − 1 m & i.e. v= − 1 m
i.e., the lens shifts the object from 25 cm to 1 m for clear vision, i.e., his near point is 1 m . So in absence of glasses, he must hold the newspaper at a distance of 1 m away from his eyes for clear vision.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q9
rows.question: How can we determine the focal length and power of the concave lens required to correct a myopic eye?
rows.answer:
Let x be the distance of the actual far point from the eye and hence from the concave lens placed close to the eye. The rays coming from infinity, after refraction through the concave lens, appear to come from the far point F .
∴ u= − ∞, v= − x, f= ?
By lens formula,
1/f= 1/v − 1/u= (1)/(− x) − (1)/(− ∞) = − 1/x + 0= − 1/x
∴ Required focal length, f= − x
Required power, P= 1/f= − 1/x
The negative sign shows that the correcting lens is a concave lens.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q10
rows.question: How can we determine the focal length and power of the convex lens required to correct a hypermetropic eye?
rows.answer:
(b)
(c)
Let y= distance of the near point N′ from the defective eye. Now the near point N of the normal eye is at distance D= 25 c m. The object placed at N forms its virtual image at N′ Due to the convex lens.
∴ u= − D, v= − y, f= ?
By lens formula
1/f= 1/v − 1/u= (1)/(− y) − (1)/(− D) = (y − D)/(y D)
Required focal length, f= (y D)/(y − D)
∴ Required power, P= 1/f= (y − D)/(y D)
As y> D, so both f and D are positive. That is the correcting lens must be a convex lens.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q11
rows.question: A person having short sight (near sight) unable to see clearly beyond 2 m develops presbyopia by which he is unable to read a book held closer than 1 m . Prescribe lenses of suitable power for distant vision and near vision.
rows.answer:
For distant vision, defect is short sight
Focal length f= distance of defective far point = − 2 m
∴ P= − (1)/(2 m) = − 0.5 dioptre (concave)
For near vision, u= normal distance of distinct vision = 0.25 m; v= defective near point = − 1 m (virtual image)
P= 1/f= 1/v − 1/u= (1)/(− 1) − (1)/(− 0.25)
= − 1 + 4= 3 dioptre (convex)

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q12
rows.question: State one main function each of iris, pupil and cornea.
rows.answer:
(i) Iris : Gives colour to eyes, controls size of pupil.
(ii) Pupil : regulate amount of light or through which light enters the eyes.
(iii) Cornea : It acts like a lens refracts most of the light into eyes.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q13
rows.question:
Study the diagram given below and answer the following questions:
(i) Name the defect of vision depicted in the diagram.
(ii) List two causes of the defect.
(iii) Draw a ray diagram for the correction of the above defect using an appropriate lens.
rows.answer:
(i) Hypermetropia, eyeglass with convex lens is used to rectify this problem.
(ii) A focal length of the eye lens is too long. The eyeball becomes too small.
(iii) Diagram showing correction of this defect using convex lens.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q14
rows.question: Due to gradual weakening of ciliary muscles and diminishing flexibility of the eye lens a certain defect of vision arises. Write the name of this defect. Name the type of lens required by such persons to improve the vision. Explain the structure and function of such a lens.
rows.answer:
The defect caused due to gradual weakening of ciliary muscles which control lens focusing and diminishing flexibility of the crystalline eye lens is presbyopia.
The type of lens required by presbyopia to improve the vision is convex lens.
When convex lens of suitable power is placed in front of the hypermetropic eye, then the diverging rays of light coming from the nearby object are first converged by the convex lens. Due to this, the convex lens forms a virtual image of the nearby object at the near point of the hypermetropic eye.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q15
rows.question: What is a rainbow? Draw a labelled diagram to show the formation of a rainbow.
rows.answer:
A rainbow is a band of seven colours extending from violet to red. It is formed by the combined effect of dispersion, reflection and refraction of the sunrays through raindrops. A rainbow is always formed in a direction opposite to that of the Sun, the water droplets
act as a small prisms,they refract and disperse the sunlight, then reflect it internally and finally refract it again when it comes out of the raindrop. Due to dispersion of light and internal reflection, different colors reach the observer's eye.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Long Answer Questions — Q1
rows.question: Why does it take some time to see objects in a dim room when you enter the room from bright sunlight outside?
rows.answer: In the bright sunlight, iris causes the pupil to become smaller so that only a small portion of light enters the eye and rods of the retina are also adjusted in the same way. But when a person enters into a dimly-lit room, each iris takes some time to increase the diameter of the pupil, so that more amount of light can enter the eyes to see the object clearly and rod cells of the retina also take some time to adjust themselves to get the picture of the object in the dim light.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Long Answer Questions — Q2
rows.question: A prism causes dispersion of white light while a rectangular glass block does not. Explain.
rows.answer: In a prism the refraction of light takes place at the two slant surfaces. The dispersion of white light occurs at the first surface of prism where its constituent colours are deviated through different angles. At the second surface, these split colours suffer only refraction and they get further separated. But in a rectangular glass block, the refraction of light takes place at the two parallel surfaces. At the first surface, although the white light splits into its constituent colours on refraction, but these split colours on suffering refraction at the second surface emerge out in form of a parallel beam, which give an impression of white light.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Long Answer Questions — Q3
rows.question: When yellow paint and blue paint are mixed we get green colour. When yellow light and blue light are mixed, white light is obtained. Give reasons.
rows.answer:
Yellow light is 'made up' of two of the primary colours, namely red and green. Hence, when this light is mixed with the light of the third primary colour, namely blue light, we get white light.
Yellow light + Blue light = ( Red light + Green light ) + Blue light = White light
The effect of mixing of two paints of different colours is not the same as that of mixing of two pure spectral lights. This is because each paint appears to have a colour that depends on the lights that it can reflect. Yellow paint is known to reflect red, green and yellow lights. Similarly blue paint reflects blue and green lights. Hence, when these two paints are mixed, the mixture can reflect only green light and thus appears green in colour.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Long Answer Questions — Q4
rows.question: Explain working of human eye.
rows.answer:
Working of human eye : When you look at an object, light rays from the object enter into the eye through cornea which also acts as lens to refract maximum light.
This light then passes through pupil which allows the required amount of light to pass through it. When this light falls on eye lens image is formed on retina. This is a real and inverted image. This image is sent to the brain through optical nerves in the form of electric signal and brain interpret the image into erect image.
Most relaxed state of ciliary muscles
(A) Eye-lens has its largest focal lenght
Ciliary muscles contract
Nearby object
(B) Eye-lens has a shorter focal length

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Long Answer Questions — Q5
rows.question: Name the eye defect in which eye lens becomes cloudy or milky. Mention the method for its correction. a person uses a lens of power -1.0 D for correcting his distant vision and for correcting his near vision he uses a lens of power +2.0 D . Calculate the focal length of the lenses required to correct these defects. Explain why a normal eye is not able to see clearly the objects placed closer than the near points.
rows.answer:
(i) Sometime, the crystalline lens of people at old age becomes milky and cloudy. With people age, cataracts, grow progressively darker and more dense, preventing light from easily passing through the lens. This condition is called cataract. This causes partial or complete loss of vision.
It is possible to restore vision through a cataract surgery.
(ii) Focal length for lens for distant vision
= − 1/1= − 1 m
Focal length for lens for near vision
= + 1/2= + 0.5 m
(iii) The focal length of eye lens cannot be decreased below a certain minimum limit. If we try to read a printed page by holding it very close to our eyes, we may see the image being blurred or feel strain in the eye. To see an object comfortably and distinctly, we must hold it at about 25 cm from the eyes.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Long Answer Questions — Q6
rows.question: State the cause of dispersion of white light by a glass prism. How did Newton, using two identical glass prisms, show that white light is made of seven colours? Draw a ray diagram to show the path of a narrow beam of white light, thought a combination of two identical prisms arranged together in inverted position with respect to each other, when it is allowed to fall obliquely on one of the face of the first prism of the combination.
rows.answer:
Light is formed of different colours which travel at their own speed inside a prism. Due to which the light bend the through different angles with respect to the incident ray, as they pass through a prism. The red light bends the least while the violet most causing dispersion this can be explained by the fact that light of different colours having different wavelengths has different velocities while travelling in a medium vm= n λm.
Newton showed that the reverse of dispersion of light is also possible. If we kept two prisms close to each other one in erect position and the other in an inverted position. The light get dispersed when passes through the first prism. The second prism receives all the seven coloured rays from first prism and combines into original while
light i.e. , recombine the different colour of spectrum \& hence gives white light. This proves that white light is made of seven colours.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Long Answer Questions — Q7
rows.question: What is meant by scattering of light? The sky appears blue and the sun appears reddish at sunrise and sunset. Explain these phenomena with reason.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Long Answer Questions — Q8
rows.question:
(i) Draw aray diagram to explain the term angle of deviation.
(ii) Why do the component colours of incident white light split into a spectrum while passing through a glass prism, explain.
(iii) Draw a labelled ray diagram to show the formation of a rainbow.
rows.answer:
The defect of the vision is hypermetropia. The formula used is 1/f= 1/v − 1/u
Given, u= − 25 c m and v= − 75 c m.
∴ 1/f= − 1/75 + 1/25= + 2/75 or f= + 75/2= + 37.5 c m
Power of the lens
= (1)/(f (in metres)) = (100)/(75 / 2) = + 8/3= + 2.67 D

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Long Answer Questions — Q9
rows.question:
(a) A student is unable to see clearly the words written on the blackboard placed at a distance of approximately 3 m from him. Name the defect of vision the boy is suffering from. State the possible causes of this defect and explain the method of correcting it.
(b) Why do stars twinkle? Explain.
rows.answer:
Here, v= − 3 m, u= − 12 m, f= ?
1/v − 1/u= 1/f or − 1/3 + 1/12= 1/f or f= − 4 m
A concave lens of focal length 4 m should be used.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Long Answer Questions — Q10
rows.question:
(a) With the help of labelled ray diagram show the path followed by a narrow beam of monochromatic light when it passes through a glass prism.
(b) What would happen if this beam is replaced by a narrow beam of white light?
rows.answer:
The image of the letters 25 cm away, now form at 50 cm away. Thus
u= − 25 c m
v= − 50 c m
Using lens formula : 1/v − 1/u= 1/f,
So, (1)/((− 50)) − (1)/((− 25)) = 1/f
⇒ f= 50 c m= 0.5 m
So, Additional power of the lens
p= 1/f= 1/0.5= 2 D
Now, total power of the lens required
p= (2.5 + 2) D
= 4.5 D

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Long Answer Questions — Q11
rows.question:
(a) A person is suffering from both myopia and hypermetropia.
(i) What kind of lenses can correct this defect?
(ii) How are these lenses prepared?
(b) A person needs a lens of power + 3D for correcting his near vision and -3D for correcting his distant vision. Calculate the focal lengths of the lenses required to correct these defects.
rows.answer: In the morning, the sun is nearer to the horizon. Blue light have shorter wavelength, so , it scatters more. Therefore the red light which have longer wavelength reaches upto the eye of observer and the sun appears reddish.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Reasoning Based Questions — Q1
rows.question: Why is a normal human eye not able to see clearly the objects placed closer than 25 cm ?
rows.answer: The near point of a normal human eye is 25 cm . If we try to read a book very close to our eyes, we will feel a lot of strain, and the printed matter also look blurred.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Reasoning Based Questions — Q2
rows.question: Explain why, we cannot see our seats first when we enter a darkened cinema hall from bright light but gradually they become visible.
rows.answer: It is because, the adjustment of the size of the pupil takes some time. In bright light the pupil of our eye is small, so when we enter the darkened cinema hall very little light enters our eyes and we cannot see properly, but gradually they become visible as the size of pupil increases.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Reasoning Based Questions — Q3
rows.question: Why does the eye-lens not have to do all the work of converging incoming light rays?
rows.answer: Because cornea of the eye also converges light rays entering the eye.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Reasoning Based Questions — Q4
rows.question: Explain clearly why, a person who has lost the sight of one eye is at a disadvantage compared with the normal person who has two good eyes?
rows.answer: Having two eyes gives a wider field of view i.e., 180° but in case of single eye field of view is limited to 150°. Also having two eyes enables us to judge distance more accurately.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Reasoning Based Questions — Q5
rows.question: Among animals, the predators (like lions) have their eyes facing forward at the front of their heads, whereas the animals of prey (like rabbit) usually have eyes at the sides of their head. Why is this so?
rows.answer: The predators like lions have their eyes at the front of head so that they can judge the distance of their prey accurately and catch them easily. The prey like rabbit has eyes on the opposite sides of their heads, so that they can see their enemies in a very large area and try to escape from them.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Reasoning Based Questions — Q6
rows.question: Explain why, if we look at objects through the hot air over a fire, the objects appear to be moving (or shaking) slightly.
rows.answer: When we see the objects through the air over a fire, the light coming from them through hot and cold air layers having different optical densities, then refraction of light takes place randomly due to which the objects appear to be moving slightly.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Reasoning Based Questions — Q7
rows.question: What happens when a beam of sunlight enters a dusty room through a window? Explain your answer.
rows.answer: Path of a beam of sunlight becomes visible. It is because the tiny dust particles present in the air of room scatter the beam all around the room. And when this scattered light enters our eyes we can see the beam of light.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Reasoning Based Questions — Q8
rows.question: Explain why, when the sun is overhead at noon, it appears white, but when the same sun is near the horizon at sunset, it appears red?
rows.answer: When the sun is overhead at noon, then the light coming from the sun has to travel relatively shorter distance through the atmosphere to reach our eyes, so only a little of the blue colours of the white light is scattered i.e., almost all its component colours in the right proportions, hence the sun in the sky overhead appears white to us. But when the sun is near the horizon at sunset due to large distance most of the blue colour present in sunlight has been scattered out and away from our line of sight (LOS) leaving behind mainly red colour in the direct sunlight beam that reaches our eyes.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — HOTS Questions — Q1
rows.question: A person with a myopic eye cannot see objects beyond 1.2 m distinctly. What should be the type of the corrective lens used to restore proper vision?
rows.answer:
In normal eye, the far point is infinity. The lens used should be such that an object at infinity forms virtual image at 1.2 m .
Now, v= − 1.2 m; u= − ∞, f= ?
We know 1/f= 1/v − 1/u
or 1/f= (1)/(− 1.2) − (1)/((− ∞)) = − 1/1.2 or f= − 1.2 m
Power, P= 1/f= (1)/(− 1.2) = (− 5)/(6) = − 0.83 D
A concave (or divergent) lens of focal length 1.2 m and power -0.83 D should be used to restore proper vision.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — HOTS Questions — Q2
rows.question: A person with a defective eye-division is unable to see the objects nearer than 1.5 m . He wants to read books at a distance of 30 m . Find the nature, focal length and power of the lens he needs in his spectacles.
rows.answer:
This person is hypermetropic
So, his u= − 30 c m, v= − 1.5 m= − 150 c m
Focal length of corrective lens :
1/f= 1/v − 1/u ⇒ 1/f= (1)/(− 150) − (1)/(− 30) ⇒ 1/f= (− 1)/(150) + 1/30
⇒ 1/f= (− 1 + 5)/(150) = 4/150 ⇒ f= 150/4= + 37.5 c m
+ sign shows that he needs a convex lens of focal length 37.5 cm .
∴ Power of lens (P) = 100/37.5= 2.67 D

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World

Study the figure given below and answer the questions that follow.
(a) Which defect of vision has been shown in the figure?

(d)

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Passage Based Questions — HOTS Questions — Q4
rows.question:
In the diagram given below, a defective eye has been corrected. Study the diagram and answer the questions that follow.
(a) Name the defect which has been corrected.
(b) Name the point N .
(c) Why a convex lens, instead of a concave lens, has been used ?
(d) Redraw the figure of the defective eye without the corrective lens.
rows.answer:
(a) Hypermetropia or long-sightedness has been corrected.
(b) The point represent the near point of the hypermetropic eye.
(c) In a hypermetropic eye, the rays of light coming from a near object don't converge in appropriate way so as to focus at retina but they get focussed beyond the retina. When a convex lens is used before the eye, extra focussing power is provided and the rays are focussed at the retina.
If a concave lens, on the other hand, will be used then it will diverge the rays and they will get focussed at a point more beyond the retina.
(d)
Hypermetropic eye

rows.question:

The phenomenon of decomposition of the white light into its seven component colours when passing through a prism or through a transparent object delimited by non parallel surfaces is called dispersion of light. A beam of light containing all the visible spectrum of the light is white, because the sum of all the colors generates the white color. The light is decomposed in all the component colours, Violet, Indigo, Blue, Green, Yellow, Orange and Red, called as VIBGYOR. The band of the coloured components of a light beam is called its spectrum. The phenomenon can be explained by thinking that light of different colours (different wavelengths) has different velocities while travelling in a medium vm= f λm.
Hence, the change in velocity of light observed when the light passes from the air to the glass, depends on the wavelength.

The magnification produced is _ _ _ _ times.
(a) 10
(b) 8
(c) 6
(d) 4
rows.answer:
(a) f0= 50 c m, fe= 5 c m, D= 25 c m and u0= 200 c m
Separation between the objective and eye lens is
L= (μ0 f0)/((u0 − f0)) + (fe D)/((fe + D)) = (200 × 50)/(200 − 50) + (5 × 25)/(5 + 25) = 71 c m
Magnification, M= f0/fe= 50/5= 10.

rows.question: The length of a microscope is 14 cm and for relaxed eye the magnifying power is 25 . The focal length of the eyepiece is 5 cm . Calculate the distance of the object and the focal length of the objective.
rows.answer:
( 1.8 c m, 1.5 c m ) Length of microscope
L= v0 + fe or 14= v0 + 5 ⇒ v0= 9 c m
Now, M= − v0/u0 × D/fe, or 25= − 9/u0 × 25/5
or, u0= − 9/5= − 1.8 c m
Now, 1/f0= 1/v0 − 1/u0 but u0 is negative.
Therefore 1/f0= 1/9 + 5/9= 6/9 or f0= 1.5 c m

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: Integer/ Numeric Questions — Directions — Q37
rows.question:
A parallel beam of light is incident from air at an angle α on the side PQ of a right angled triangular prism of refractive index = n √(2).
Light undergoes total internal reflection in the prism at the face PR when a has a
minimum value of 45°. Find the angle θ of the prism
rows.answer:
(15°) Applying Snell's law at A
1 × sin 45° = √(2) × sin r1
∴ r1= 30°
Applying Snell's law at B
√(2) sin C= 1 × sin 90°
∴ C= 45°
In Δ A M B, 90° + θ + r1 + (90° − C) = 180° (From fig.)
∴ θ= 15°

rows.answer: (20) mrequired= m0 × me ⇒ 100= 5 × me ⇒ me= 20

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 2.1
rows.question: A man who wears the corrective lenses of power +3 D , has to hold a newspaper at 25 cm away to see the print clearly. How far away would the newspaper have to be, if he took off the corrective lenses and still, wanted a clear vision?
rows.answer:
Here, u= − 25 c m; P= + 3 D; v= ?
The corrective lenses have the focal length f= 1/P= 1/3 m= 100/3 c m
And the lens formula gives 1/v − 1/u= 1/f
⇒ 1/v − (1)/(− 25) = (1)/(100 / 3) ⇒ 1/v= 3/100 − 1/25 ⇒ v= − 100 c m
which means that the corrective lenses used by man form the clear images at 100 cm of the objects placed at 25 cm , i.e, the near point of man is at 100 cm . Therefore, without wearing the corrective lenses the man must hold the newspaper at 100 cm for the clear vision.

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 2.2
rows.question: What should be the focal length of corrective lenses required by a person for reading a book, if his near point is at 9 0 c m ?
rows.answer:
The book is kept at the least distance of distance vision D= 25 c m, i.e., u= − 25 c m and corrective lenses used form the clear images at 90 cm , which is the near point of person, i.e., v= − 90 c m. Therefore, according to the lens formula, we have
& 1/v − 1/u= 1/f ⇒ & (1)/(− 90) − (1)/(− 25) = 1/f ⇒ 1/f= (− 5 + 18)/(450) ⇒ f= 34.6 c m

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 2.3
rows.question: A ray of light falls normally on a refracting face of a prism of the refractive index μ= 1. 5 of its material. Find the angle of prism, if the ray just fails to emerge from the prism.
rows.answer:
The angle of incidence of the ray of light at the refracting face A B is i1= 0 which implies that the angle of refraction at this face is r1= 0 and then,
r1 + r2= A ⇒ r2= A
At the second refracting face A C, the ray just fails to emerge and therefore, r2= A= ic
A= sin−¹ (1/μ) = sin−¹ (1/1.5) = sin−¹ (2/3) = 42°
Fig. 2.14

rows.chapter_name: The Human Eye and the Colourful World
rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 2.4
rows.question: An equilateral prism is having the refractive index μ= 1.5 of its material. Calculate the angle of emergence i2 of the ray of light for the maximum deviation δmax.
rows.answer:
For the maximum deviation δmax of the light ray, we have i1= 90°
& r1= ic= sin⁻¹ (1/1.5) = sin⁻¹ (2/3) = 42° ⇒ & r2= A − r1= 60° − 42° = 18° [As prism is equilateral∴ ∠ A= 60°]
Then, by the Snell's Law, we have
sin i2= μ sin r2= 1.5 × sin 18° = 1.5 × 18 × π/180= 0.465 ⇒ i2= 28°
## DISPERSION OF WHITE LIGHT BY A GLASS PRISM
The phenomenon of decomposition of the white light into seven component colours when passing through a prism or through a transparent object delimited by non parallel surfaces is called dispersion of light. A beam of light containing all the visible spectrum of the light is white, because the sum of all the colors generates the white color. Normally the light we use is white. It's the light containing all the colors mixed together. We can realize this fact when the beam of light passes through a glass prism: the light is decomposed in all the component colours, Violet, Indigo, Blue, Green, Yellow, Orange and Red, called as VIBGYOR. The band of the coloured components of a light beam is called its spectrum.
The phenomenon can be explained by thinking that light of different colours (different wavelengths) has different velocities while travelling in a medium: vm= f λm.
Hence, the change in velocity of light observed when the light passes from the air to the glass, depends on the wavelength.
Fig. 2.15
By passing the interface air-glass, lower is the wavelength lower becomes the velocity of the light, so, for example, red light rays are faster than violet light rays.
This change in velocity coupled with the direction of the light beam to the air-glass interface explains the decomposition of a white light ray in the component colours while it is passing through a prism.
This phenomenon is known as "dispersion of light through a prism" and it is also responsible for rainbows during storms: as a matter of fact, each raindrop can be regarded as a little prism; when a light ray strikes a raindrop it is refracted and decomposed, spreading out all the visible colours ranging from red to violet.
Colours in the increasing order with respect to wavelength are as follow: Violet (min.), Indigo, Blue, Green, Yellow, Orange, red (max.)
## NEWTON'S TWO PRISMS EXPERIMENT
## Recombination of Different colours of spectrum to give white light
Newton took two prisms P1 and P2 of the same material and having the same refracting angle. He made white light pass through a slit and allowed it to fall on prism P1 (See Fig. 2.16). He obtained a spectrum (VIBGYOR) on the white screen. He removed the white screen and placed prism P2 in an inverted position as shown in Figure. It was observed that the light coming out of the second prism P2 was almost white.
Fig. 2.16 Recombination of different colours forms white light
This experiment proved that the prism P1 dispersed the white light into its constituent colours. Hence, this prism was called the dispersing prism. The second prism P2 recombined the seven constituent colours to form white light. Hence, this prism was called the recombination prism. This experiment thus proved that the prism by itself produces no colours. the recombination of the seven constituent colours forms white light.

rows.question: Least distance of distinct vision is 2 5 c m. What is the magnifying power of simple microscope of focal length 5 cm?
rows.answer:
In general, the simple microscope is used with image, at a distance D= 25 c m.
So, magnifying power, m= 1 + D/f= 1 + 25/5= 6

rows.question: A simple telescope, consisting of an objective of focal length 60 cm and a single eye lens of focal length 5 cm is focussed on a distant object in such a way that parallel rays come out from the eye lens. If the object subtends an angle 2° at the objective, then find the angular width (i.e., angle subtended by the image) of the image
rows.answer:
Since m= fo/fe Also m= (Angle subtended by the image)/(Angle subtended by the object) ∴ fo/fe= α/β ⇒ α= (fo × β)/(fe) = (60 × 2)/(5) = 24° ## MISCELLANEOUS SOLVED EXAMPLES 1. A person needs a lens of power − 5. 5 dioptres for correcting his distant vision. For correcting his near vision he needs a lens of power +1.5 diopters. What is the focal length of the lens required for correcting (i) distant vision, and (ii) near vision? Sol. (i) Focal length of distance viewing = 1/Power × 100 c m= (− 100)/(5.5) c m= − 18.18 c m. (ii) Focal length in near vision = 100/1.5 c m= 66.6 c m. 2. Make a diagram to show how hypermetropia is corrected. The near point of a hypermetropic eye is 1 m . What is the power of the lens required to correct this defect? Assume that the near point of the normal eye is 2 5 c m. Sol. Hypermetropic eye Corrected hypermetropic eye To correct the defect, the image of an object at 25 cm should be brought at 100 cm . ∴ 1/f= 1/v − 1/u= (1)/(− 100) − (1)/(− 25) i.e., 1/f= (− 1)/(100) + 1/25= (− 1 + 4)/(100) = 3/100∴ f= + 100/3= + 33.3 c m So, a convex lens of focal length 33.3 cm or, Power, P= 100/33.3= 3.0 D is required. 3. Why is a normal eye not able to see clearly the objects placed closer than 2 5 c m ? Sol. The maximum accommodation of a normal eye is reached when the object is at a distance of 25 cm from the eye. The focal length of the eye lens cannot be decreased below this minimum limit. Thus, an object placed closer than 25 cm cannot be seen clearly by a normal eye because all the power of accommodation has already been exhausted. 4. A farsighted person cannot focus clearly an object that are less than 1 4 5 c m from his eyes. To correct this problem, the person wear eyeglasses that are located 2.0 cm in front of his eyes. Determine the focal length that will permit this person to read a newspaper at a distance of 32.0 cm from his eyes. Sol. The near point is 145 cm and eyeglasses are 2.0 cm in front of the eyes. Therefore, v= − 145 + 2= − 143 c m. The object is placed 32.0 cm from the eyes so, u= − 30.0 c m. The focal length is obtained from equation 1/f= 1/v − 1/u= (1)/((− 143)) − (1)/((− 30)) = 0.026 c m−¹ Hence, f= 38 c m. 5. A certain eye has a near point of 11.0 cm and a far point of 15.0 cm . (a) What is the refractive power of the lens that is required to place the far point at infinity? (b) What is the refractive power of the lens required to yield a near-point distance of 25 cm ? Sol. (a) We want to move the far point to infinity, so power, using 1/f= 1/v − 1/u ⇒ 1/f= (1)/((− 0.15)) − (1)/((− ∞)) = − 6.7 diopters (b) For the near point at 25 cm : 1/f= (1)/((− 0.11)) − (1)/((− 0.25)) = − 5.1 diopters 6. A figure divided into squares, each of size 1 m m², is being viewed at a distance of 9 cm through a magnifying glass (a converging lens of focal length 1 0 c m ) held close to the eye. (i) What is the magnification (image size/object size) produced by the lens? How much is the area of each square in the virtual image? (ii) What is the angular magnification (magnifying power) of the lens? (iii) Is the magnification in (i) equal to the magnifying power in (ii) ? Explain. Sol. (i) Here, u= − 9 c m, f= 10 c m. Now, 1/v − 1/u= 1/f or 1/v= 1/f + 1/u or, 1/v= 1/10 + (1)/(− 9) = (− 9 + 10)/(− 90) = − 1/90 or v= − 90 c m. Magnification, m= v/u= (− 90)/(− 9) = 10 Since, magnification is 10 therefore the area of each square in the virtual image is 10 × 10 × 1 m m² i.e., 100 m m². (ii) Angular magnification, α= D/u= (− 25)/(− 9) = 2.78
(iii) No, magnification of an image by a lens and angular magnification (or magnifying power) of an optical instrument are two separate things. The latter is the ratio of the angular size of the object (which is equal to the angular size of the image even if the image is magnified) to the angular size of the object if placed at the near point (25 cm). Thus, magnification magnitude isv/uand magnifying power is (25)/(u). Only when the image is located at the near point (v= 25 c m ), are the two quantities equal.
7. A lady uses +1.5 D glasses to have normal vision from 25 cm onwards. She uses a 20 D lens as a magnifying glass to see an object. Calculate the maximum magnifying power if she uses the magnifying glass (a) together with her glass (b) without her glass. Sol. (a) In this case, magnifying power, M= 1 + D/f= 1 + (25)/(100/20) = 6 (b) Focal length of glasses = 100/1.5= 1000/15= 200/3 1/v − (1)/(− 25) = 3/100 or 1/v= 3/200 − 1/25= (3 − 8)/(200) = − 5/200= − 1/40 ⇒ v= − 40 c m. Now, magnifying power, M= 1 + P v= 1 + 20 (40/100) = 1 + 8= 9 8. Complete the path of the monochromatic ray of light in each (a), (b), (c) of the right angled isosceles triangle. Refractive index of the material of right angled isosceles triangle is 1. 5 (figure). Sol. (b) 9. The light ray is incident at an angle of 60° on a prism of angle 45°. When the light ray falls on the other surface at 9 0° then find the refractive index of the material of prism. Sol. From figure, ∠ e= ∠ r2= 0 & using, A= r1 + r2 & ⇒ A= 45° & ∴ μ= (sin i)/(sin r) = (sin 60°)/(sin 45°) = √(3/2) ≃ 1.22 10. A ray of light is incident at an angle of incidence i, on one face of a prism of angle A (assumed to be small) and emerges normally from the opposite face. If the refractive index of the prism is μ then find the angle of incidence i, in terms of μ and A. Sol. For normally emerge, e= 0∴ r2= 0 and r1= A Snell's law for incident ray, μ= (sin i)/(sin r) ⇒ (−)/(1) = (sin i)/(sin A) ⇒ sin i= μ sin A ∴ For small angle, i= μ A 11. White light passes through a thin prism of angle 6°. If the refractive indices for the red and violet colours are 1.641 and 1.659 , respectively, what is the angular dispersion produced by the prism? Sol. Given: Angle of prism, ∠ A= 6°; μR= 1.641; μv= 1.659; angular dispession θ= ? The angular dispersion produced by the prism (μy − 1) A + (μy − 1) A′ = 0= (1.659 − 1.641) 6° = 0.018 × 6° = 0.108° 12. The crown glass has the refractive indices 1.51 and 1.49 for the blue and red lights, respectively, and the flint glass has the refractive indices 1.77 and 1.73 for the blue and red lights, respectively. A beam of white light is incident on a combination of two thin isosceles prisms, one of crown glass of angle A= 6° and other of flint glass of unknown angle A′. If there is no net deviation of the light in passing through the combination, calculate the angle A′ and the net angular dispersion produced by the combination. Sol. The net deviation of light produced by the combination of two prisms is zero and therefore, & (μy − 1) A + (μy − 1) A′ = 0 ⇒ & A′ = − ((μy − 1) A)/((μ_y′ − 1)) = − ([(μV + μR) / 2 − 1] A)/([(μ_V′ + μ_R′) / 2 − 1]) = − ((μV + μR − 2) A)/((μ_V′ + μ_R′ − 2)) = − ((1.51 + 1.49 − 2) 6°)/((1.77 + 1.73 − 2)) = 4° where the minus sign indicates that the second prism of flint glass of angle A′ = 4° is placed inverted with respect to the first prism of crown glass of angle A= 6°. Then the net angular dispersion of light produced by the combination is θ & = (μ_V′ − μ_R′) A′ + (μV − μR) A & = − (1.77 − 1.73) 4° + (1.51 − 1.49) 6° = − 0.04 × 4° + 0.02 × 6= − 0.04 13. A compound microscope has the magnifying power of magnitude 30 . The focal length of it's eyepiece is 5 cm . If the final image is formed at the least distance of distinct vision D= 25 c m, calculate the magnification produced by the objective. Sol. The magnifying power of compound microscope is M= m0 × me= m0 × (1 + D/fe) Therefore, the magnification produced by the objective is m0= M/me= (M)/((1 + D/fe)) = (− 30)/((1 + 25/5)) = − 5 where the minus sign indicates that the image formed by the objective is inverted. 14. An astronomical telescope, consisting of an objective of focal length 60 cm and eyepiece of focal length 3 cm , is focussed on moon whose final image is formed at the least distance of distinct vision D= 25 c m from the eyepiece. If the angular size of moon is 0.5°, what will be the linear size of the final image of moon? Sol. The magnifying power of telescope has the magnitude
M= f0/fe (1 + fe/D) = 60/3 (1 + 3/25) = 22.4
Then, the angular size of the final image of moon will be
=M× 0.5° = 22.4 × 0.5° = 11.2° = 11.2 × π/180 radian
and it's linear size will be = D (11.2 × π/180) c m= 25 (11.2 × π/180) c m= 25 × 0.2 c m= 5 c m ## Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) DIRECTIONS : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has 4 choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which ONLY ONE is correct. 1. The human eye possesses the power of accommodation. This is the power to : (a) alter the diameter of the pupil as the intensity of light changes (b) distinguish between lights of different colours (c) focus objects at different distances (d) decide which of the two objects is closer. 2. How does the eye change in order to focus on near or distant objects? (a) The lens moves in or out (b) The retina moves in or out (c) The lens becomes thicker or thinner (d) The pupil gets larger or smaller 3. (a) The pupil becomes larger (b) The lens becomes thicker (c) The ciliary muscle relaxes (d) The pupil becomes smaller 4. A person got his eyes tested. The optician's prescription for the spectacles reads: Left eye : -3.00 D Right eye : -3.50 D The person is having a defect of vision called : (a) presbyopia (b) myopia (c) astigmatism (d) hypermetropia 5. A student sitting on the last bench in the class cannot read the writing on the blackboard clearly but he can read the book lying on his desk clearly. Which of the following statement is correct about the student? (a) The near point of his eyes has receded away. (b) The near point of his eyes has come closer to him. (c) The far point of his eyes has receded away. (d) The far point of his eyes has come closer to him. 6. A man driving a car can read a distant road sign clearly but finds difficulty in reading the odometer on the dashboard of the car. Which of the following statement is correct about this man? (a) The near point of his eyes has receded away. (b) The near point of his eyes has come closer to him. (c) The far point of his eyes has receded away. (d) The far point of his eyes has come closer to him. 7. Which of the following is not caused by the atmospheric refraction of light? (a) Twinkling of stars at night (b) Sun appearing higher in the sky than it actually is (c) Sun becoming visible two minutes before actual sunrise (d) Sun appearing red at sunset 8. The sky appears dark to passengers flying at very high altitudes mainly because : (a) Scatterings of light is not enough at such heights. (b) There is no atmosphere at great heights. (c) The size of molecules is smaller than the wavelength of visible light. (d) The light gets scattered towards the earth. ## Assertion-Reason DIRECTIONS: Two statements are given- one labeled Assertion (A) and the other labelled Reason (R). Select the correct answer to these questions from the codes (a), (b), (c) and (d) as given below (a) If both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion. Sunlight comprises _ _ _ _ colours. ## True / False ## DIRECTIONS : Read the following statements and write your answer as true or false. 1. Lens which is used for correcting the presbyopia defect of the eye is concave. 2. The colour that deviates maximum while passing through a glass prism is violet. 3. Water droplets act as tiny prism in the formation of rainbow. 4. The transparent spherical membrane covering the front of the eye is known as cornea. 5. The sun is visible two minutes before the actual sunrise due to atmospheric refraction. ## Column I (A) Myopia (B) Hypermetropia (C) Astigmatism (D) Presbyopia ## Column II (p) Convex lens (q) Concave lens (r) Cylindrical lens (s) Bi-focal lens ## Match the Following ## DIRECTIONS : Each question contains statements given in two columns which have to be matched. Statements ( A, B, C, D) in column I have to be matched with statements ( p, q, r, s ) in column II. 1. Column II gives lens that can be use to correct the defect of vision given in column I, match them correctly. ## Passage Based Questions DIRECTIONS : Study the given passage and answer the following questions. ## Passage Human eye is spherical in shape and has diameter of about 2.5 cm . Sclerotic is a tough, opaque and white substance forming the outermost coating of the eyeball. The front portion is sharply curved and covered by a transparent protective membrane called the 'cornea'. Inner to the sclerotic there is a layer of black tissue called as choroids consisting of a mass of blood vessels, which nourishes the eye. The black colour does not reflect the light and hence rules out the blurring of image by reflection within the eyeball. Behind the cornea, the space is filled with a liquid called the aqueous humour and behind that a crystalline lens. 'Iris' is a muscular diaphragm lying between the aqueous humour and the crystalline lens. Iris has an adjustable opening in the middle called the pupil of the eye. The pupil appears black because all the light entering is absorbed by the 'retina', which covers the inside of the rear part of the ball. Iris controls the amount of light entering because the retina absorbs nearly all the light, which falls upon it. This is done by varying the aperture of the pupil with the help of the iris. In dim light the iris dilates the pupil so that more light can enter in. When the light is bright the pupil contracts. The crystalline lens divides the eyeball into two chambers. The chamber between the cornea and the lens is called the anterior chamber filled with a fluid called aqueous humour while the chamber between the lens and the retina is called the posterior chamber which is filled with a transparent gelatinous substance called vitreous humour. The refractive indices of the cornea, pupil lens and fluid portion of the eye are quite similar. So, when a ray of light enters the eye, it is refracted at the cornea. This refraction produces a real inverted and diminished image of distant objects on the retina. When the object is kept at different distances then, we may expect the image to be formed at different distances from the lens. It means, it may not form on the retina always. But in reality it is not so. Image is always formed on the retina. This is possible because the curvature of the crystalline lens is altered by ciliary muscles. When the eye is focused on infinity the muscles are relaxed and the eye lens remains thin. If the object is brought near by, the curvature increases so that the image can be formed on the retina. This property of the eye lens is called accommodation. 1. The change in focal length of an eye lens to focus the image of objects at varying distances is done by the action of (a) pupil (b) ciliary muscles (c) retina (d) blind spot 2. The fluid between the retina and the lens is called _ _ _ _ (a) aqueous humour (b) vitreous humour (c) aqua (d) humus 3. The part of the eye where optic nerves enter the eye (a) pupil (b) ciliary muscles (c) retina (d) blind spot 4. The inner back surface of the eyeball is called (a) pupil (b) ciliary muscles (c) retina (d) blind spot ## Very Short Answer Questions 1.
