## L
One example of a water system is a garden hose that waters a garden. Another is the cooling system of an automobile. Which of these exhibits behavior more analogous to that of an electric circuit? Why?
The garden hose is more analogous to an electric circuit as it contains a source of water in which there is a pressure difference like an electric circuit contains a source of electrical energy which has a potential difference. Also, the water is supplied through a pipe in the same way as a conducting path is provided for the flow of electrical energy in a circuit.
## ELECTRICAL RESISTANCE
Directly connecting the poles of a voltage source together with a single piece of wire is dangerous because the magnitude of electric current may be very large in such a short circuit, and the release of energy very dramatic (usually in the form of heat),you may have heard about firing due to short circuit. Usually, electric circuits are constructed in such a way as to make practical use of that released energy, in as safe a manner as possible. One practical and popular use of electric current is for the operation of electric lighting. The simplest form of electric lamp is a tiny metal "filament" inside of a clear glass bulb, which glows white-hot (incandesces) with heat energy when sufficient electric current passes through it. Like the battery, it has two conductive connection points, one for electrons to enter and the other for electrons to exit. Connected to a source of voltage, an electric lamp circuit looks something like this:
Fig. 3.13
As the electrons work their way through the thin metal filament of the lamp, they encounter more opposition to motion than they typically would in a thick piece of wire. This opposition to electric current depends on the type of material, its cross-sectional area, and its temperature. It is technically known as resistance.
Resistance is the obstruction offered to the flow of electric current. (It can be said that conductors have low resistance and insulators have very high resistance.) This resistance serves to limit the amount of current through the circuit with a given amount of voltage supplied by the battery, as compared with the "short circuit" where we had nothing but a wire joining one end of the voltage source (battery) to the other.
The S.I. unit of resistance is ohm (Ω).
The reciprocal of resistance is called conductance G= 1 / R
Its S.I. unit is ohm −¹ or mho or siemen (s).
When electrons move against the opposition of resistance, due to friction heat is generated. Just like mechanical friction, the friction produced by electrons flowing against a resistance manifests itself in the form of heat. The concentrated resistance of a lamp's filament results in a relatively large amount of heat energy dissipated at that filament. This heat energy is enough to cause the filament to glow white-hot, producing light, whereas the wires connecting the lamp to the battery (which have much lower resistance) hardly even get warm while conducting the same amount of current. How to deal with resistance mathematically? You will find the answer in Ohm's law section.
## Factors Affecting Resistance
The resistance of a conductor depends on temperature, nature of material, length and area of cross-section.
## Resistance depends on temperature:
If the resistance of the wire at 0° C be R0 and at t° C be Rt, then the value of Rt is obtained by the following formula
Rt= R0 (1 + α t)
where α is a constant which is called the 'temperature coefficient of resistance' of the material of the wire.
From eq. (1), we have α= (Rt − R0)/(R0 × t) per ° C
If R0= 1 o h m, t= 1° C then α= (Rt − R0) = increase in resistance.
Therefore, if the resistance of a wire at 0° C be 1 ohm , then on raising its temperature to 1° C, the increase in its resistance will be equal to the "temperature coefficient of resistance' of the material of that wire. For most of the metals, the value of α is nearly (1/273) per ° C. Hence, by eq. (1), we have
Rt= R0 (1 + t/273) = R0 ((273 + t))/(273) = R0 (T/273)
where T is the absolute temperature. Thus, Rt ∝ T
Thus, the resistance of a pure metallic wire is directly proportional to its absolute temperature.
The resistivity of alloys also increases with rise in temperature, but this increase is much smaller compared to pure metals. There are certain alloys such as manganin, constantan, nichrome, etc., whose resistivity is little affected by temperature, that is, their temperature coefficient of resistance is negligible. On account of their high resistivity and negligible temperature coefficient of resistance, these alloys are used to make wires for standard resistances, resistance boxes, etc.
Resistance depends on the length and the cross-section of the wire :
The resistance of ohmic circuit elements such as metal wires or carbon resistor depends on their geometries.
To find dependence, first we will keep the thickness of the object (like a copper wire) fixed and just increase or decrease the length of the wire. If we apply a potential difference across the ends of the wire and use current and potential difference measurements, we can determine its resistance as a function of length. We find that there is a proportionality between the length L of the wire and the resistance of the wire R. Thus we can write
R ∝ L i.e., R= k L
If instead we fix the length of the copper wire and vary the thickness or cross-sectional area A then we find the resistance of the copper decreases as the cross-sectional area A of the wire increases.
In fact we get an inverse relationship so that R ∝ 1/A or, R= k′ 1/A
Combining these two expressions gives us : R= k k′ L/A
To simplify this expression, we can replace the product of the two constants with a single new constant and write R= ρ L/A
Where ρ is a constant, which is called the specific resistance or resistivity of the conducting material. It does not depend on the area of cross-section. Resistivity depends only on the nature of material and temperature.
Resistivity, ρ= R A/L∴ The SI unit of ρ= (o h m × m²)/(m) = ohm × meter (Ω m)
Definition of resistivity : If L= 1 meter, A= 1 square meter then ρ= R (numerically) i.e. resistance of a wire of unit length and unit area of cross section is numerically equal to the resistivity or specific resistance of the material of wire.
Resistance is a property of an object. Resistivity is a property of a material.
Resistance of some metallic conductors rises with increase in temperature, e.g. copper, silver etc. Resistance of some alloys such as manganin and constantan changes very slowly with increase in temperature. Resistance of semiconductors such as germanium (Ge), silicon (Si) etc. decreases with increase in temperature.
On decreasing temperature of some metals, the resistance becomes zero at a certain temperature.
Resistance of mercury becomes zero at 4.2 K temperature and acts as a superconductor.
## To show dependence of resistance on length, area of cross-section and material of the conducting wire
Complete an electric circuit consisting of a cell, an ammeter, a nichrome wire of length l [say, marked (1)] and a plug key, as shown in fig. 3.16. Now, plug the key. Note the current in the ammeter.
Fig. 3.16 : Electric circuit to study the factors on which the resistance of conducting wires depends.
Replace the nichrome wire by another nichrome wire of same thickness but twice the length, that is 2 l [marked ( 2 ) in the Fig]. Note the ammeter reading.
Now replace the wire by a thicker nichrome wire, of the same length l [marked (3)]. A thicker wire has a larger cross-sectional area. Again note down the current through the circuit.
Instead of taking a nichrome wire, connect a copper wire [marked (4) in Fig.] in the circuit. Let the wire be of the same length and same area of cross-section as that of the first nichrome wire [marked (1)]. Note the value of the current.
Notice the difference in the current in all cases.
Does the current depend on the length of the conductor?
Does the current depend on the area of cross-section of the wire used?
Does the current depend on the material of the wire used?

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 3.9
rows.question: Two wires A and B of equal masses and of same metal are taken. The diameter of the wire A is half the diameter of the wire B. If the resistance of the wire A be 24 ohm , calculate the resistance of the wire B.
rows.answer:
Let rA be the radius and ℓA the length of the wire A, and rB the radius and ℓB the length of the wire B. Both the wires are of the same material (density d ). Since the masses of the two wires are equal, we have
π r_A² ℓA × d= π r_B² ℓB × d∴ ℓA/ℓB= r_B²/r_A²
Resistance of the wire A is RA= ρ (ℓA)/(π r_A²), Resistance of the wire B is RB= ρ (ℓB)/(π r_B²) where ρ is the specific resistance of the material of the wires
∴ RA/RB= ℓA/ℓB × r_B²/r_A²= r_B⁴/r_A⁴
But rA= 1/2 rB (given) ∴ RA/RB= 16 or RB= 1/16 RA= 1/16 × 24 ohm = 1.5 ohm

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 3.10
rows.question: Calculate the resistance of 1 km long copper wire of radius 1 mm . (Resistivity of copper is 1.72 × 10−⁸ Ω m )
rows.answer:
Given: ℓ= 1 k m= 1000 m, r= 1 m m= 10−³ m, A= π r²= 3.14 × (10−³)²= 3.14 × 10−⁶ m² Resistivity ρ= 1.72 × 10−⁸ Ω m Resistance, R= ρ l/A= (1.72 × 10−⁸ × 1000)/(3.14 × 10−⁶) = 5.5 Ω. ## OHM'S LAW According to Ohm's law "The current passing through a conductor is directly proportional to the potential difference between its ends, provided the physical conditions such as temperature of conductor remain unchanged." i.e., V ∝ I or V= R I where R is a constant which is called resistance of the material. R= V/I= volt/ampere= ohm (Ω) Fig. 3.17 Unit of resistance The SI unit of resistance is ohm ( Ω ). If I= 1 ampere, V= 1 volt then R= 1 Ω. The resistance of a conductor is 1 ohm if a potential difference of 1 volt is produced across its ends when 1 ampere of the current flows through it. The conductors, which obey the Ohm's law are called the ohmic conductors or linear resistances. All metallic conductors (such as silver, aluminium, copper, iron, etc.) are the ohmic conductors or linear resistances. The conductors, which do not obey the Ohm's law are called the non-ohmic conductors or non-linear resistances. Examples are, diode valve, triode valve, transistors, electrolyte, etc. ## აગ!აჩყᲫ It is sometime contended that R= Δ V / i (or Δ V= i R ) is a statement of Ohm's law. That is not true! This equation is the defining equation for resistance, and it applies to all conducting devices, whether they obey Ohm's law or not. If we measure the potential difference Δ V across and the current i through the device, even a bulb or other non-ohmic device, we can find its resistance at that value of Δ V as R= Δ V / i. The essence of Ohm's law, however, is a plot of i versus Δ V that it is straight line, so that the value of R is independent of the value of Δ V. - Take a nichrome wire, a torch bulb, a 10 W bulb and an ammeter ( 0 − 5 A range), a plug key and some connecting wires. - Set up the circuit by connecting four dry cells of 1.5 V each in series with the ammeter leaving a gap X Y in the circuit, as shown in Figure. Fig. 3.18 - Complete the circuit by connecting the nichrome wire in the gap XY. Plug the key. Note down the ammeter reading. Take out the key from the plug. Always take out the key from the plug after measuring the current through the circuit. - Replace the nichrome wire with the torch bulb in the circuit and find the current through it by measuring the reading of the ammeter. - Now repeat the above step with the 10 W bulb in the gap X Y. - Are the ammeter readings differ for different components connected in the gap X Y ? What do the above observations indicate? - You may repeat this activity by keeping any material component in the gap. Observe the ammeter readings in each case. Analyse the observations. ## VERIFICATION OF OHM'S LAW Set up a circuit as shown in figure with a battery, switch ( s ), a rheostat ( R h ), ammeter ( A ), a resistor ( R ) and a voltmeter ( V ). Close the circuit and adjust the rheostat such that the ammeter and the voltmeter show a measurable reading I and V respectively. Repeat the experiment for different values of I and V by adjusting the rheostat. Tabulate your results as shown below :
Trial No.Potential difference V (in volt)Current I (in ampere)V/I= R (in ohm)

2.

Plot a graph of V against I. If it is a straight line passing through the origin Fig. 3.20 we can conclude that P.D. ' V ' is directly proportional to the current ' I ' and Ohm's law is verified. Fig. 3.20 ## Limitation of Ohm's Law 1. Ohm's law does not apply to conductors such as diode, radio valves, metal rectifiers, electricity through gases. 2. Ohm's law is applicable only when the physical conditions remain constant, for example the resistance of some conductors will vary if the wires are heated or placed under tension. 3. Ohm's law is applicable only when the temperature of the conductor is constant. Generally the resistance of pure metals increases with temperature but the resistance of certain other conducting materials like carbon decreases with temperature. There are certain metal alloys of magnesium and copper whose resistance changes with temperature. ## Learn (O) The substances which obey Ohm's law are called Ohmic or linear conductors. The resistance of such conductors is independent of magnitude and polarity of applied potential difference. Here the graph between I and V is a straight line passing through the origin. The reciprocal of slope of straight line gives resistance Fig. 3.21 (a) R= (Δ V)/(Δ I) = (1)/(tan θ) = constant. Examples silver, copper, mercury, carbon, mica etc. The substances which do not obey Ohm's law are called non-ohmic or non linear conductors. The I − V curve is not a straight line. Examples p − n junction diode, transistors, thermionic valves, rectifiers etc. Fig. 3.21 (b) ## Time to Check Your Knowledge Is Ohm's law universally applicable for all conducting elements? If not, give examples of elements which do not obey Ohm's law. Ohm's law is not a fundamental law in nature. It is not universally followed. Semiconductor diodes, transistor, thermistors, vacuum tubes do not follow ohm's law. ## ILLUSTRATIOM : 3.11 Calculate the potential difference required across a conductor of resistance 5 Ω to flow a current of 1.5 A through it. rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 3.14
rows.question: The potential difference between the terminals of an electric heater is 60 V when it draws a current of 4 A from the source. What current will the heater draw if the potential difference is increased to 120 V ?
rows.answer:
We are given, potential difference V= 60 V, current I= 4 A.
According to Ohm's law, R= V/I= (60 V)/(4 A) = 15 Ω
When the potential difference is increased to 120 V the current is given by
I= V/R= (120 V)/(15 Ω) = 8 A
The current through the heater becomes 8 A .

## COMBINATION OF RESISTORS
In many practical applications to have desired value of resistance two or more resistances are required to be combined. This can be done in two ways: (i) In series and (ii) In parallel. Sometimes resistances are to be combined in such a way that some resistances be in series and some in parallel. Such a combination is called mixed grouping.
If, in an electrical circuit, two or more resistances connected between two points are replaced by a single resistance such that there is no change in the current of the circuit and in the potential difference between those two points, then the single resistance is called the 'equivalent resistance'.
When the resistance of a circuit is to be increased, they are combined in series and when heavy current is to be passed, they are combined in parallel so as to decrease the total resistance.
## Series Combination of Resistors
In this combination, the resistances are joined end to end. Thus the second end of each resistance is joined to the first end of the next resistance, and so on. The first end of the first resistance and the second end of the last resistance are connected to the cell. In this combination, the same current flows in all the resistances but the potential differences between their ends are different according to their resistances.
Infigure, three resistances A B, B C and CD are connected in series. Suppose their resistances are R1, R2 and R3 respectively. Let the 'equivalent resistance' of these resistances be R. Suppose a current is flowing in all the three resistances. Suppose the potential differences between the ends of the resistances R1, R2 and R3 are V1, V2 and V3 respectively. Then, according to Ohm's law we have
V1= i R1, V2= i R2 and V3= i R3.
If the potential difference between points A and D be V, then
Fig. 3.22
V= V1 + V2 + V3= i R1 + i R2 + i R3= i (R1 + R2 + R3)
The equivalent resistance between points A and D is R. Therefore
V= i R
Comparing eq. (1) and (2), we get
i R & = i (R1 + R2 + R3) R & = R1 + R2 + R3
Therefore,
That is, the equivalent resistance of the resistances connected in series is equal to the sum of those resistances and is thus greater than any individual resistance. Equivalent resistance, R= R1 + R2 + R3 + … + Rn.

## Parallel Combination of Resistors
When two or more resistances are combined in such a way that their first ends are connected to one point and the second ends to another point then this combination is in parallel. In this combination, the potential difference between the ends of all the resistances is same but the current in different resistances are different.
In Fig. 3.25, three resistances R1, R2 and R3 are joined in parallel between the points A and B. Suppose the current flowing from the cell is i. At the point A, this current is divided into three parts. Suppose i1, i2 and i3 are the currents in R1, R2 and R3 respectively. At the point B, these three currents meet and form the main current i. Thus, we have
Fig. 3.25
i= i1 + i2 + i3
Let the potential difference between the points A and B be V. Since each resistance is connected between A and B, the potential difference between the ends of each will be V. Therefore,
i1= V/R1, i2= V/R2 and i3= V/R3
Substituting these values in eq. (1) we get,
i= V/R1 + V/R2 + V/R3
If the equivalent resistance between the points A and B be R, then
i= V / R
Comparing eq. (2) and (3), we get
V/R= V/R1 + V/R2 + V/R3 or 1/R= 1/R1 + 1/R2 + 1/R3 1/R, = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + 1/R3 … 1/Rn
That is, the reciprocal of the equivalent resistance of the resistances connected in parallel is equal to the sum of the reciprocals of those resistances. Equivalent resistance,
In parallel combination, the equivalent resistance R is less than even the smallest resistance connected
Make a parallel combination, of three resistors having resistances R1, R2, and R3, respectively. Connect it with a battery, a plug key and an ammeter, as shown in Fig 3.26 (a). It gives the potential difference V, across the combination. The potential difference across each resistor is also V. This can be checked by connecting the voltmeter across each individual resistor [Fig. 3.26(b)].
Fig. 3.26
Take out the plug from the key. Remove the ammeter and voltmeter from the circuit. Insert the ammeter in series with the resistance R1, as shown in Fig. 3.26 (b). Note the ammeter reading, I1.
Similarly, measure the currents through R2 and R3. Let these be I2 and I3, respectively. What is the relationship between I, I1, I2 and I3 ?
- Given n resistors each of resistance R, how will you combine them to get the (i) maximum (ii) minimum effective resistance? What is the ratio of the maximum to minimum resistance?
(i) For maximum effective resistance, all the resistors should be joined in series.
Rmax= R + R + R + … … … n or Rmax= n R
(ii) For minimum effective resistance all the resistors should be joined in parallel
1/Rmin= 1/R + 1/R + 1/R + … … … n or 1/Rmin= n/R So, Rmin= R/n
Now, Rmax/Rmin= n²

rows.answer:
Given: R1= 2 Ω, R2= 3 Ω and R3= 4 Ω
(i) In series, the equivalent resistance
R= R1 + R2 + R3= 2 + 3 + 4= 9 Ω.
(ii) In parallel, the equivalent resistance is R, then
1/R= 1/R1 + 1/R2 + 1/R3 or 1/R= 1/2 + 1/3 + 1/4= 13/12 or R= 12/13

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 3.18
rows.question: Two resistors of 4 Ω and 6 Ω are connected in parallel. The combination is connected across a 6 V battery of negligible resistance. Calculate the current (i) through the battery (ii) through each resistor. Draw the circuit diagram.
rows.answer:
The circuit diagram is shown in fig. 3.28.
(i) If resistance of the combination is R, then
1/R= 1/4 + 1/6= (3 + 2)/(12) = 5/12
or R= 12/5 Ω= 2.4 Ω
Let current through the battery be I.
Fig. 3.28
I= V/R= 6/2.4= 2.5 A
(ii) The potential difference across each resistor is V= 6 volt (same as of battery) since they are in parallel.
Current through 4 Ω resistor, I1= V/R1= 6/4= 1.5 A
Current through 6 Ω resistor, I2= V/R2= 6/6= 1 A

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 3.19
rows.question:
6 Ω and 3 Ω resistors are connected in parallel and an 8 Ω resistor is connected in series with them. A current of 2 A passes through the 8 Ω resistor. Find
(a) the combined resistance
(b) the potential difference across the combined resistor
(c) the current through the 3 Ω resistor
rows.answer:
The combined resistance R of the resistors in parallel.
Given: R1= 6 Ω, R2= 3 Ω,
1/R= 1/R1 + 1/R2= 1/6 + 1/3= 3/6= 1/2
or, R= 2 Ω
The equivalent circuit is shown in Fig. 3.29 (b).
Fig. 3.29 (b)
(a) Let the combined resistance of all three resistors be R
Equivalent resistance, R= R1 + R2= 8 Ω
& R1= 8 Ω, R2= 2 Ω & R= R1 + R2= 8 Ω + 2 Ω= 10 Ω
(b) Let the potential difference across the combined resistors be V.
& V=?, I= 2 A, R= 10 Ω & V= I × R= 2 A × 10 Ω= 20 V
(c) The combined resistance of the parallel network is 2 Ω. Hence potential difference V across the parallel resistor is
V= I × R= 2 A × 2 Ω= 4 V
For the 3 Ω resistor V= 4 V, I=?, R= 3 Ω
I= V/R= (4 V)/(3 Ω) = 1 1/3 A

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 3.20
rows.question:
Three resistors are connected in series to 24 volt battery, and an ammeter in the circuit reads 0.50 amperes. The first resistor is rated at 22 ohms , and the second at 8 ohms . Find (a) the total resistance (b) the resistance of the third resistor, and
(c) the potential difference across the third resistor.
rows.answer:
Given: Current in the circuit, I = 0.5 A; V= 24 V
(a) The total resistance of the circuit
R= (Δ V)/(I) = (24 V)/(0.50 A) = 48 Ω
(b) In a series circuit, resistance add, so 22 Ω + 8 Ω + R3= 48 Ω ⇒ R3= 18 Ω.
(c) For any circuit element, Δ V= I R so V3= (0.50 A) (18 Ω) = 9 V

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 3.21
rows.question:
In the circuit shown, find :
(a) the equivalent resistance between points 𝐀 and 𝐁.
(b) the current through the battery
rows.answer:
Fig. 3.30
Given : R1= 12 Ω, R2= 12 Ω, R3= 6.0 Ω, V= 12 V
(a) The three resistors are in a parallel combination.
1/Rp= 1/R1 + 1/R2 + 1/R3= (1)/(12 Ω) + (1)/(12 Ω) + (1)/(6.0 Ω) = (1)/(12 Ω) + (1)/(12 Ω) + (2)/(12 Ω) = (4)/(12 Ω) = (1)/(3.0 Ω)
⇒ Rp= 3 Ω
(b) From Ohm's law, I= V/R= V/Rp= (12 V)/(3.0 Ω) = 4.0 A

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 3.27
rows.question: Compute the heat generated while transferring 96000 coulomb of charge in one hour through a potential difference of 5 0 V.

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 3.28
rows.question: There are 5 rooms in a house. Each room has a 100 W bulb and a 40 W tube light. If every day the bulb is used for 1 hour and tube light is used for 5 hours in each room then what will be the cost of total electric energy consumed in 30 days when 1 unit of electric energy costs ₹ 2.5.
rows.answer:
Energy used every day in 5 bulbs = 5 × 100 × 1 W h= 500 W h
Energy used everyday in 5 tube lights = 5 × 40 × 5 W h= 1000 W h
Total energy used everyday by bulb and tube light = (500 + 1000) W h= 1500 W h
Total energy used in 30 day = 1500 × 30 W h= 45000 W h= 45000/1000 K W h= 45 K W h (unit)
Cost of the total energy used = 45 × 2.50= ₹ 112.50

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 3.29
rows.question: A cell of e.m.f. E is connected with an external resistance R, then p.d. across cell is V. What is the internal resistance of cell?
rows.answer:
According to Ohm's law, current i= V/R
Across the cell E= V + ir here r= internal resistance of cell
∴ r= (E − V)/(I) = (E − V)/(V) · R

rows.title: ILLUSTRATION : 3.30
rows.question: A car battery has an emf of 12 V if the internal resistance of the battery is 0.5 Ω then find the maximum current that an be drawn from the battery.
rows.answer:
Here, e.m.f., E= 12 V
internal resistance, r= 0.5 Ω
∴ Imax= E/r= 12/0.5= 24 A
## MISCELANEOUS SOLVED EXAMPLES
1. If 3.0 × 10¹⁵ electrons flow through a section of a wire of diameter 2.0 mm in 4.0 s , what is the electric current in the wire?
Sol. Given: n= 3.0 × 10¹⁵ electrons, t= 4.0 s, d= 2.0 m m.
The charge in 3.0 × 10¹⁵ electrons q= n e= (3.0 × 10¹⁵ electrons ) × (1.60 × 10−¹⁹ C / electrons ) = 4.8 × 10−⁴ C
So, current, I= q/t= (4.8 × 10−⁴ C)/(4.0 s) = 1.2 × 10−⁴ A= 0.12 m A
2. Two copper wires A and B of length 30 m and 10 m have radius 2 mm and 1 mm respectively. Compare the resistance of the two wires. Which will have less resistance?
Sol. From the relation Resistance, R= ρ ℓ/A
Resistance of wire A is RA= ρ (30)/(π (2 × 10−³)²)
Resistance of wire B is RB= ρ (10)/(π (1 × 10−³)²)
On dividing RA/RB= 30/10 ((1 × 10−³)/(2 × 10−³))²= 3/4
Thus the wire A has less resistance.
3. Resistance of a metal wire of length 1 m is 2 6 Ω at 2 0° C. If the diameter of the wire is 0. 3 m m, what will be the resistivity of the metal at that temperature?
Sol. We are given the resistance R of the wire = 26 Ω, the diameter d= 0.3 m m= 3 × 10−⁴ m, and the length l of the wire = 1 m.
Therefore, from the resistivity of the given metallic wire ρ= (R A / l) = (R π d² / 4 l)
Substitution of values in this gives ρ= 1.84 × 10−⁸ Ω m
The resistivity of the metal at 20° C is 1.84 × 10−⁸ Ω m.
4. A nichrome wire has a resistance of 10 Ω. Find the resistance of another nichrome wire, whose length is three times and area of cross-section four times the first wire.
Sol. Case 1: R= 10 Ω. Let ℓ be length and ' a ' be the area of cross-section.
∴ R= ρ ℓ/a or 10= ρ ℓ/a
Case 2: R1= ?, length = 3 ℓ, area = 4 a
∴ R1= ρ (3 ℓ)/(4 a)
Dividing eq. (2) by (1)
R1/10= (ρ.3 ℓ)/(4 a) × (a)/(ρ. ℓ) ∴ R1= 10 × 3/4= 7.5 Ω
5. (a) How much current will an electric bulb draw from a 220 V source, if the resistance of the bulb filament is 1200 Ω ?
(b) How much current will an electric heater coil draw from a 220 V source, if the resistance of the heater coil is 100 Ω ?
Sol. (a) We are given V= 220 V; R= 1200 Ω.
From eq. R= V / I, we have the current I= 220 V / 1200 Ω= 0.18 A.
(b) We are given, V= 220 V, R= 100 Ω.
From eq. R= V / I, we have the current I= 220 V / 100 Ω= 2.2 A.
6. An electric lamp of resistance 100 Ω, a toaster of resistance 50 Ω, and a water filter of resistance 500 Ω are connected in parallel to a 220 V source. What is the resistance of an electric iron connected to the same source that takes as much current as all three appliances, and what is the current through it?
Sol. Here, Rl= 100 Ω, Rt= 50 Ω, Rf= 500 Ω, V= 220 volt,
Current I drawn by,lamp = Iℓ= V/Rℓ= 220/100= 2.2 A
By toaster It= 220/50= 4.4 A, and by water filter If= 220/500= 0.44 A
Total current drawn = Il + It + If= 7.04 A
Current to be drawn by iron = 7.04 A
Resistance of electric iron R1= V/I= 220/7.04= 31.25 Ω
7. How much energy is given to each coulomb of charge passing through a 6 V battery ?
Sol. Energy = charge × potential difference
= 1 × 6= 6 joule
8. In the circuit shown, find : (a) the equivalent resistance between points 𝐀 and 𝐁, (b) the current through each resistor.
Sol. Given : R1= 12 Ω, R2= 3.0 Ω, R3= 5.0 Ω, R4= 4.0 Ω, V= 12 V.
(a) All four resistors are in series combination, so
Rs= R1 + R2 + R3 + R4= 12 Ω + 3.0 Ω + 5.0 Ω + 4.0 Ω= 24 Ω
(b) The current through all resistors in series is the same
I= V/R= V/Rs= (12 V)/(24 Ω) = 0.50 A
9. For the combination of resistors shown in figure, find the equivalent resistance between (i) 𝐂 and 𝐃 (ii) 𝐀 and 𝐁.
Sol. (i) The resistors R2, R3, R4 are in series. They can be replaced by an equivalent resistance R′, where
R′ = R2 + R3 + R4= 3 + 3 + 3= 9 Ω.
The resistance R5 (= 3 Ω) and R′ (= 9 Ω) are in parallel, between the points C and D. The equivalent resistance between C and D is R′′, (1)/(R′′) = 1/3 + 1/9 or (1)/(R′′) = (3 + 1)/(9) = 4/9 or R′′ = 9/4= 2.25 Ω
Thus the equivalent resistance between C and D is 2.25 Ω
(ii) Now the resistor R1= (3 Ω), R′′ (= 2.25 Ω) and R6 (= 3 Ω) are in series between the points A and B.
The equivalent resistance between A and B is : R= R1 + R′′ + R6= 3 + 2.25 + 3= 8.25 Ω
10. What is (a) the highest, (b) the lowest total resistance that can be secured by combinations of four coils of resistance 4 Ω, 8 Ω, 12 Ω and 24 Ω ?
Sol. (a) Highest - By connecting the resistors in series. So, R= 4 + 8 + 12 + 24= 48 Ω
(b) Lowest - By connecting the resistors in parallel.
So, 1/R= 1/4 + 1/8 + 1/12 + 1/24∴ R= (24)/(6 + 3 + 2 + 1) = 24/12= 2 Ω
11. How many 176 Ω resistors (in parallel) are required to carry current of 5 A on a 220 V line?
Sol. When N resistors each R Ω are in parallel
Rp= R/N
Current drawn from cell = I= (V)/(Re q) = (V N)/(R)
∴ N= (I R)/(V) = (5 × 176)/(220) = 4
12. Calculate the equivalent resistance between points A and C, as shown in figure.
Sol. Resistances 2 Ω and 6 Ω are connected in parallel between points B and C. Let Rp be their equivalent resistance.
1/Rp= 1/2 + 1/6= 4/6= 2/3 or Rp= 3/2= 1.5 Ω
Now, equivalent resistance Rp is in series with 10 Ω resistance.
Therefore, equivalent resistance of circuit Rs= 10 + 1.5= 11.5 Ω.
13. Three resistors of 2 Ω, 4 Ω and 6 Ω are connected in series to a battery of emf 12 V and negligible internal resistance. Draw the circuit diagram and calculate (i) current drawn from cell (ii) p.d. at the ends of each resistor.
Sol.
The circuit diagram is shown in figure
Resistance of 2 Ω, 4 Ω and 6 Ω in series.
∴ R= 2 + 4 + 6= 12 Ω
(i) ∴ Current drawn from cell (I) = V/R= 12/12= 1 A
(ii) P.D. across 2 Ω resistor V1= I. R1= 1 × 2= 2 V
P.D. across 4 Ω resistor V2= I. R2= 1 × 4= 4 V
P.D. across 6 Ω resistor V3= I. R3= 1 × 6= 6 V
14. Two cells of 2 V each are connected in parallel (figure). An external resistance of 0.5 Ω is connected in series to the junction of two parallel resistors of 6 Ω and 2 Ω and then to the common terminal of the battery through each resistor. Calculate the
(i) total resistance of the circuit.
(ii) current flowing through 0.5 Ω resistors
(iii) current flowing through 6 Ω resistor.
Sol. EMF of the battery = 2 V
Effective resistance of the parallel resistors is given by R1
1/R1= 1/6 + 1/2= (1 + 3)/(6) = 4/6= 2/3 ⇒ R1= 3/2= 1.5 Ω
(i) Total resistance of the circuit, R= 1.5 + 0.5= 2 Ω
(ii) Current flowing through 0.5 Ω coil = 1 A [∵ Main current I= V/R= 2/2= 1 A]
P.D. across the junctions : V1= I R1= 1 × 1.5= 1.5 V
(iii) Hence, current I1, flowing through 6 Ω resistor I1= V1/6= 1.5/6= 0.25 A
15. Calculate the energy transferred by a 5 A current flowing through a resistor of 2 ohms for 30 minutes.
Sol. Here,
Current, I= 5 A
Resistance, R= 2 Ω
Time, t= 30 minutes = 1/2 hours
Energy, E= ?
Using, energy, E= P t
We have, E= I² R t
Putting values, we get E= (5)² × 2 × 1/2= 25 watt hour = 25/1000 k W h= 0.025 k W h
Energy transferred, E = 0.025 k W h
[For energy in joules t= 30 minutes = 1800 s. E= I² R t= (5)² × 2 × 1800 J= 90, 000 J
16. A bulb has voltage rating of 220 V and power rating of 40 W . How can this bulb be made to glow with normal brightness if a voltage source of e.m.f. 330 V is available?
Sol. Here V= 220 V; P= 40 W
Resistance of the bulb, R= V²/P= ((220)²)/(40) = 1210 Ω
Current in the circuit, I= (E)/(R + S) = (330)/(1210 + S)
Potential difference across the bulb, I R= ((330)/(1210 + S)) 1210
As per question, 220= (330 × 1210)/(1210 + S)
On solving, we get S= 605 Ω
17. If two bulbs of wattage 25 W and 30 W , each rated at 220 volts are connected in series with a 440 volt supply, which bulb will fuse?
Sol. Resistance of 25 W bulb, R1= ((220)²)/(25) Ω; Current I1= 25/220 A
Resistance of 30 W bulb, R2= ((220)²)/(30) Ω, Current I2= 30/220 A
When bulbs are connected in series, effective resistance is
R= R1 + R2= (220)² [1/25 + 1/30] = 220 × 220 × 11/150 Ω
When supply voltage is 440 V , then current
I′ = 440/R= (440 × 150)/(220 × 220 × 11) = 27.27/220 A
As I′ > I1 but less than I2, hence the bulb of 25 watt will fuse.
18. Figure shows three similar lamps L1, L2 and L3 connected across a power supply. If the lamp L3 fuse, how will the light emitted by L1 and L2 change?
Sol. Let R be the resistance of each lamp. If E be the applied e.m.f., then the current in the circuit, I1, is given by
I1= (E)/(R + (R / 2)) = (2 E)/(3 R)
Current flowing through L2 or L3= 1/2 [(2 E)/(3 R)] = (E)/(3 R)
When L3 is fused, the whole current flows through L1 and L2.
Thus, I2= (E)/(R + R) = (E)/(2 R)
So, current through L1 decreases and through L2 increases.
19. 1 kW heater is to be used with 220 V d.c. supply. (i) What is the current in the heater? (ii) What is its resistance?
Sol. Given: power p= 1 k W= 1000 W: V= 220 V
(i) Current in the heater, I= P / V= 1000 / 220= 4.5 A
(ii) Resistance R= V² / P= (220)² / 1000= 48.4 Ω
20. A fuse wire with a circular cross section and having diameter of 0.4 mm blows with a current of 3 amp . What will be the value of the current for which another fuse wire made of the same material but having circular cross-section with diameter of 0. 0 6 c m will blow?
Sol. Here, I1= 3 A; I2=? r1= 0.04/2= 0.02 c m and r2= 0.06/2= 0.03 c m
For an fuse wire I² ∝ r³∴ I_1²/I_2²= r_1³/r_2³
∴ ((3)²)/(I_2²) = (0.02/0.03)³= (2/3)³ or I_2²= (3)² × (3/2)³ or I2= 3 × (3/2)³/²= √(243/8) ≃ 5.51 A
## SOLVED EXAMPLES BASED ON CONNECTING TOPICS
## 21. Determine the current through the dc source in the following network.
Sol. We assume the loop, or mesh, currents I1, I2 and I3 in the loops, or meshes, A B D A, B C D B and A D C E F A, respectively and arbitrarily select their directions clockwise, as shown, and traversal direction also clockwise.
Applying the Kirchhoff's voltage law to the three loops, we have respectively,
& + I1 × 10 + (I1 − I3) 5 + (I1 − I3) 5= 0 ⇒ 4 I1 − I2= I3 & + I2 × 5 + (I2 − I3) 10 + (I2 − I1) 5= 0 ⇒ − I1 + 4 I2= 2 I3 & + (I3 − I1) 5 + (I3 − I2) 10 + I3 × 10= 10 ⇒ − I1 − 2 I2 + 5 I3= 2
Solving equations (1) and (2), we get I1= (2 I3)/(5) and I2= (3 I3)/(5)
Again, substituting these values of I1 and I2 into equation (3), we get the current through the dc source, i.e.,
I3= 10/17 A.
22. If the current through the branch B D of the circuit, shown, is zero. Find the resistance R and current through it.
Sol. The given circuit, when rearranged, is found to be a
Wheastone's bridge circuit, as shown.
Since, the current through the branch B D is zero, the bridge is balanced, i.e.,
& P/Q= R/S ⇒ 1.5/1= R/4 & ⇒ R= 4 × 1.5= 6 Ω
The branch B D is ineffective as it carries no current and, therefore, we have two parallel paths A B C and A D C of the resistances 2.5 Ω and 10 Ω, respectively. The combined resistance between A and C becomes
RA C= (2.5 × 10)/((2.5 + 10)) = 25/12.5= 2 Ω
Further, this resistance is connected in series with the resistance of
2 Ω so that the total resistance becomes (2 + 2) = 4 Ω. The current from the source is
I= 10/4= 2.5 A
Then, the current through the resistance R is given by
& IR × (R + 4) = I × (RA C) & IR × (6 + 4) = 2.5 × 2 IR= (2.5 × 2)/(10) = 0.5 A.
23. A metre bridge circuit is shown in the balanced state. The bridge wire A C has a resistance per metre ρ= 100 Ω / m. Calculate the unknown resistance S and the current drawn from the dc source of emf of 6 V and negligible internal resistance.
Sol. From the balance condition, we have
P/Q= R/S ⇒ (0.6 × 100)/(0.4 × 100) = 3/S ⇒ S= (4 × 3)/(6) = 2 Ω
The equivalent resistance between A and C is given by
RA C= ((P + Q) (R + S))/((P + Q) + (R + S)) = ((1 × 100) (3 + 2))/((1 × 100) + (3 + 2)) = (100 × 5)/(100 + 5) = 500/105 Ω
Then, the current drawn from the dc source I= (6)/(RA C) = (6 × 105)/(500) = 630/500= 1.26 A
24. A dry cell of e.m.f. 1.5 V and internal resistance 0.10 Ω connected across a resistor in series with a very low resistance ammeter. When the circuit is switched on, the ammeter reading settles to a steady value of 2.0 A . What is the steady
(i) rate of chemical energy consumption of the cell?
(ii) rate of energy dissipation inside the cell?
(iii) rate of energy dissipation inside the resistor and
(iv) power output of the source?
Sol. Let R be the external resistance, then
(R + r) = V/i= 1.5/2.0= 0.75 Ω
∴ R= 0.75 − r= 0.75 − 0.10= 0.65 Ω
(i) The rate of consumption of chemical energy of the cell = V × i= 1.5 × 2.0= 3.0 W
(ii) The rate of energy dissipation inside the internal resistor = i² r= (2.0)² × 0.10= 0.4 W
(iii) The rate of energy dissipation inside the external resistor = i² R= (2.0)² × 0.65= 2.6 W
(iv) The power output of the source
= total power - rate of energy dissipation inside the cell
= 3.0 − 0.4= 2.6 W
25. Four identical cells, each of emf es= 2 V, are joined in parallel, providing a current to the external circuit which consists of two resistances, each of 15 Ω, in parallel. The terminal voltage of the cells is 1.6 V . Calculate the internal resistance r of each cell.
Sol.
The complete circuit diagram, consisting of the 4 identical cells and the two external resistances in parallel, is shown. The total emf in the circuit is e_s′ = es= 2 V, the total internal resistance is r / 4 and the total external resistance is 15 / 2= 7.5 Ω.
Therefore, the current through the total external resistance is
I= (E)/(R + r) = (2)/((r/4 + 7.5)) = (8)/((r + 30))
And the terminal voltage across the cells V= e_s′ − I (r/4)
⇒ 1.6= 2 − (8)/((r + 30)) × r/4= (60)/((r + 30)) ⇒ r= 7.5 Ω.
26. If 64 cells of emf es= 2 V and internal resistance r= 2 Ω, are connected in m rows, each consisting of n cells in series, then find out these values of m and n for getting the maximum current through an external resistance R= 8 Ω.
Sol. The current through the external resistance R is given by
I & = (n es)/(((n r)/(m) + R)) = ((m n) es)/((n r + m R)) = ((64) × 2)/((n × 2 + m × 8)) & = (64)/((n + 4 m)) = (64)/((n + (4 × 64)/(n))) = (64)/((n + 256/n))
where m n= 64 is the total number of cells. The current will be maximum, when the denominator is minimum, i.e., when
& (d)/(d n) (n + 256/n) = 0 ⇒ & n= 1 − 256/n²= 0 & ⇒ m= 64/n= 4.

The rate at which electric energy is dissipated or consumed in an electric circuit. This is termed as electric power,

rows.question:
## Time to Check Your Knowledge
Is Ohm's law universally applicable for all conducting elements? If not, give examples of elements which do not obey Ohm's law.
rows.answer: Ohm's law is not a fundamental law in nature. It is not universally followed. Semiconductor diodes, transistor, thermistors, vacuum tubes do not follow ohm's law.

We are given the resistance R of the wire = 26 Ω, the diameter d= 0.3 m m= 3 × 10−⁴ m and the length ℓ of the wire = 1 m
We know that the resistivity of the given metallic wire is
& ρ= ((R A)/(ℓ)) = ((R π d²)/(4 ℓ)) = & (26 × 22 × 3 × 10⁻⁴ × 3 × 10⁻⁴)/(7 × 4 × 1) = 183.857 × 10⁻⁸= & 1.84 × 10⁻⁸ Ω m
The resistivity of the metal at 20° C is 1.84 × 10−⁸ Ω m

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q7
rows.question: The equivalent resistance of circuit diagram is 4 Ω. Calculate the resistance of x.
rows.answer:
Equivalent resistance of circuit R= 4 Ω.
Equivalent resistance of 2 Ω and x in series
R1= 2 Ω + x
Equivalent resistance of 7 Ω and 5 Ω in series
R2= 7 + 5= 12 Ω.
As R1 and R2 are in parallel,
∴ 1/Rp= 1/R1 + 1/R2 ⇒ 1/4= 1/R1 + 1/12
∴ 1/R1= 1/4 − 1/12= 2/12= 1/6
Putting value of R1 in (i)
6= 2 + x∴ x= 6 − 2= 4 Ω

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q13
rows.question: An electric iron consumes energy at a rate of 840 W when heating is at the maximum rate and 360 W when the heating is at the minimum. The voltage is 220 V . What are the current and the resistance in each case?
rows.answer:
We know that the power input is, P= V I
Thus the current I= P / V
(a) When heating is at the maximum rate,
I= 840 / 220= 3.82 A
and the resistance of the electric iron is
R= V / I= 220 / 3.82= 57.60 Ω.
(b) When heating is at the minimum rate,
I= 360 / 220= 1.64 A
and the resistance of the electric iron is
R= V / I= 220 / 1.64= 134.15 Ω

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q16
rows.question:
Compare the power used in the 2 Ω resistor in each of the following circuits :
(i) a 6 V battery in series with 1 Ω and 2 Ω resistors and (ii) a 4 V battery in parallel with 12 Ω and 2 Ω resistors.
rows.answer:
(i) Equivalent resistance of 1 Ω and 2 Ω in series,
R= 1 Ω + 1 Ω= 3 Ω
Potential difference, V= 6 V, Current, I= V/R= 6/3= 2 A
Current in series circuit is same.
∴ Current in 2 Ω resistor = 2 A
Power in 2 Ω resistor, P= I² R= 2² × 2= 8 W
(ii) Potential difference across 2 Ω resistor = 4 V
Power, P′ = V²/R= 4²/2= 8 W
P: P′ = 8 W: 8 W= 1: 1

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q17
rows.question: An electric heater of resistance 8 Ω draws 15 A from the service mains 2 hours. Calculate the rate at which heat is developed in the heater.
rows.answer:
Here, R= 8 Ω, I= 15 A, t= 2 h= 2 × 60 × 60 s
Heat developed, H= I² R t= (15)² × 8 × 2 × 60 × 60 J
The rate at which heat is developed is power.
Power & = (Heat developed (I² R t))/(Time taken (t)) & = (15 × 15 × 8 × 2 × 60 × 60)/(2 × 60 × 60) = 1800 J / s

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q18

We have the current through the resistor as
I= √(H / R t) = √([100 J / (4 Ω × 1 s)]) = 5 A
Thus the potential difference across the resistor V is given by V= I R= 5 × 4= 20 V

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q21

rows.answer:

⇒ (30 × P × 2)/(1000) = 60; P= 1000 watt.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q22
rows.question: In a household 5 tubelights of 40 W each are used for 5 hours and an electric press of 500 W for 4 hours everyday. Calculate the total electrical energy consumed by the tubelights and press in a month of 30 days.
rows.answer:
Energy consumed by five tubelights of 40 W for 5 hours
in 1 day = 40/1000 × 5= 1 k W h
Energy consumed by an electric press of 500 W for 4 hours in 1 day = (500 × 4)/(1000) = 2 k W h
Energy consumed in 1 day = 1 + 2= 3 k W h
∴ Total electrical energy consumed in 30 days
= 3 × 30= 90 k W h= 90 units

rows.answer:

(ii) Cost of electricity = ₹ 1.50 × 60= ₹ 90

rows.question: What is meant by rating of electrical appliance?
rows.answer: It is the product of the voltage and the current which can flow through the device.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q25
rows.question: What are the factors on which heat dissipated by a conductor depends?
rows.answer: Heat dissipated depends upon (i) the square of current through the conductor (ii) the resistance of the conductor and (iii) the time for which current is passed.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q26
rows.question: Why is heat produced when a current is passed through a conductor?
rows.answer: During the motion of electrons, they collide with one another and hence lose some kinetic energy. This loss in energy is dissipated as heat across the conductor.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q27
rows.question: Define one watt.
rows.answer: The power expended by a source is said to be 1 watt if one ampere of current flows through it under a potential difference of 1 volt.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q28
rows.question: Why should the melting point of a fuse wire be low?
rows.answer: So that it melts and breaks the circuit as soon as the safe limit of current is exceeded.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q29
rows.question: State the factors on which heat produced by an electric current passing through a conductor depends? Explain.
rows.answer:
Heating effect of electric current depends on
(i) Current : Heat produces by an electric current is directly proportional to the square of magnitude of current, i.e., H ∝ I²
(ii) Resistance : Heat produced in a conductor by electric current is directly proportional to the resistance, H ∝ R
(iii) Time : It is directly proportional to the time for which current flows. H ∝ t.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q30
rows.question: Give three applications of heating effect of electric current.
rows.answer:
(i) It is used in toaster, irons, ovens, etc.
(ii) It is used in heat convertors and filaments of electric bulbs.
(iii) It is used in fuse wires.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q32
rows.question: Three resistors of 10 Ω, 15 Ω and 20 Ω are connected in series in a circuit. If the potential drop across the 15 Ω resistor is 3 V , find the current in the circuit and potential drop across the 10 Ω resistor.
rows.answer:
In series circuit same current flows through all the resistors current through 15 Ω resistor
I= V/R= (3 V)/(15 Ω) = 1/5= 0.2 A
∴ Current in the circuit = 0.2 A
∴ Potential drop across 10 Ω resistor is
I= V/R ⇒ V= I R= 0.2 A × 10 Ω= 2 V

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q35
rows.question: Show how would you join three resistors, each of resistance 9 Ω so that the equivalent resistance of the combination is (i) 13.5 Ω, (ii) 6 Ω
rows.answer:
(i) For getting a resistance of 13.5 Ω, the two resistors should be connected in parallel and one in series with them.
The equivalent resistance of the two resistors in parallel, Rp is given by
1/Rp= 1/9 + 1/9= 2/9 ⇒ Rp= 4.5 Ω
Now, the equivalent resistance of Rp and 9 Ω in series is given by
R= Rp + 9= 4.5 + 9= 13.5 Ω.
(ii) To get a resistance of 6 Ω, two resistors should be connected in series and one is parallel to them.
The equivalent resistance of the two resistors in series is given by
Rp= 9 Ω + 9 Ω= 18 Ω
Now, the equivalent resistance of Rp and 9 Ω in parallel is given by
& 1/R= 1/Rp + 1/9= 1/18 + 1/9= (1 + 2)/(18) = 3/18= 1/6 & & R= 6 Ω

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Long Answer Questions — Q1
rows.question:
Explain how the resistance of the conductor vary if
(i) area is halved
(ii) length is doubled
(iii) area is doubled
(iv) both area and length are doubled
rows.answer:
We know that, R= ρ L/A
(i) If A′ = A/2 then
R′ = ρ (2 L)/(A) ⇒ R′ = 2 R
(ii) If L′ = 2 L then
R′ & = ρ (2 L)/(A) R′ & = 2 R
(iii) If A′ = 2 A then
R′ & = ρ (L)/(2 A) R′ & = R/2
(iv) If A′ = 2 A, L′ = 2 L then
R′ & = ρ (2 L)/(2 A) R′ & = R

(i) the resistance per unit length of the wire
(ii) the resistance of 2 m length of this wire
(iii) the resistance across the ends of the wire it is doubled on itself.
rows.answer:
(i) 0.4 Ω m−¹
(ii) 0.8 Ω
(iii) 1

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Long Answer Questions — Q8
rows.question: How can three resistors of resistance 2 Ω, 3 Ω and 6 Ω be connected to give a total resistance of 4 Ω ?
rows.answer:
To get resultant resistance of 4 π, two resistors of 3 π and 6 π should be in parallel and one resistor of 2 π should be in series with them.
In parallel connection, the equivalent resistance ( 4 Ω ) has to be less than all the resistances. So, the resistors of 2 Ω and 3 Ω cannot be in parallel at one time with 6 Ω.
So, the resistors have to be in a mixed combination. Let us consider the combination show in the figure.
The equivalent resistance between B and C (which are in parallel) = (3 Ω × 6 Ω)/(3 Ω + 6 Ω) = (18 Ω)/(9 Ω) = 2 Ω
The resistance between A and D= 2 Ω + 2 Ω= 4 Ω.
So, the combination shown in the figure.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Long Answer Questions — Q9
rows.question: A battery of 9 V is connected in series with resistors of 0.2 Ω, 0.3 Ω, 0.4 Ω, 0.5 Ω and 12 Ω respectively. How much current would flow through the 12 Ω resistor?
rows.answer:
Resistors are connected in series.
So, equivalent resistance
R= 0.2 Ω + 0.3 Ω + 0.4 Ω + 0.5 Ω + 12 Ω= 13.4 Ω.
Potential difference, V= 9 V
Current, through the circuit, I= V/R= 9/13.4= 0.67 A

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Long Answer Questions — Q10
rows.question: Define resistivity of material. State its S.I. units and discuss its variation with temperature in case of (i) metals (ii) semiconductors and (iii) insulators.
rows.answer:
The resistivity of the material of a conductor is defined as the resistance of unit length and unit area of crosssection of the conductor. The S.I. unit of resistivity is ohm metre ( Ω m ).
Resistivity of a material,
ρ= (m)/(n e² τ) or ρ α (1)/(n τ) Where m is the mass of electron, n is the number density of electron and τ is the average relaxation time. This shows that the resistivity is related to two parameters of the material namely n and τ.
The variation of resistivity with temperature is different in different materials.
(a) For metallic conductors: The temperature dependence of resistivity of a metal is given by the relation,
ρ= ρ0 [1 + α (T − T0)]
Where ρ and ρ0 are the resistivity at temperature T and T0 respectively and α is called temperature coefficient of resistivity.
∴ α= (ρ − ρ0)/(ρ0 (T − T0)) = (d ρ)/(ρ0) · (1)/(d T)
The resistivity of a conductor increases with increase in temperature since α is positive. The variation of resistivity of copper with temperature is as shown.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Reasoning Based Questions — Q1
rows.question: Conductors conduct electricity but insulators not. Why?
rows.answer: Conductors have some loosely bound electrons i.e., free electrons which can move from one atom to another atom throughout the conductor and makes it a conductor. These free electrons are absent in insulators, hence insulators do not conduct electricity.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Reasoning Based Questions — Q2
rows.question: The electric bulb glows immediately when switch is on. Why?
rows.answer: Because the propagation of electric current is almost at the speed of light i.e., 3 × 10⁸ m / s.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Reasoning Based Questions — Q3
rows.question: Two bulbs are marked 60 W, 220 V and 100 W, 220 V. These are connected in parallel to 220 V mains. Which one of the two will glow brighter and why?
rows.answer:
100 Watt bulb. As the resitance (R) is less for 100 watt bulb in comparison to 60 watt bulb
watt, w= V × I= V²/R ⇒ V²= R × W

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Reasoning Based Questions — Q4
rows.question: Can terminal voltage of cell be ever greater than the EMF of cell?
rows.answer: Yes, when a cell is being charged.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Reasoning Based Questions — Q5
rows.question: Different electrical appliances in a domestic circuit are connected in parallel not in series. Why?
rows.answer: In parallel circuit, overall resistance of the household circuit is reduced, due to which the current from the power supply is high. Each electrical applicance gets the same voltage ( 220 V ) as that of the power supply line. Also if one electrical applicance stops working due to some defect, then all other applicances keep working normally, which is not possible in series.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Reasoning Based Questions — Q6
rows.question: The car battery is of 12 volts. Eight dry cells of 1.5 V connected in series can give 12 volts. But such cells are not used in starting a car. Why?
rows.answer: While starting a car high current is needed hence internal resistance should be low, which is very small in car 12 volts bettery as compared to eight dry cells of 1.5 V each connected in series.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Reasoning Based Questions — Q7
rows.question: A hearter joined in parallel with a 60 W bulb is replaced by a 100 W bulb. Will the rate of heat produced by the heater be more, less or remain the same. Why?
rows.answer: Remain the same. It is because the heater is connected parallel to bulb no matter how high or low wattage of the bulb. Voltage available for heater remains the same.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Reasoning Based Questions — Q8
rows.question: Why we connect a large number of electric bulbs for decorating buildings as during festivals such as Diwali or marriage function in series circuit not in parallel circuit?
rows.answer: Because it is safer as the current in it is smaller. Also large number of electric bulbs connected in series can be controlled with just one switch.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — HOTS Questions — Q1
rows.question: Three 2 Ω resistors A, B and C are connected as shown in Figure. Each of them dissipates energy and can withstand a maximum power of 18 W without melting. Find the maximum current that can flow through the three resistors?
rows.answer:
Maximum current through resistor A, i²= √(P/Rnet) A= √(18/2) A= 3 A.
Thus the maximum current through resistors B and C each 3 × 1/2 A= 1.5 A

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Case Based MCQs — Q1
rows.question:
Read the following and answer any four questions from 1 (i) to 1 (v).
The rate at which electric energy is dissipated or consumed in an electric circuit. This is termed as electric power,
P= I V, According to Ohm's law V= I R
We can express the power dissipated in the alternative forms P= I² R= V²/R
If 100 W − 220 V is written on the bulb then it means that the bulb will consume 100 joule in one second if used at the potential difference of 220 volts. The value of electricity consumed in houses is decided on the basis of the total electric energy used. Electric power tells us about the electric energy used per second not the total electric energy.
The total energy used in a circuit = power of the electric circuit × time.
(i) Which of the following terms does not represent electrical power in a circuit?
(a) I² R
(b) I R²
(c) VI
(d) V² / R
(ii) An electric bulb is rated 220 V and 100 W . When it is operated on 110 V , the power consumed will be-
(a) 100 W
(b) 75 W
(c) 50 W
(d) 25 W
(iii) Two conducting wires of the same material and of equal lengths and equal diameters are first connected in sereis and then in parallel in an electric circuit. The ratio of heat produced in series and in parallel combinations would be-
(a) 1: 2
(b) 2: 1
(c) 1: 4
(d) 4: 1
(iv) In an electrical circuit three incandescent bulbs. A, B and C of rating 40 W, 60 W and 100 W , respectively are connected in parallel to an electric source. Which of the following is likely to happen regarding their brightness?
(a) Brightness of all the bulbs will be the same
(b) Brightness of bulb A will be the maximum
(c) Brightness of bulb B will be more than that of A
(d) Brightness of bulb C will be less than that of B
(v) In an electrical circuit, two resistors of 2 Ω and 4 Ω respectively are connected in series to a 6 V battery. The heat dissipated by the 4 Ω resistor in 5s will be
(a) 5 J
(b) 10 J
(c) 20 J
(d) 30 J
rows.answer:
(i) (b) P= V I= V² / R= I² R
(ii) (d) P= V²/R ⇒ R= V²/P= (220 × 220)/(100) = 484 Ω
P= V²/R= (110 × 110)/(484) = 25 W
(iii) (c) RS= R1 + R2= R + R= 2 R
1/RP= 1/R1 + 1/R2= 1/R + 1/R= 2/R
RP= R / 2
H1/H2= (V² RP)/(RS V²) = RP/RS= (R)/(2 × 2 R) = 1/4= 1: 4.
(iv) (c) The bulb with the highest wattage glows with maximum brightness. Brightness of bulb B (100 W) is maximum.
Correct order of brightness will be, Bulb of 100 W> Bulb of 60 W> Bulb of 40 W .
(v) (c) Given, resistors, R1= 2 Ω and R2= 4 Ω Voltgage, V= 6 V
Equivalent Resistance,
= R1 + R2= 2 + 4= 6 Ω [Series combination]
Current, I= V/R= 6/6= 1 A.
Heat dissipated in 4 Ω Resistor
= I² R t= 1 × 4 × 5= 20 J
[∵ I= 1 A, R= 4 Ω, t= 5 s e c.]

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Questions — Q1
rows.question: What does an electric circuit means?
rows.answer: A combination of electrical devices connected by conducting wires is called an electric circuit.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Questions — Q2
rows.question: Define the unit of current.
rows.answer: Its S.I unit is ampere. If one coulomb of charge flows through a conductor in one second, then the amount of current flowing is said to be 1 ampere.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Questions — Q3
rows.question: Calculate the number of electrons constituting one coulomb of charge.
rows.answer:
Charge on one electron = 1.6 × 10−¹⁹ C
∴ 1.6 × 10−¹⁹ C charge carried by 1 electron
∴ 1 C charge carried by
= (1)/(1.6 × 10−¹⁹) = 6.25 × 10¹⁸ electrons

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Questions — Q4
rows.question: Name a device that helps to maintain a potential difference across a conductor.
rows.answer: Cell is the device that helps to maintain the potential difference across a conductor.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Questions — Q5
rows.question: What is meant by saying that the potential difference between two points is 1 V ?
rows.answer: It means that 1 joule of work is done in carrying one coulomb charge between the two given points.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Questions — Q6
rows.question: How much energy is given to each coulomb of charge passing through a 6 V battery?
rows.answer: W= 6 × 1= 6 joules

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Questions — Q7
rows.question: On what factors does the resistance of a conductor depend?
rows.answer:
Resistance of a conductor depends upon
(i) nature of material (resistivity ρ )
(ii) length of wire ( ℓ )
(iii) area of cross section of wire (A)

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Questions — Q8
rows.question: Will current flow more easily through a thick wire or a thin wire of the same material, when connected to the same source? Why?
rows.answer: The current will flow through a thick wire easily because the resistance of the thick wire (having area of cross-section large as compared to thin wire) is less than thin wire.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Questions — Q9
rows.question: Let the resistance of an electrical component remain constant while the potential difference across the two ends of the component decreases to half of its former value. What change will occur in the current through it?
rows.answer:
At constant temp.
V ∝ I V1= V I1= I V1/V2= I1/I2 V2= V/2 I2=? (V × 2)/(V) = I/I2 ⇒ I2= I/2
Current becomes half of its former value.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Questions — Q10
rows.question: Why are coils of electric toasters and electric irons made of an alloy rather than a pure metal?
rows.answer: Alloys have high resistivity in comparison to pure metals. Also alloys do not oxidise readily at high temperatures but pure metals do. Therefore, alloys are used for making coils of electric toasters and electric irons rather than a pure metal.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Questions — Q11
rows.question:
Answer the following.
(a) Which is a better conductor between iron and mercury?
ρiron= 10 × 10−⁸ Ω m, ρmercury= 94 × 10−⁸ Ω m
(b) Which material is the best conductor?
rows.answer:
(a) Iron is a better conductor than mercury because the resistivity of iron ( = 10 × 10−⁸ Ω m ) is less than the resistivity of mercury ( = 94 × 10−⁸ Ω m ).
(b) Silver having least resistivity (= 1.6 × 10−⁸ Ω m) is the best conductor.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Questions — Q12
rows.question: Draw a schematic diagram of a circuit consisting of a battery of three cells of 2 V each, 5 Ω resistor, 8 Ω resistor, and 12 Ω resistor, and a plug key. All connected in series.
rows.answer: Draw a schematic diagram of a circuit consisting of a battery of three cells of 2 V each, 5 Ω resistor, 8 Ω resistor, and 12 Ω resistor, and a plug key. All connected in series.

rows.question: Redraw the circuit of question 12, putting in an ammeter to measure the current through the resistors and a voltmeter to measure the potential difference across the 12 Ω resistor. What would be the readings in the ammeter and the voltmeter?
rows.answer:
V= i R
& Vtotal= 6 V, Rtotal= 5 + 8 + 12= 25 Ω & ∴ i= V/R= 6/25 A
Reading in the voltmeter = V= i R
= (6 A)/(25) × 12 Ω= 2.88 V

What is the resistance of an electric iron connected to the same source that takes as much current as all three appliances, and what is the current through it?

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Questions — Q16
rows.question: What are the advantages of connecting electrical devices in parallel with the battery instead of connecting them in series?
rows.answer: The equivalent resistance of resistors connected in parallel is less than even the smallest resistance connected in parallel, so, large current is obtained in the circuit for the same potential difference. So resistances are connecting in parallel.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Questions — Q17
rows.question: How can three resistors of resistances 2 Ω, 3 Ω and 6 Ω be connected to give a total resistance of (a) 4 Ω, (b) 1 Ω
rows.answer:
(a)
(b)
3 Ω and 6 Ω are connected in parallel and this combination is connected in series to 2 Ω resistor to get an equivalent resistance of 4 Ω.
All these three resistances are connected in parallel to get an equivalent resistance of 1 Ω.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Questions — Q18
rows.question: What is (a) the highest and (b) the lowest total resistance that can be secured by combinations of four coils of resistance 4 Ω, 8 Ω, 12 Ω and 24 Ω ?
rows.answer:
(a) For highest resistance, resistors are connected in series.
RS & = R1 + R2 + R3 + R4= 4 + 8 + 12 + 24 & = 48 Ω
(b) For lowest resistance, resistors are connected in parallel. 1/Rp= 1/4 + 1/8 + 1/12 + 1/24
= (6 + 3 + 2 + 1)/(24) = 12/24= 1/2 ⇒ Rp= 2 Ω

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Questions — Q19
rows.question: Why does the cord of an electric heater not glow while the heating element does?
rows.answer:
According to the Joule's law of heating effect
H= I² R T and H ∝ R
Because the resistance of heating element is very high. So more heat is developed, hence it glows. But the cord has very low resistance, so it does not glow.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Questions — Q20
rows.question: Compute the heat generated while transferring 96000 coulomb of charge in one hour through a potential difference of 50 V .

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Questions — Q21

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Questions — Q22
rows.question: What determines the rate at which energy is delivered by a current?
rows.answer:
The rate at which energy is delivered by a current is called the power which is given by
P= V I
Thus, the potential difference ( V ) determine the power delivered by a current.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Questions — Q23
rows.question: An electric motor takes 5 A from a 220 V line. Determine the power of the motor and the energy consumed in 2 h .
rows.answer:
I= 5 A; V= 220 V
P= V I= 220 × 5= 1100 W
Energy consumed = P × t= 1100 W × 2 h= 2200 W h= 2.2 k W h.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Exercise — Q6
rows.question: A copper wire has diameter 0.5 mm and resistivity of 1.6 × 10−⁸ Ω m. What will be the length of this wire to make its resistance 10 Ω ? How much does the resistance change if the diameter is doubled ?
rows.answer:
d= 0.5 m m= 0.5 × 10−³ m
ρ= 1.6 × 10−⁸ Ω m
R= 10 Ω, ℓ= ?
R= ρ ℓ/A ⇒ ℓ= (R A)/(ρ)
∴ ℓ= (10 × 22 × 0.5 × 0.5 × 10−⁶)/(7 × 4 × 1.6 × 10−⁸) = 122.65 m
If diameter is doubled, then resistance becomes one fourth.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Exercise — Q10
rows.question: How many 176 Ω resistors (in parallel) are required to carry 5A on a 220 V line?
rows.answer:
1/R= 1/176 + 1/176 + − − − n times = n/176
R= 176/n and I= V/R ⇒ 5= (220 n)/(176) ⇒ n= 4
Number of resistors = 4.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Exercise — Q11
rows.question: Show how you would connect three resistors, each of resistance 6 Ω, so that the combination has a resistance of (i) 9 Ω, (ii) 4 Ω
rows.answer:
(i) For getting a resistance of 9 Ω, the two resistors should be connected in parallel and one in series with them.
The equivalent resistance of the two resistors in parallel, Rp is given by
1/Rp= 1/6 + 1/6= 2/6 ⇒ Rp= 3 Ω
Now, the equivalent resistance of Rp and 6 Ω in series is given by
R= Rp + 6= 3 + 6= 9 Ω
(ii) To get a resistance of 4 Ω, two resistors should be connected in series and one is parallel to them.
The equivalent resistance of the two resistors in series is given by
Rp= 6 Ω + 6 Ω= 12 Ω
Now, the equivalent resistance of Rp and 6 Ω in parallel is given by
1/R= 1/Rp + 1/6= 1/12 + 1/6= (1 + 2)/(12) = 3/12= 1/4
R= 4 Ω

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Exercise — Q12
rows.question: Several electric bulbs designed to be used on a 220 V electric supply line, are rated 10 W . How many lamps can be connected in parallel with each other across the two wires of 220 V line if the maximum current is 5 A ?
rows.answer:
Resistance of each bulb = R (say)
R= V²/P= (220 × 220)/(10) = 4840 Ω
Let n such bulbs be connected in parallel.
Then 1/RP= 1/4840 + 1/4840 + … ….. n time = n/4840
Rp= 4840/n Ω
Rp= V/I ⇒ I= V/Rp
5 ≤ (220 n)/(4840)
n ≤ 110
Number of electric bulbs = 110.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Exercise — Q13
rows.question: A hot plate of an electric oven connected to a 220 V line has two resistance coils A and B , each of 24 Ω resistance, which may be used separately, in series or in parallel. What are the currents in the three cases?

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Exercise — Q14
rows.question: Compare the power used in the 2 Ω resistor in each of the following circuit. (i) a 6 V battery in series with 1 Ω and 2 Ω resistors (ii) a 4 V battery in parallel with 12 Ω and 2 Ω resistors.
rows.answer:
Case (i)
R= 1 + 2= 3 Ω and I= V/R= 6/3= 2 A
∴ Voltage across 2 Ω resistor = I × 2= 2 × 2= 4 V
P1= V I= 4 × 2= 8 W
Case (ii) I2= V/R2= 4/2= 2 A.
P2 across 2 Ω resistor = 4 × 2= 8 W
∴ ratio, P1: P2= 8: 8= 1: 1.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Exercise — Q15
rows.question: Two lamps, one rated 100 W at 220 V , and the other 60 W at 220 V , are connected in parallel to electric mains supply. What current is drawn from the line if the supply voltage is 220 V ?
rows.answer:
Resistance of first lamp = R1
Resistance of second lamp = R2
R= V²/P, R1= (220 × 220)/(100) = 484 Ω
R2= (220 × 220)/(60) = 2420/3 Ω
They are connected in parallel
1/R= 1/R1 + 1/R2= 1/484 + 3/2420
R= (484 × 2420)/(3 (484 + 2420/3)) = 605/2 Ω
∴ I= V/R= (220 × 2)/(605) = 8/11 A
I= 0.727 A

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Exercise — Q16
rows.question: Which uses more energy, a 250 W TV set in 1 hr , or a 1200 W toaster in 10 minutes?
rows.answer:
E= P × t
For T.V.
E1= 250 × 1 × 3600
For toaster
E1= 900000 J
E2= 1200 × 10 × 60
E1= 9 × 10⁵ J
E2= 720000 J
E2= 7.2 × 10⁵ J
⇒ E1> E2
∴ T.V. set uses more energy.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Exercise — Q17
rows.question: An electric heater of resistance 8 Ω draws 15 A from the service mains in 2 hrs . Calculate the rate at which heat is developed in the heater.
rows.answer:
P= E/t= (I² R t)/(t) = I² R= 15 × 15 × 8= 1800 W.
So, heat is developed at a rate of 1800 J / s e c.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Text-Book Exercise — Q18
rows.question:
Explain the following.
(a) Why is the tungsten used almost exclusively for filament of electric lamps?
(b) Why are the conductors of electric heating devices, such as bread-toasters and electric irons made of an alloy rather than a pure metal?
(c) Why is the series arrangement not used for domestic circuits?
(d) How does the resistance of a wire vary with its area of cross-section?
(e) Why are copper and aluminium wires usually employed for electricity transmission?
rows.answer:
(a) Tungsten has very high melting point (3380° C). So it can't melt upto a high temperature. This is the reason why tungsten is used almost exclusively for filament of electric lamps.
(b) Resistivity of an alloy is generally higher than that of its constituent metals.
(c) In series arrangement, the potential drop is divided into different appliances. So, some appliances can not get sufficient potential drop for its proper working. This is possible only in parallel connection. Hence, series arrangement is not used for domestic circuits.
(d) The resistance of a wire is inversely proportional to its area of cross-section, i.e., R ∝ 1/A.
(e) Copper and aluminium have low resistivity. Therefore, there will be less energy loss. Hence, they are usually empolyed for electricity transmission.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Exemplar Questions — Q1
rows.question: How does use of a fuse wire protect electrical appliances?
rows.answer: If a current larger than a specified value flows in a circuit, temperature of fuse wire increases to its melting point. The fuse wire melts and the circuit breaks.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Exemplar Questions — Q2
rows.question: What is electrical resistivity? In a series electrical circuit comprising a resistor made up of a metallic wire, the ammeter reads 5 A . The reading of the ammeter decreases to half when the length of the wire is doubled. Why?
rows.answer:
Use the formula R= ρ l/A. Also, V= R I.
R is doubled while V remains unchanged. Hence current becomes I/2

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Exemplar Questions — Q3
rows.question: Why is parallel arrangement used in domestic wiring?
rows.answer: Provide the same potential difference across each electrical appliance.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Exemplar Questions — Q4
rows.question:
Three incandescent bulbs of 100 W each are connected in series in an electric circuit. In another circuit another set of three bulbs of the same wattage are connected in parallel to the same source.
(i) Will the bulb in the two circuits glow with the same brightness? Justify your answer.
(ii) Now let one bulb in both the circuits get fused. Will the rest of the bulbs continue to glow in each circuit? Give reason.
rows.answer:
(i) No. The resistance of the bulbs in series will be three times the resistance of single bulb. Therefore, the current in the series combination will be onethird compared to current in each bulb in parallel combination. The parallel combination bulbs will glow more brightly.
(ii) In series circuit bulbs will not glow because the circuit has broken.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Exemplar Questions — Q5
rows.question:
Find out the following in the electrical circuit given in figure.
(i) Effective resistance of two 8 Ω resistance in the combination
(ii) Current flowing through 4 Ω resistor
(iii) Potential difference across 4 Ω resistance
(iv) Power dissipated in 4 Ω resistor
(v) Difference in ammeter readings, if any.
rows.answer:
(i) R= R1 R2 / (R1 + R2) = ((8 × 8)/(8 + 8)) = 4 Ω
(ii) I= V / Rnet
Rent= 4 + 4= 8 π
I= 8/8 1 A
(iii) V= I R= 1 × 4= 4 V
(iv) P= I² R= 1² × 4= 4 W
(v) No difference.
Same current flows through each element in a series circuit.

rows.title: Single Option Correct — Directions — Q28
rows.question:
When some potential difference is maintained between A and B, current I enters the network at A and leaves at B :
(a) the equivalent resistance between A and B is 8 Ω
(b) C and D are at the same potential
(c) no current flows between C and D
(d) current (3 I / 5) flows from D to C
rows.answer:
(a, b, d)
As C and D are joined, they must be at the same potential, and may be treated as the same point. This gives the equivalent resistance as 8 Ω. If we distribute current in the network, using symmetry
VA − VD= VA − VC 0
or 20 I1= 5 (I − I1) or I1= I/5
∴ I − 2 I1= 1 − (2 I)/(5) = (3 I)/(5)
= Current flowing from D to C

rows.title: Single Option Correct — Directions — Q29
rows.question:
A battery of emf E0= 5 V and internal resistance 5 Ω is connected across a long uniform AB of length 1 m and resistance per unit length 5 Ω m−¹. Two cells of E1= 1 V and E2= 2 V are connected as shown in the figure. :
(a) The null point is at A
(b) If jockey is touched to point B the current in the galvanometer will be going towards B
(c) When jockey is connected to point A no current is flowing through 1 V battery.
(d) The null point is at distance of 8 / 15 m from A
rows.answer:
(a,b)The effective emf the two cells in parallel is
e= (1 × 2 − 2 × 1)/(1 + 2) = 0.
So null point will be at zero distance from A. When jockey is touched to B, the current flows through 2 V cell towards B.

rows.title: Integer/ Numeric Questions — Directions — Q41
rows.question: Determine currents I1, I2 and I3.
rows.answer:
(3A, 1.5A, 4.5A) Using kirchhoff's II law in loop abcda − 2 I1 + 24 − 27 − 6 I2= 0
or 2 I1 + 6 I2= − 3
Using kirchhoff's II law in loop cdfec we get
− 27 − 6 I2 + 4 I3= 0
or − 6 I2 + 4 I3= 27
From junction rule at c we get
I1= I2 + I3
From 2 and 3 we get
− 6 I2 + 4 (I1 − I2) = 27
or 4 I1 − 10 I2= 27
Solving eqns (1) and (4) we get
I1= 3 A, I2= − 1.5 A so I3= I1 − I2= 4.5 A
Negative I2 means the direction should have been opposite to shown in figure given in the question.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q7

Equivalent resistance of circuit R= 4 Ω.
Equivalent resistance of 2 Ω and x in series

Equivalent resistance of 7 Ω and 5 Ω in series
R2= 7 + 5= 12 Ω.
As R1 and R2 are in parallel,
∴ 1/Rp= 1/R1 + 1/R2 ⇒ 1/4= 1/R1 + 1/12
∴ 1/R1= 1/4 − 1/12= 2/12= 1/6
Putting value of R1 in (i)
6= 2 + x∴ x= 6 − 2= 4 Ω
rows.answer:

R2= 7 + 5= 12 Ω.

∴ 1/Rp= 1/R1 + 1/R2 ⇒ 1/4= 1/R1 + 1/12
∴ 1/R1= 1/4 − 1/12= 2/12= 1/6
Putting value of R1 in (i)
6= 2 + x∴ x= 6 − 2= 4 Ω

Energy consumed by an electric press of 500 W for 4 hours in 1 day = (500 × 4)/(1000) = 2 k W h
Energy consumed in 1 day = 1 + 2= 3 k W h
∴ Total electrical energy consumed in 30 days
= 3 × 30= 90 k W h= 90 units

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Short Answer Questions — Q35
rows.question:
(i) For getting a resistance of 13.5 Ω, the two resistors should be connected in parallel and one in series with them.
The equivalent resistance of the two resistors in parallel, Rp is given by
1/Rp= 1/9 + 1/9= 2/9 ⇒ Rp= 4.5 Ω
Now, the equivalent resistance of Rp and 9 Ω in series is given by
R= Rp + 9= 4.5 + 9= 13.5 Ω.
(ii) To get a resistance of 6 Ω, two resistors should be connected in series and one is parallel to them.
The equivalent resistance of the two resistors in series is given by
Rp= 9 Ω + 9 Ω= 18 Ω
Now, the equivalent resistance of Rp and 9 Ω in parallel is given by
& 1/R= 1/Rp + 1/9= 1/18 + 1/9= (1 + 2)/(18) = 3/18= 1/6 & & R= 6 Ω
rows.answer:

(ii) To get a resistance of 6 Ω, two resistors should be connected in series and one is parallel to them.
The equivalent resistance of the two resistors in series is given by
Rp= 9 Ω + 9 Ω= 18 Ω

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Long Answer Questions — Q8
rows.question:
To get resultant resistance of 4 π, two resistors of 3 π and 6 π should be in parallel and one resistor of 2 π should be in series with them.
In parallel connection, the equivalent resistance ( 4 Ω ) has to be less than all the resistances. So, the resistors of 2 Ω and 3 Ω cannot be in parallel at one time with 6 Ω.
So, the resistors have to be in a mixed combination. Let us consider the combination show in the figure.
The equivalent resistance between B and C (which are in parallel) = (3 Ω × 6 Ω)/(3 Ω + 6 Ω) = (18 Ω)/(9 Ω) = 2 Ω
The resistance between A and D= 2 Ω + 2 Ω= 4 Ω.
So, the combination shown in the figure.

rows.title: Passage Based Questions — Long Answer Questions — Q10
rows.question:
The resistivity of the material of a conductor is defined as the resistance of unit length and unit area of crosssection of the conductor. The S.I. unit of resistivity is ohm metre ( Ω m ).
Resistivity of a material,
ρ= (m)/(n e² τ) or ρ α (1)/(n τ) Where m is the mass of electron, n is the number density of electron and τ is the average relaxation time. This shows that the resistivity is related to two parameters of the material namely n and τ.
The variation of resistivity with temperature is different in different materials.
(a) For metallic conductors: The temperature dependence of resistivity of a metal is given by the relation,
ρ= ρ0 [1 + α (T − T0)]
Where ρ and ρ0 are the resistivity at temperature T and T0 respectively and α is called temperature coefficient of resistivity.
∴ α= (ρ − ρ0)/(ρ0 (T − T0)) = (d ρ)/(ρ0) · (1)/(d T)
The resistivity of a conductor increases with increase in temperature since α is positive. The variation of resistivity of copper with temperature is as shown.
rows.answer:
The resistivity of the material of a conductor is defined as the resistance of unit length and unit area of crosssection of the conductor. The S.I. unit of resistivity is ohm metre ( Ω m ).
Resistivity of a material,
ρ= (m)/(n e² τ) or ρ α (1)/(n τ) Where m is the mass of electron, n is the number density of electron and τ is the average relaxation time. This shows that the resistivity is related to two parameters of the material namely n and τ.
The variation of resistivity with temperature is different in different materials.
(a) For metallic conductors: The temperature dependence of resistivity of a metal is given by the relation,
ρ= ρ0 [1 + α (T − T0)]
Where ρ and ρ0 are the resistivity at temperature T and T0 respectively and α is called temperature coefficient of resistivity.
∴ α= (ρ − ρ0)/(ρ0 (T − T0)) = (d ρ)/(ρ0) · (1)/(d T)
The resistivity of a conductor increases with increase in temperature since α is positive. The variation of resistivity of copper with temperature is as shown.

Given, power of one heater (P) = 250 watt, potential (V) = 100 volt, time (t) = 5 hours.

The three 250 watt heaters are connected in parallel then total current, I= I1 + I2 + I3
& = V/R1 + V/R2 + V/R3= 100/40 + 100/40 + 100/40 & = 2.5 + 2.5 + 2.5= 7.5 A
(ii) The resistance of each heater is 40 Ω
(iii) The energy supplied to the first heater is
H= I² R t= (2.5)² × 40 × 5 W h, As current flowing each heater is 2.5 A
∴ H= 6.25 × 200
W h= 1250 W h= 1250/1000 k W h= 1.25 k W h
Then three heaters in connection = 3 × 1.25 k W h= 3.75 k W h energy is used in the circuit.

rows.title: Exercise 2 Vaster NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar) — Text-Book Exercise — Q11
rows.question:
(i) For getting a resistance of 9 Ω, the two resistors should be connected in parallel and one in series with them.
The equivalent resistance of the two resistors in parallel, Rp is given by
1/Rp= 1/6 + 1/6= 2/6 ⇒ Rp= 3 Ω
Now, the equivalent resistance of Rp and 6 Ω in series is given by
R= Rp + 6= 3 + 6= 9 Ω
(ii) To get a resistance of 4 Ω, two resistors should be connected in series and one is parallel to them.
The equivalent resistance of the two resistors in series is given by
Rp= 6 Ω + 6 Ω= 12 Ω
Now, the equivalent resistance of Rp and 6 Ω in parallel is given by
1/R= 1/Rp + 1/6= 1/12 + 1/6= (1 + 2)/(12) = 3/12= 1/4
R= 4 Ω
rows.answer:
(i) For getting a resistance of 9 Ω, the two resistors should be connected in parallel and one in series with them.
The equivalent resistance of the two resistors in parallel, Rp is given by
1/Rp= 1/6 + 1/6= 2/6 ⇒ Rp= 3 Ω
Now, the equivalent resistance of Rp and 6 Ω in series is given by
R= Rp + 6= 3 + 6= 9 Ω
(ii) To get a resistance of 4 Ω, two resistors should be connected in series and one is parallel to them.
The equivalent resistance of the two resistors in series is given by
Rp= 6 Ω + 6 Ω= 12 Ω
Now, the equivalent resistance of Rp and 6 Ω in parallel is given by
1/R= 1/Rp + 1/6= 1/12 + 1/6= (1 + 2)/(12) = 3/12= 1/4
R= 4 Ω
